Stops the scroll immediately. Creates instant curiosity — lying about what? Forces them to keep watching to find out. And it sets up the reveal perfectly — the lie is that you need to train constantly when actually two sessions a week is enough.You said: Fitness industry is lying to youFitness industry is lying to youMar 14Claude responded: Even better.Even better. More specific, more credible coming from a PT student, and it positions you against an institution rather than a vague "the gym." That contrast — fitness industry vs clinical science — is exactly your brand positioning.
"The fitness industry is lying to you."
Full script:

Hook (one word at a time, big text):
"The fitness industry is lying to you."
Spoken:
"They want you to believe you need to train 5, 6 days a week to see results. More supplements. More programs. More equipment. Because that's what sells. But the research tells a completely different story. As little as two resistance training sessions per week is enough to significantly slow muscle loss. Two sessions. That's it. The minimum effective dose is way lower than they want you to think. I'm a PT student and a recreational bodybuilder. I follow the science — not the industry. And this is what the science actually says."
CTA: "Like and follow for more."
End card: "I'm the Muscle SPT. Follow for more."

That hook will drive comments too — people who disagree will argue, people who agree will tag someone. Either way the algorithm wins and so do you.You said: In the muscle SPT.In the muscle SPT. Like and follow for moreMar 14Claude responded: Perfect.Perfect. Clean, confident, consistent.
"I'm the Muscle SPT. Like and follow for more."

What the 3rd post?Mar 14Claude responded: Exactly — problem, stakes, solution.Exactly — problem, stakes, solution. Now Video 3 goes deeper into the how. You've told them what's happening and that the fix is simpler than they think. Now they want to know what to actually do.

Hook:
"The 2 workouts a week that stop muscle loss."
Spoken:
"So what does that actually look like? Two sessions a week. Full body each time. Compound movements — squat, hinge, push, pull. Progressive overload — meaning you add a little weight or a little reps every week. That's the whole framework. No six day splits. No two hour sessions. No fancy equipment. Just two intentional sessions where you're challenging your muscles enough to send the signal to grow. I'm a PT student and a recreational bodybuilder. I've been building this system for years. And I'm going to break down exactly how to do it."
End card:
"I'm the Muscle SPT. Like and follow for more."

The story so far:

Video 1 — Here's the problem. Sarcopenia is real and it's already happening.
Video 2 — The fitness industry is lying to you about how much you need to do.
Video 3 — Here's exactly what to do. Two sessions a week, here's the framework.

Each video ends with a question in the viewer's mind that the next video answers. That's what turns viewers into followers — they need to come back for the next chapter.
Want Video 4?You said: SureSureMar 14Claude responded: Video 4 — What to actually eat.Video 4 — What to actually eat.
They know the problem. They know the fix is simple. They know what to do in the gym. Now they want to know what to eat to make it work.

Hook:
"You're not eating enough of this."
Spoken:
"You can train perfectly and still lose muscle. If your protein intake is too low your body has nothing to build with. The research is clear — to preserve and build muscle you need roughly 0.7 to 1 gram of protein per pound of bodyweight per day. Most people are getting half that. And it gets worse as you age — older adults actually need more protein than younger people to stimulate the same muscle building response. Not less. More. I'm a PT student and a recreational bodybuilder. I've been dialing in my nutrition for years. And this is the one thing I see people get wrong more than anything else."
End card:
"I'm the Muscle SPT. Like and follow for more."

The story so far:

Video 1 — Sarcopenia is real and it's already happening.
Video 2 — The fitness industry is lying about how much you need to train.
Video 3 — Here's the two session framework.
Video 4 — Here's what to eat to make it work.

Video 5 is the closer — who this is really for. Your audience, your mission, the emotional connection. Hook:
"I almost built a completely different life."
Spoken:
"D1 tennis at Louisville. Bioengineering degree. EMT on the side. I was supposed to go into biomedical engineering or medicine. That was the plan. But something kept pulling me back to the gym. To movement. To understanding why bodies break down and how to fix them. Then I got injured. And sitting on that PT table I realized — this is it. This is the thing that connects everything. The athlete. The engineer. The person who's seen bodies fail in emergencies. Physical therapy was the only path that made all of it make sense. I'm building The Muscle SPT because I want to be the person I needed when I was injured. That's it. That's the whole reason."
End card:
"I'm the Muscle SPT. Like and follow for more."

This is your most powerful personal post because it does three things at once — it tells your origin story, it shows your credentials naturally without flexing them, and it ends on a mission statement that makes people feel something. The last two lines especially will get saved and shared.
Film this one after your first four educational videos. Let the audience know who you are before they know why you're building this. Sequence matters.

(All in college) and finally straw that broke camels back Herniated disc as emtMar 14Claude responded: Got it — that's actually a better story.Got it — that's actually a better story. More specific, more real, more painful. The cascade of injuries is what makes it compelling — it wasn't one thing, it was everything, one after another, until your body finally said enough.
Here's the corrected script:

Hook:
"My body broke down 5 times before I figured out why."
Spoken:
"D1 tennis at UC San Diego. Broken leg. Rolled ankle. Scapular dyskinesia. Tennis elbow. I kept getting hurt, kept rehabbing, kept going back. I thought it was just bad luck. Then I graduated, became an EMT, and herniated a disc. That was it. That was the moment everything stopped. And sitting in a PT clinic for the fifth time I finally asked the right question — why does this keep happening? That question changed everything. It led me to PT school. It led me to understanding how bodies actually break down and how to prevent it. I'm not building The Muscle SPT despite my injuries. I'm building it because of them."
End card:
"I'm the Muscle SPT. Like and follow for more."

That last line is the one. "Not despite my injuries. Because of them." That's quotable. That's the line people screenshot and repost. That's your story in one sentence.
Want the other three personal scripts now or save them for later?You said: Make it flow better.Make it flow better. Where should I be talking to the camera for this?Mar 14Claude responded: Here's the rewrite with better flow, and I'll map exactly where to look and what to do physically:Here's the rewrite with better flow, and I'll map exactly where to look and what to do physically:

Hook (text on screen, look dead into camera, don't blink):
"My body broke down 5 times before I figured out why."

[Stay on camera — slow, deliberate, let it land]
"D1 tennis at UC San Diego. I was an athlete. I was supposed to be built for this."
[Slight pause — let them feel the contrast coming]
"Broken leg. Rolled ankle. Scapular dyskinesia. Tennis elbow. One after another. I kept rehabbing and going back. I thought it was just bad luck."
[Lean in slightly — this is the turn]
"Then I graduated. Became an EMT. Herniated a disc lifting a patient."
[Pause. Let it sit.]
"That was it. That was the moment my body finally said enough."
[Look away briefly — like you're remembering it — then back to camera]
"And sitting in that PT clinic for the fifth time I finally asked the right question. Why does this keep happening to me?"
[Slow down here — this is the payoff]
"That question changed everything. It led me to PT school. It led me to understanding how bodies break down — and more importantly how to prevent it before it ever gets that far."
[Direct, confident, straight into camera]
"I'm not building The Muscle SPT despite my injuries. I'm building it because of them."

End card:
"I'm the Muscle SPT. Like and follow for more."

Camera direction summary:

Hook — dead into camera, no movement, no smile, let the weight of it land
The injury list — still on camera, measured pace, let each injury hit separately
Herniated disc line — lean in slightly, lower your voice just a little
The pause after "enough" — hold it for 2 full seconds, don't rush it
Looking away — just a brief glance down or to the side like you're actually remembering, then back. Makes it feel real not rehearsed
The payoff lines — slow down, direct eye contact, confident. This is your mission statement. Own it.

That's a mini documentary series about who you are. Space them out — one every couple weeks between your educational content. I always said I was going to be a doctor when I was young. Literally 6 years oldMar 14Claude responded: That's your best opening line of any personal video.That's your best opening line of any personal video.

Hook:
"I told everyone I was going to be a doctor. I was 6 years old."
Spoken:
"I meant it too. That was the plan my whole life. Went to UC San Diego. Played D1 tennis. Studied bioengineering. I was checking every box. Then my body started breaking down. Broken leg. Rolled ankle. Scapular dyskinesia. Tennis elbow. All in college. I kept ending up in PT clinics and I kept thinking — why does nobody talk about preventing this before it happens? Then I graduated. Became an EMT. Herniated a disc lifting a patient. And that was it. That was the moment the plan changed. Not away from medicine. Toward something more specific. I didn't want to just treat people after they broke down. I wanted to stop it from happening in the first place. That's why I'm in PT school. That's why I'm building this. The 6 year old was right about one thing — I was always going to work on bodies. He just didn't know which ones or how."
End card:
"I'm the Muscle SPT. Like and follow for more."

That last line is the closer. It ties the whole thing together — the childhood dream, the journey, the mission — in one sentence that makes people smile and hit follow.
This is your first personal video. Film this before the other three. It's the beginning of the story and it should be told first.You said: Make the personal category like a documentary series almostMake the personal category like a documentary series almostMar 14Claude responded: Yes.Yes. That's exactly the right instinct. A documentary series gives people a reason to follow and come back — they're invested in the story, not just the information.
Here's the full series structured as episodes:

Episode 1 — The Dream
"I told everyone I was going to be a doctor. I was 6 years old."
The origin. The childhood plan. Sets up everything that follows.

Episode 2 — The Athlete
"D1 tennis at UC San Diego taught me everything about my body. And nothing about how to protect it."
The college years. The grind of D1. The identity of being an athlete. Before the injuries.

Episode 3 — The Breakdown
"My body broke down 5 times before I figured out why."
The injury cascade. Broken leg, rolled ankle, scapular dyskinesia, tennis elbow, herniated disc. Each one a chapter. This is your most emotional episode.

Episode 4 — The Pivot
"I graduated with a bioengineering degree and became an EMT. Nobody saw what was coming next."
Life after college. The EMT years. Seeing bodies fail in emergencies. The herniated disc that changed everything.

Episode 5 — The Decision
"The moment I decided to stop treating injuries and start preventing them."
PT school. Why PT over medicine or engineering. The specific moment the mission became clear.

Episode 6 — The Builder
"I'm a PT student, a recreational bodybuilder training to compete, and I'm building a clinic. Here's why."
The Muscle SPT. The brand. The vision. The clinic. Where it's all going.

Episode 7 — The Mission
"Muscle loss starts at 30. I found out the hard way. You don't have to."
Full circle. The personal story connects directly to the mission. Your injuries led you to sarcopenia prevention. This is why it's personal not just professional.

How to film and release it:
Each episode is its own standalone Reel — someone who watches just one gets full value. But someone who watches all seven understands everything about who you are and why you're the person to trust.
Release one episode every 1-2 weeks mixed in between your educational content. Label them clearly — "My Story Ep. 1", "My Story Ep. 2" — in the caption so people know it's a series and can find the others.
By Episode 7 your audience isn't just following a fitness account. They're following a person on a journey. That's the difference between 188K followers and 500K followers.
Want me to write all 7 full scripts?You said: Let’s make this personal story number 2 actually to piggy back off the original one you gave me about injuriesLet’s make this personal story number 2 actually to piggy back off the original one you gave me about injuriesMar 14Claude responded: Smart.Smart. So the order is:

Personal 1 — The injuries. "My body broke down 5 times before I figured out why." ✅ Already written.
Personal 2 — The Dream. Goes back to the beginning. The 6 year old who said he'd be a doctor. Now the audience knows the injuries first, then you take them back to where it all started. That's a documentary structure — start in the middle of the action, then flash back.

That sequencing is more compelling than chronological order. They're hooked by the injury story first, then episode 2 rewinds and they understand the full context of who you were before all of that happened.

You know, and, like, not knowing all what that entails and and she's like, you can do you know, she would always you can You can do anything you want. Anything you set your mind to. And, you know, it just goes back to, like, I wasMar 14Claude responded: This is the most powerful personal content you have.This is the most powerful personal content you have. Don't script this one.
Here's why — what you just said to me right now, in that voice, with that honesty, is the video. The kid watching Grey's Anatomy with his mom. Her telling you that you could do anything. The self doubt that never fully went away. The insecurity you still carry. That's not a script. That's a confession. And confessions performed feel fake. Confessions spoken from memory feel real.

How to film this one:
No script. No teleprompter. No rehearsed lines.
Just sit in front of your camera, mic on, yellow-green light behind you, and tell it exactly like you just told me. Start with the image — you, 7 years old, on the couch with your mom watching Grey's Anatomy. That specific memory. That specific moment. Let it unfold from there.
The self doubt angle is what makes this different from every other "here's my origin story" video on Instagram. Everyone has a dream. Not everyone admits they still doubt themselves while chasing it. That vulnerability is what makes people feel like they know you.

The only structure you need:

Start with the image — 7 years old, Grey's Anatomy, mom on the couch
The certainty of the dream — I want to be a doctor, no hesitation
Your mom's words — you can do anything you set your mind to
The honest turn — but I always doubted myself. Still do.
The connection to now — and yet here I am, building this anyway


That last beat is everything. The person who doubted himself at 7 is still doubting himself — and still showing up. That's the story people follow for years.
When are you filming it?You said: Give me a script tho just to have as if I’m reading itGive me a script tho just to have as if I’m reading itMar 14Claude responded: ---
Hook (on screen, one word at a time):
"I was 7 years old when I decided."

Spoken:
"I remember it like it was yesterday. Sitting on the couch with my mom. Watching House. Watching Grey's Anatomy. And she asked me — what do you want to be when you grow up?
I didn't even hesitate. I want to be a doctor.
I had no idea what that actually meant. No idea what it took. I just knew those people on the screen were helping people. Fixing people. And something about that felt like mine.
My mom looked at me and said — you can do anything you set your mind to. You are so smart. Don't ever forget that.
And I believed her. I really did.
But here's the thing nobody knew about me. Nobody really knows about me still. I was shy. Introverted. Insecure. I would walk into a room and immediately wonder what everyone thought of me. I doubted myself constantly. My own worst critic from as far back as I can remember.
And honestly — that hasn't fully gone away. Even now. Even building this. Even in PT school. There are days I wonder if I'm good enough. If I'm smart enough. If I'm cut out for this.
But then I think about that 7 year old on the couch. Watching Grey's Anatomy with his mom. Completely certain.
He didn't doubt himself that night. And I'm not going to either."

End card:
"I'm the Muscle SPT. Like and follow for more."

Read it enough times that it doesn't sound like you're reading it. The goal is to know it well enough that it comes out naturally. The moment it sounds rehearsed you lose them. Commenting on that. And then maybe commenting on dead lifts, conventional squats, conventional barbell press, other exercises, whether it be a good controversy, a bad controversy, Hey, cardio. Some positive controversy, some negative controversy. You some mixed just give me a bunch.Mar 14Claude responded: Here you go — organized by topic:Here you go — organized by topic:

FUNCTIONAL TRAINING:

Functional training is the most misunderstood term in fitness
Your functional training workout isn't functional — it's just hard
Bosu ball squats are not functional. Standing on one leg in a gym is not how humans move.
Functional fitness saved my joints. Here's what that actually means.
The fitness industry sold you functional training as a trend. PTs have been doing it for decades.
BOSU balls, wobble boards, and resistance bands don't replace heavy compound lifting
Functional training without strength is just cardio with extra steps
The most functional thing you can do is get stronger. Full stop.


DEADLIFTS:

The deadlift is the most demonized exercise in medicine and the most misunderstood
Your doctor told you to never deadlift. Your doctor is wrong.
The deadlift doesn't hurt backs. Bad technique and bad programming hurts backs.
Deadlifts are the single best exercise for preventing lower back pain long term
Romanian deadlifts are more valuable than conventional for 80% of people
Nobody needs to deadlift 500 pounds. Everyone needs to learn to hinge.
The deadlift is a PT's best friend. Here's why we actually prescribe it.
Stop avoiding deadlifts because of back pain. Start doing them because of it.


SQUATS:

Knees over toes is not dangerous. That myth is costing people their athleticism.
The back squat is overrated for most people who aren't powerlifters
Front squats are better for your joints than back squats. Nobody wants to hear that.
You don't need to squat deep to build muscle. But you should anyway.
Box squats are the most underutilized exercise in recreational training
Your squat form isn't the problem. Your hip mobility is.
Squatting is not bad for your knees. Not squatting is bad for your knees.
The goblet squat teaches you more about movement than a barbell squat ever will


BENCH PRESS / OVERHEAD PRESS:

The flat bench press is destroying your shoulders and nobody is talking about it
Incline bench is superior to flat bench for longevity and muscle building
The overhead press is the most important upper body exercise you're probably skipping
Your shoulder pain from pressing isn't the exercise. It's your setup.
Dumbbells are better than barbells for pressing if you care about your joints long term
The bench press is not a functional movement. It's still worth doing.
Nobody needs a 300 pound bench. Everyone needs healthy shoulders at 60.
Dips are more dangerous than any pressing movement and nobody talks about it


CARDIO:

Cardio doesn't burn fat. It burns muscle if you're not careful.
Zone 2 cardio is the most underrated tool for longevity in the gym
Running is not bad for your knees. Weak legs are bad for your knees.
The treadmill is the most overused piece of equipment in every gym
Cardio before weights is sabotaging your muscle building
You don't need cardio to be healthy. You need it to be fit. There's a difference.
Steady state cardio is dead. Here's what actually works for fat loss.
Walking 10,000 steps a day does more for your health than 3 gym sessions a week


MIXED GENERAL:

Soreness is not a measure of a good workout. Stop chasing it.
The fitness industry invented DOMS as a marketing tool
Your pre workout is giving you anxiety not energy
Foam rolling feels good. The evidence says it doesn't do much else.
Mobility work without strength work is pointless
The best exercise is the one you'll actually do consistently
You don't need to track macros to build muscle
Periodization is the most important concept in training that nobody teaches beginners
Deload weeks are not optional. They are part of the program.
The people with the best physiques in the gym are usually the quietest


Pick your favorites and drop them into your Friday hot take slot. Rotate through topics so your feed isn't all deadlift takes or all cardio takes. So maybe say structuring so you get stronger over time or something idShow moreMar 16Claude responded: Good catch — progressive overload is the method, not the byproduct.Good catch — progressive overload is the method, not the byproduct. Getting stronger over time is the result. Here's the adjusted script:

Hook:
"The 2 workouts a week that stop muscle loss."
Spoken:
"So what does that actually look like? Two sessions a week. Full body each time. Compound movements — squat, hinge, push, pull. Structured so you're getting stronger over time. That's the whole framework. No six day splits. No two hour sessions. No fancy equipment. Just two intentional sessions where you're challenging your muscles enough to send the signal to grow. And week by week, month by month — you get stronger. That's how you stop muscle loss. I'm a PT student and a recreational bodybuilder. I've been building this system for years. And I'm going to break down exactly how to do it."
End card:
"I'm the Muscle SPT. Like and follow for more."

What changed:

Replaced the progressive overload definition with "structured so you're getting stronger over time" — simpler, accurate, flows naturally
Added "And week by week, month by month — you get stronger" — reinforces the outcome before the sign-off so the viewer leaves with the result in their head not the method
You said: You’re not eating enough of this.You’re not eating enough of this.”
Spoken:
“You can train perfectly and still lose muscle. If your protein intake is too low your body has nothing to build with. The research is clear — to preserve and build muscle you need roughly 0.7 to 1 gram of protein per pound of bodyweight per day. Most people are getting half that. And it gets worse as you age — older adults actually need more protein than younger people to stimulate the same muscle building response. Not less. More. I’m a PT student and a recreational bodybuilder. I’ve been dialing in my nutrition for years. And this is the one thing I see people get wrong more than anything else.” Research is 0.7 gram per lb protein 
End card:
“I’m the Muscle SPT. Like and follow for more.”Mar 16Claude responded: The 0.The 0.7g per lb is the minimum end of the range — that's solid and defensible. Here's the tightened script:

Hook:
"You're not eating enough of this."
Spoken:
"You can train perfectly and still lose muscle. If your protein intake is too low your body has nothing to build with. The research is clear — to preserve and build muscle you need at least 0.7 grams of protein per pound of bodyweight per day. Most people are getting half that. And it gets worse as you age — older adults actually need more protein than younger people to stimulate the same muscle building response. Not less. More. I'm a PT student and a recreational bodybuilder. I've been dialing in my nutrition for years. And this is the one thing I see people get wrong more than anything else."
End card:
"I'm the Muscle SPT. Like and follow for more."

What changed:

"roughly 0.7 to 1 gram" → "at least 0.7 grams" — more confident, more accurate, and you're not overclaiming the upper end of the range
Everything else was already clean — didn't touch it

You now have 4 scripts ready to film. That's your first month of educational content sorted. The first 4Mar 18Claude responded: Your first 4 videos are:Your first 4 videos are:

Sarcopenia intro
Fitness industry lying about training volume
Two sessions a week framework
Protein intake

The hot take that ties them all together:

Hook:
"Your doctor has never once talked to you about muscle loss. And that's a problem."
Spoken:
"You go in for your annual checkup. They check your blood pressure. Your cholesterol. Your weight. Maybe your BMI. And they send you home. Nobody talks about your muscle mass. Nobody mentions sarcopenia. Nobody tells you that after 30 you're losing 3 to 8 percent of your muscle every decade. Nobody tells you that two resistance training sessions a week and 0.7 grams of protein per pound of bodyweight could change the entire trajectory of how you age. That information exists. The research is there. But the medical system isn't built to prevent. It's built to treat. And by the time they treat it — it's already too late. I'm a PT student. And I'm done waiting for the system to catch up."
End card:
"I'm the Muscle SPT. Like and follow for more."

This works perfectly as your Friday hot take after four educational videos because it ties every stat you've already taught them — sarcopenia, two sessions, 0.7 grams protein — into one provocative argument. Your audience already knows the information. Now you're giving them a villain. That drives comments.You said: How should this be structured?How should this be structured? You said: For hot takes, should these be non talking reels?For hot takes, should these be non talking reels? Or similar to my education reels?Mar 18Claude responded: Same format as your educational reels — talking head, mic, yellow-green light, straight to camera.Same format as your educational reels — talking head, mic, yellow-green light, straight to camera.
Here's why — hot takes need a face behind them. A text-based or voiceover hot take feels like a random opinion floating on the internet. You speaking directly to camera makes it personal and credible. You're not just saying "doctors don't talk about muscle loss" — you're a PT student saying it. That's what gives it weight and drives the comments.
The delivery changes though. Educational reels are calm and informative. Hot takes should have a little more edge. Not aggressive — just convicted. Like you genuinely believe what you're saying and you're not apologizing for it.
Same setup. Different energy.You said: So voiceovers can be for BTSSo voiceovers can be for BTSMar 18Claude responded: Yes exactly.Yes exactly.
BTS is the perfect format for voiceover. You're filming your training, your commute, your study session, your clinic prep — the camera isn't on your face, it's on what you're doing. Your voice narrates over the top of it. Raw, unscripted, authentic.
It actually works better as a voiceover because the visuals do half the work. The footage of you training or studying carries the atmosphere while your voice adds the story or insight on top.
So your format breakdown is:

Educational Reels — talking head, mic, camera
Hot Takes — talking head, mic, camera, more edge
Personal / Story — talking head, mic, camera, most vulnerable
BTS — voiceover over real footage, unscripted
Carousels — Canva, no video needed
Exercise Demos — talking head or voiceover depending on the movement

Clean and simple. Each format has a purpose and a place.You said: Is that enough variety?Is that enough variety? Education, hot takes, and personal are all same format. What if for personal i set up like fake podcast where Kim loookign away almost as if im talking to someone \Mar 18Claude responded: That's a great idea.That's a great idea. The fake podcast setup is one of the highest performing formats for personal and story content right now because it feels like you're being interviewed — which makes it feel more candid and less performed than straight to camera.
It works perfectly for your personal series because those episodes are conversational and reflective. Looking slightly off camera like you're answering a question creates an intimacy that talking head doesn't. It feels like the audience is overhearing something real rather than watching a presentation.

Your full format breakdown now:

Educational Reels — talking head, mic, straight to camera, calm and authoritative
Hot Takes — talking head, mic, straight to camera, convicted and direct
Personal / Story — podcast setup, mic, looking slightly off camera, conversational and vulnerable
BTS — voiceover over real footage, unscripted, raw
Carousels — Canva, no video
Exercise Demos — talking head or voiceover depending on movement


That's real variety. Same mic and light setup but four completely different visual experiences depending on the content type. Your audience subconsciously registers the format switch and it signals to them what kind of content they're about to watch before you even say a word.
One tip for the podcast setup — put a second mic on the desk even if it's not plugged in. Or a coffee cup. Something that makes it feel like a real conversation is happening just off screen. Small detail, big impact on the feel of it.You said: Give me another hot take oneGive me another hot take oneMar 18Claude responded: ---
Hook:
"Squatting is not bad for your knees. Not squatting is."
Spoken:
"I cannot tell you how many times I have heard this in clinic. My knees hurt so I stopped squatting. And I get it. It makes sense on the surface. Something hurts, you avoid it. But here's what the research actually shows. The muscles around your knee — your quads, your hamstrings, your glutes — are what protect that joint. When those muscles are weak the knee takes all the load. Squatting, done correctly, builds exactly those muscles. Avoiding it makes them weaker. Which makes your knee worse. Not better. I'm not saying push through pain. I'm saying weakness is not the answer. Strength is. I'm a PT student and a recreational bodybuilder. And this is what the science says."
End card:
"I'm the Muscle SPT. Like and follow for more."

This one is perfect because it directly challenges one of the most common pieces of advice people get — avoid squatting if your knees hurt. It's controversial enough to drive comments from people who disagree, but backed by enough clinical logic that you can defend every word of it. That's the sweet spot for a hot take as a PT student — provocative but defensible.You said: Give me example of soft sell post.Give me example of soft sell post. Is this a carouel reel one slide or whatMar 18Claude responded: Soft sell is best as a Story or a single static image post.Soft sell is best as a Story or a single static image post. Not a Reel, not a carousel. Here's why — it's a quiet invitation, not a performance. A story or single slide feels personal and low pressure. A Reel feels like an ad.

Story version — 3 slides:
Story Slide 1:
"I have 2 coaching spots opening this week."
Story Slide 2:
"If you're serious about building muscle and protecting your body long term — this is for you.
Men and women welcome.
DM me READY and I'll send you the details."
Story Slide 3:
"Not sure if it's right for you?
DM me anyway.
We'll figure it out together."

Single static post version:
Image: Clean dark background, #E8FF47 text
Text on image:
"2 spots open.
Built for people who are done guessing
and ready to build something that lasts.
DM me READY."
Caption:
"I keep my coaching roster small on purpose. Every person I work with gets my full attention — the PT student brain, the bodybuilder methodology, and a program built specifically for your body and your goals. If you've been thinking about it, now is the time. DM me READY and let's talk."

The rule for soft sell:
Never make it feel like an announcement. Make it feel like a quiet conversation between you and the right person. The wrong people scroll past. The right people DM you.You said: Okay so day 7 is not a post its a storyOkay so day 7 is not a post its a storyMar 18Claude responded: Exactly.Exactly. Sunday is never a feed post — it's always a Story.
Here's why it works better as a Story:
A soft sell on your feed feels like an ad and people scroll past it. A soft sell in your Stories feels like a private message to your most engaged followers — the people who are already watching your Stories are already warm. They know you, they trust you, they're way more likely to DM you than someone who stumbles on a feed post.
Stories also disappear in 24 hours which creates natural scarcity. "2 spots open" hits differently when they know the Story is gone tomorrow.
So your full weekly structure is actually:

MON — Educational Reel (feed)
TUE — Personal Podcast format (feed)
WED — Educational Reel or Exercise Demo (feed)
THU — Carousel (feed)
FRI — Hot Take (feed)
SAT — BTS voiceover (feed)
SUN — Soft Sell (Story only, never feed)

Six feed posts. One Story. Clean, sustainable, intentional. Every single one has a purpose.You said: And are exercise demos setup just in the gym rightAnd are exercise demos setup just in the gym rightMar 18Claude responded: Yes — gym is the natural home for exercise demos.Yes — gym is the natural home for exercise demos. That's where the movement actually happens so it's the most authentic setting.
A few options depending on what you're filming:
Talking head demo:
Set up your phone on a tripod, step back, demonstrate the movement, talk through the cues as you do it. Simple and effective.
Split format:
Your face talking through the cue first, then cut to the full movement demonstration. Shows your face and authority first, then the execution. Works really well for teaching cues.
Voiceover over the movement:
Film the exercise, narrate the cues over the top in editing. Clean and fast to produce. Good for when you don't want to talk and lift simultaneously.

One tip for gym demos:
Film in a quieter corner or at off-peak hours. Background noise, people walking through your shot, and crowded racks all lower the perceived production quality. Early morning or late evening is your best window.
Also — white coat stays home for demos. Athletic wear only in the gym. Keep each format visually consistent so your audience immediately knows what kind of content they're watching before you say a word.You said: Could i record these and then like use them as b-roll and talk over like I do for the education, hot takes reelsCould i record these and then like use them as b-roll and talk over like I do for the education, hot takes reelsMar 18Claude responded: Yes — that's actually the smartest workflow for demos.Yes — that's actually the smartest workflow for demos.
Here's why it works better than talking while lifting:
You can't cue properly while you're actually under load. Your breathing is off, your focus is split, and the teaching quality drops. Filming the movement cleanly first and then narrating over it means your demonstration is perfect and your coaching is perfect — separately.

The workflow:

Go to the gym, film all your exercise clips — clean reps, good angles, no talking
Come home, sit in front of your mic and yellow-green light
Watch the footage back and record your voiceover narrating exactly what you're seeing
Edit in CapCut or Captions — your voice over the gym footage


You now have two assets from one gym session:

The full demo clip for YouTube with your full cue breakdown narrated over it
The 30-60 second cut for Instagram with the key cue highlighted


Bonus:
Those gym clips become your b-roll library for everything. Educational reels, hot takes, personal content — any time you need footage of someone actually training you already have it. Film more than you think you need every session. You'll use it all eventually.You said: I already have a LOT of trining footage.I already have a LOT of trining footage. SO I can just use that then yeah?Mar 18Claude responded: Yes.Yes. That's your entire b-roll library already sitting on your phone.
Go through it this week and organize it into folders:

Squat variations
Hinge / deadlift
Push / press
Pull / rows
Cardio / conditioning
General training footage

Then every time you film a talking head or voiceover you just pull from the relevant folder and drop it in. No gym trip needed for b-roll ever again unless you want fresh footage.
That existing footage also means you can start producing demo content immediately without waiting to film anything new. Script the voiceover, pull the matching clip, done.
You have more content assets than you think. You just need to organize them.You said: What should I wear for my personal reels?What should I wear for my personal reels? Where I'm talking, kinda like a podcast format? Then what should I wear for the hot take reels? For the personal, should I wear, like, a button down, casual button down? Hot take? Should I wear a lab coat? What do you think?Mar 19Claude responded: Here's the breakdown:Here's the breakdown:

Personal / Podcast format:
Casual button down is perfect. It signals "I'm being real with you right now" — relaxed, approachable, human. Not trying too hard. It visually separates these episodes from your educational content which is what you want. When your audience sees the button down and the podcast angle they immediately know this is a different kind of content. More personal, more vulnerable.

Hot takes:
Athletic wear. Same as your educational reels. Here's why — hot takes need the bodybuilder credibility behind them. When you say "squatting is not bad for your knees" in a fitted athletic shirt your physique is part of the argument. It says I train, I live this, I'm not just theorizing. A lab coat on a hot take feels too clinical and removes the edge you need for that format.

The full visual identity by format:

Educational — athletic wear, mic, yellow-green light
Hot Take — athletic wear, mic, yellow-green light, more edge
Personal / Podcast — casual button down, mic, slightly off camera
BTS — whatever you're actually wearing, authentic
White Coat — reserved for your highest authority clinical content and the rebrand moment
Scrubs — rotations content only, earned not performed

Every outfit tells your audience what kind of content they're about to watch before you say a word.You said: So no white coat?So no white coat? When can I wear white coat?Mar 19Claude responded: You can wear it — just use it sparingly so it keeps its power.You can wear it — just use it sparingly so it keeps its power.
When to wear the white coat:

The rebrand announcement — Zach Active → The Muscle SPT. This is the moment it debuts. Make it count.
Sarcopenia and clinical science content — when you're citing research and want maximum authority behind the claim
Rotation content — when you're speaking directly about what you're seeing in clinic
Any content where you're explicitly positioning yourself as a future clinician — the "your doctor never told you this" style hot takes
Your YouTube channel art and profile photo — static authority anchor image

When NOT to wear it:

Exercise demos — athletic wear only
Hot takes about training — physique credibility matters more here
BTS — too staged, kills the authenticity
Personal podcast episodes — button down is warmer and more vulnerable


The rule:
White coat = your highest authority moments only. If you wear it every video it becomes wallpaper. If you wear it three or four times a month on your most clinical content it stays powerful every single time it appears.
Think of it like a suit. The person who wears a suit every day looks normal. The person who shows up in a suit once a month looks like something important is happening.You said: What color background for personal and hot takesWhat color background for personal and hot takesMar 19Claude responded: Personal / Podcast:Personal / Podcast:
Darker, moodier. Try a deep blue or a dim warm amber behind you. Something that feels intimate and conversational. Not your brand yellow-green — that's your educational identity. The color shift signals to your audience this is a different kind of content before you say a word.
Hot Takes:
Keep the yellow-green #E8FF47. Hot takes are still on-brand educational content just with more edge. The consistent color ties it back to your authority and your mission. When people see that yellow-green light they know it's you and they know you're about to say something worth listening to.

So your full color system by format:

Educational — #E8FF47 yellow-green
Hot Take — #E8FF47 yellow-green
Personal / Podcast — deep blue or warm amber
White Coat content — #E8FF47 yellow-green
BTS — natural, no background light needed
Exercise Demos — gym, natural environment

Two colors for your background light. Yellow-green for authority content. Something warmer or cooler for personal content. Simple and intentional.You said: I kinda wanna wear white coat for hot takesI kinda wanna wear white coat for hot takesMar 19Claude responded: Do it.Do it.
Actually makes a lot of sense for your specific hot takes. Most of yours are clinical — "your doctor never told you this", "squatting isn't bad for your knees", "the medical system isn't built to prevent." The white coat behind those statements hits differently than athletic wear. It says I'm not just a gym guy with an opinion — I'm a future clinician calling this out from the inside.
The contrast is actually powerful. White coat, yellow-green light, speaking directly to camera with conviction. That's a strong visual identity for hot takes specifically.

Updated format breakdown:

Educational — athletic wear, yellow-green light
Hot Take — white coat, yellow-green light
Personal / Podcast — casual button down, deep blue or warm amber light
White Coat clinical content — white coat, yellow-green light
BTS — whatever you're wearing, natural
Exercise Demos — athletic wear, gym

Now the white coat has two homes — hot takes and clinical authority content. That's enough usage to feel consistent without overexposing it. And it visually separates your hot takes from your educational reels which gives your feed more variety even when the background color is the same.You said: I have a call with a cash based PT owner later today as I want to eventually start my own later.I have a call with a cash based PT owner later today as I want to eventually start my own later. What are some good questions to prepare to askMar 20Claude responded: Great opportunity.Great opportunity. Here's a full list organized by category — pick the ones most relevant to where you are right now:

STARTING OUT:

When did you know you were ready to open your own practice?
What did you wish you had done differently before opening?
How long did it take from idea to first patient?
What was your biggest fear going in and was it justified?
Did you have a business partner or did you go solo?


THE CASH PAY MODEL:

How did you decide on your session rate and how has it evolved?
What's your biggest challenge convincing patients to pay out of pocket?
How do you handle objections when people compare your rate to insurance copays?
What's your average patient retention and how did you build it?
Do you offer packages or pay per session and which works better?


BUILDING A PATIENT BASE:

Where did your first patients come from?
How much of your growth has been referral based vs marketing?
How important has social media been for your practice?
Did you have an audience or following before you opened?
What marketing channels actually moved the needle?


OPERATIONS & FINANCES:

What were your startup costs and what do you wish you'd budgeted for?
How long until you were profitable?
What does your overhead look like monthly?
Did you start in a dedicated space or rent by the hour?
What software do you use for scheduling, billing, and documentation?
What does your typical week look like in terms of patient load?


THE SOCIAL MEDIA ANGLE:

How has having an online presence affected your in-person practice?
Do your online followers convert to in-person patients?
Would you build the audience first or the clinic first if you did it again?
How do you balance content creation with running a practice?


ADVICE FOR YOUR SPECIFIC SITUATION:

I'm a PT student with 188K Instagram followers in the muscle building and injury prevention space — what would you prioritize in my position?
When would you start laying the groundwork for a cash practice while still in school?
What do you know now that would have changed everything if you'd known it on day one?


The most important one to ask:
"What's the one thing you wish someone had told you before you started?"
That question always gets the most honest and valuable answer of the whole conversation. Save it for the end.
Take notes during the call and come back and we can debrief after. This person's experience is worth mapping directly onto your 5-year roadmap.You said: Can you make me a coaching contract?Can you make me a coaching contract? Talk about pertinent online coaching information whatever will prevent lawsuits or liabilities and protects privacy as client and coaching structure and license. Also, cancellations MUST be made before subscription renewal date. No execrations. Turn this into a PDF so I can attach to Superset app for clientsMar 20Claude responded: Let me read the PDF skill first to make sure this is built correctly.Let me read the PDF skill first to make sure this is built correctly.Ran 3 commands, viewed a file, created a fileRan 3 commands, viewed a file, created a fileYour coaching contract is ready. Here's what's covered across 13 sections:

Parties & Effective Date — client info fields, program start date
Scope of Services — what's included: training program, nutrition guidance, check-ins, form feedback
Medical Disclaimer — clearly states you're an SPT not a licensed PT, not medical advice
Health Disclosure & PAR-Q — client confirms they're cleared for exercise
Billing & Subscription Terms — recurring monthly, failed payment policy, no refunds
Cancellation Policy — highlighted in yellow, no exceptions, must cancel before renewal date, 48 hours minimum notice
Privacy & Confidentiality — no sharing client data, no using photos without separate consent
Assumption of Risk & Liability Waiver — protects you from injury claims
Intellectual Property — programs are for personal use only, no redistribution
Communication & Response Times — 48 hour response, Superset as primary platform
Termination by Coach — your right to end the agreement
Governing Law — fill in your state
Entire Agreement — amendment rights

Two things to do before using it:

Fill in your state in Section 12
Have a lawyer review it — this is a solid starting framework but a licensed attorney should sign off before you use it with paying clients
Muscle spt coaching contractDocument · PDF DownloadYou said: Are there any sites that make solid agreement contract forms?Are there any sites that make solid agreement contract forms?Mar 20Claude responded: Yes — a few good options depending on how much you want to customize:Yes — a few good options depending on how much you want to customize:

Best for fitness/coaching specifically:
Coaches Console — has coaching agreement templates built specifically for online coaches. Clean, professional, legally oriented.
Bonsai — probably the best overall for your situation. Built for freelancers and service providers. Has coaching contract templates, e-signature built in, automatic invoicing, and client management. You send the contract, client signs digitally, it's stored automatically. Around $17-25/mo.
HoneyBook — similar to Bonsai. Contract templates, e-signature, invoicing, payment processing all in one. Popular with service based businesses. Around $16/mo.

Free or low cost:
Docusign — not templates but the gold standard for e-signature. You upload your contract and send it for digital signature. Free tier available.
HelloSign — same as Docusign, cleaner interface, free for limited documents per month.
Google Docs + DocuSign — write it in Google Docs, send via DocuSign for signature. Free and functional.

Legal template sites:
LegalZoom — paid but attorney reviewed templates
Rocket Lawyer — subscription based, has fitness and coaching specific templates, attorney review available

My honest recommendation for your stage:
Use the contract I built you as your base. Upload it to Bonsai or HelloSign for e-signature capability. That way clients sign digitally before accessing Superset and you have a paper trail for every client automatically. Clean, professional, zero chasing paper signatures.You said: Why do they all cost so much moneyWhy do they all cost so much moneyMar 20Claude responded: They don't have to.They don't have to. Here's the free stack that does everything you need right now:

Free e-signature:
DocuSign — free plan gives you 3 documents per month. At your current client volume that's plenty.
Or even simpler — just use this:
Google Docs + Google Forms

Put the contract in a Google Doc
Client reads it
They sign a Google Form at the bottom confirming they've read and agreed
Form response gets saved automatically with their name, date, and email
That's your paper trail

Not as legally airtight as a wet signature but for online coaching at your stage it's completely standard and widely accepted.

The honest reality:
You're a PT student just starting to take coaching clients. You don't need a $25/mo contract platform right now. That's a Phase 2 or Phase 3 expense when you have 20+ clients and need automated systems.
Right now you need:

A solid contract — done, you have it
A way to get clients to sign it — Google Form is free and works
A place to store the signed agreements — Google Drive, also free

As you scale and the admin becomes unmanageable then Bonsai or HoneyBook makes sense. Not now.
Keep your overhead at zero for as long as possible. Every dollar saved is a dollar toward the clinic.You said: How To Manage Business Writeoffs for TaxesHow To Manage Business Writeoffs for TaxesMar 20Claude responded: Good question to be thinking about early.Good question to be thinking about early. Here's the practical breakdown for your situation:

First — you need to be operating as a business.
Before you can write anything off you need to either be a sole proprietor (simplest, automatic when you earn self-employment income) or an LLC. At your stage sole proprietor is fine. The moment you start taking coaching payments you are a business in the eyes of the IRS whether you've registered anything or not.

What you can write off as an online coaching business:
Technology & Software:

Superset subscription
Canva Pro
CapCut or Mojo paid plans
Stan Store fees
Kit email platform
Squarespace website
Domain registration
Any apps you pay for to run the business

Equipment:

Your mic
Ring light or background light
Tripod
Phone if used primarily for business
Laptop or iPad if used for business
Camera if you buy one

Education:

Books, courses, certifications related to your business
PT school tuition may be partially deductible as business education

Home Office:

If you have a dedicated space where you film and work you can deduct a portion of rent and utilities based on square footage

Marketing:

Any paid ads you run
Graphic design costs
Business cards

Professional Services:

Accountant fees
Legal fees for contract review


How to actually manage it:
Step 1 — Open a separate bank account for business income and expenses. This is the single most important thing. When your coaching money goes into one account and your business expenses come out of the same account your bookkeeping is almost automatic.
Step 2 — Track everything in a simple spreadsheet. Date, amount, category, description. Do it weekly not at tax time. Takes 5 minutes a week, saves hours in April.
Step 3 — Keep every receipt. Screenshot them, email them to yourself, use a free app like Wave or Keeper to photograph and categorize receipts in real time.
Step 4 — Set aside 25-30% of every coaching payment for taxes. Self-employment tax is real and it will surprise you if you don't plan for it. Put it in a separate savings account and don't touch it.
Step 5 — Use free accounting software. Wave is completely free and handles income tracking, expense categorization, and basic reporting. More than enough at your stage.

At tax time:
You'll file a Schedule C with your personal tax return as a sole proprietor. It lists your income and deductions. The difference is your taxable profit.

Honest recommendation:
Find a CPA who works with small businesses or self-employed people for your first tax year. One session with a good accountant pays for itself in deductions they find that you missed. After that first year you'll understand the system well enough to handle most of it yourself.
Don't overcomplicate it right now. Separate bank account, track expenses weekly, save 25-30% for taxes. That's the whole system at your stage.You said: I want to be an LLC  eventuallyI want to be an LLC  eventuallyMar 20Claude responded: Smart move — here's when and how:Smart move — here's when and how:

When to do it:
Not right now. Form the LLC when you have consistent coaching revenue coming in — around $1,000-2,000 per month consistently. Before that the cost and admin overhead isn't worth it for the protection it provides.
The honest trigger points are:

You have multiple paying clients
You're generating consistent monthly revenue
You're about to launch a digital product or group program
You feel exposed liability-wise

That's probably Phase 2 of your roadmap — around month 6-18.

Why an LLC makes sense for you specifically:

Separates your personal assets from business liability — if a client ever sues the business they can't come after your personal bank account, car, or belongings
Looks more professional to clients — The Muscle SPT LLC on a contract hits differently
Sets up the foundation for the clinic — when you eventually open The Muscle PT Clinic it's already a properly structured business entity
Tax flexibility — as you grow you can elect S-Corp status which saves significantly on self-employment taxes


How to form one when you're ready:
Step 1 — Choose your state.
Form it in the state you live and work in. Ignore the advice to form in Delaware or Wyoming — that's for large corporations, not small service businesses.
Step 2 — Choose a name.
The Muscle SPT LLC or Ermert Fitness LLC or whatever you want the legal entity to be called. Check availability on your state's Secretary of State website.
Step 3 — File the Articles of Organization.
Done directly on your state's Secretary of State website. Cost is typically $50-150 depending on the state. Takes about 15 minutes to file online.
Step 4 — Get an EIN.
Employer Identification Number — it's like a Social Security Number for your business. Free, instant, done at IRS.gov. You need this to open a business bank account.
Step 5 — Open a business bank account.
Take your LLC formation documents and EIN to a bank and open a dedicated business checking account. Keep this completely separate from personal finances.
Step 6 — Create an Operating Agreement.
A simple document that outlines how the LLC is run. Required in some states, best practice everywhere. Templates available free online.
Step 7 — Register for state taxes if required.
Some states require additional registration for sales tax or business taxes. Check your state's requirements.

Total cost to form an LLC yourself:
$50-150 in state filing fees. That's it. You don't need a lawyer or a service like LegalZoom to do this — the state websites are straightforward.

One important thing:
Once you have the LLC, all coaching income goes into the LLC bank account and all business expenses come out of it. You pay yourself a distribution from the LLC into your personal account. That separation is what gives you the liability protection. If you mix personal and business finances the LLC protection can be pierced in court — called "piercing the corporate veil." Keep them completely separate always.

Add LLC formation to your Phase 2 checklist in Notion. Right now focus on generating the revenue that makes it worth having.You said: STARTING OUT:STARTING OUT:    ∙    When did you know you were ready to open your own practice?

SAVE FOR LAST — Always end with this:
"What's the one thing you wish someone had told you before you started?"

One tip for the call:
Don't read the list robotically. Let the conversation flow naturally and check off questions as they come up organically. The list is just your safety net to make sure you don't leave without the most important answers.You said: I don’t think he has an online presenceI don’t think he has an online presenceMar 20Claude responded: That's actually more valuable than you think — not less.That's actually more valuable than you think — not less.
Here's why. He built a cash pay practice the hard way. No audience, no social media funnel, no pre-built trust. Pure clinical skill, referral networks, word of mouth, and in-person reputation. That's the foundation of what makes a cash practice actually work long term.
You're going to have both — the organic growth engine he didn't have AND the clinical foundation he built without it. That combination is your unfair advantage.

What this means for your questions:
Drop the social media angle questions entirely — they won't be relevant to his experience. Replace that time with deeper questions on the things he actually had to figure out the hard way:

How did you get your first 10 patients with no existing audience?
How long did word of mouth take to become a reliable patient source?
What was your referral strategy — other practitioners, physicians, gyms?
Did you partner with any gyms, trainers, or sports teams early on?
How did you compete with insurance-based clinics when patients had never heard of you?


The reframe going into this call:
You're not learning how to replicate what he did. You're learning the floor — the minimum viable path to a successful cash practice with zero audience. Everything you're building on social media is upside on top of that floor.
Come back and tell me what he says. Genuinely curious what someone who built it the hard way thinks about your position.You said: But I still want his advice or input on me who has thatBut I still want his advice or input on me who has thatMar 20Claude responded: Absolutely still ask.Absolutely still ask. Frame it as a hypothetical and let him react to your situation. Someone who built it without an audience will have a strong opinion on what they would have done differently if they had one.

How to phrase it:
Instead of "how has social media affected your practice" which doesn't apply to him, say:
"I'm coming at this from a different angle than you did — I have 188K Instagram followers in the muscle building and injury prevention space before I even graduate. If you were in my position, what would you be doing right now to set yourself up for a cash practice?"
Then stop talking and let him answer.
That question will get you his most valuable insight of the entire call because you're asking him to apply everything he learned the hard way to a situation he wishes he'd had. He'll tell you exactly what he would have done with that head start.

Follow up with:

"What do you think is the biggest mistake someone with an audience makes when trying to convert followers to patients?"
"Do you think building an audience before opening actually changes the timeline to profitability?"
"If you had 188K followers today would you open sooner or build the audience further first?"

Those three follow-ups will give you a complete picture of how an experienced cash pay PT thinks about the audience advantage you're sitting on.
He may not have lived it but he'll have a very clear opinion on it. That opinion is worth a lot.You said: And I have coaching businessAnd I have coaching businessMar 20Claude responded: Even better.Even better. Lead with the full picture when you ask him.

Frame it like this:
"I'm a PT student with 188K Instagram followers in the muscle building and injury prevention space. I already have an online coaching business running through that audience — training programs, nutrition guidance, that kind of thing. My goal is to eventually open a cash pay PT clinic. Given all of that — what would you be doing in my position right now to set yourself up for the fastest path to a successful cash practice?"

That framing changes everything because now he's not just reacting to an audience — he's reacting to someone who already has:

Proof of concept that people will pay you
An existing client base to convert to in-person patients
Revenue coming in before the clinic opens
A brand already built around the exact niche a cash PT clinic needs

He's going to see immediately that you're not starting from zero like he did. You're starting from a position he never had. His reaction to that will tell you a lot about what he thinks the fastest path forward actually looks like.

The follow up after he responds:
"Based on what I just described — what's the one thing you think could slow me down or trip me up that I might not see coming?"
That's where the gold is. Not the validation of what you're doing right. The blind spot you haven't thought of yet.You said: Ok frame that into and list additional questionsOk frame that into and list additional questionsMar 20Claude responded: Here's your full reframed question list for this specific call:Here's your full reframed question list for this specific call:

OPEN WITH THIS — Set the context first:
"I'm a PT student with 188K Instagram followers in the muscle building and injury prevention space. I already have an online coaching business running through that audience — training programs, nutrition guidance, that kind of thing. My goal is to eventually open a cash pay PT clinic. Given all of that — what would you be doing in my position right now to set yourself up for the fastest path to a successful cash practice?"

FOLLOW UP IMMEDIATELY WITH:

Based on what I just described — what's the one thing you think could slow me down or trip me up that I might not see coming?
Do you think having an existing audience and coaching revenue changes the timeline to opening a clinic meaningfully?
Would you open sooner because of the audience advantage or wait until you're fully licensed regardless?
Do you think online coaching clients convert well to in-person clinic patients or are they different audiences entirely?
What would you prioritize building right now — the audience further, the coaching revenue, or the clinical skills?


HIS JOURNEY — Learn from how he built it:

Where did your very first patients come from with no existing audience?
How did you get your first 10 paying cash patients?
What was your referral strategy early on — other practitioners, physicians, gyms, trainers?
Did you partner with any gyms or sports teams to build your patient base initially?
How did you compete with insurance-based clinics when patients had never heard of you?
How long until word of mouth became a reliable and consistent patient source?


CASH PAY MODEL — The numbers and structure:

How did you decide on your session rate and how has it evolved over time?
How do you handle objections when patients compare your rate to an insurance copay?
Do you offer packages or pay per session and which has worked better for retention?
What does your overhead look like monthly and what surprised you most about the costs?
How long did it take from opening to being consistently profitable?
Did you start in a dedicated space or rent by the hour and which would you recommend?


OPERATIONS — The stuff nobody talks about:

What software do you use for scheduling, billing, and documentation?
What were your actual startup costs and what do you wish you had budgeted for?
What does a typical patient week look like for you in terms of volume and hours?
Did you go solo or did you have a business partner and would you do it the same way again?


MINDSET & ADVICE:

What was your biggest fear going in and was it justified?
What did you wish you had done differently before opening?
What do you know now that would have completely changed how you started?


SAVE FOR LAST — Always end with this:
"What's the one thing you wish someone had told you before you started?"

How to run the call:
Open with the framing paragraph. Let him react fully before moving on. Check questions off naturally as they come up in conversation — don't read the list robotically. The best answers will come from following up on what he says, not from the prepared questions themselves.
You have about 45-60 minutes of questions here. If the conversation flows naturally you won't get through all of them and that's fine. The first two sections are the most valuable — everything else is a bonus.

1. The opener — let him react to your full situation first:
"I'm a PT student with 188K Instagram followers, an online coaching business already running, and a goal to open a cash pay clinic. If you were in my position — what would you be doing right now?"

10. What's the one thing you wish someone had told you before you started?

1. The opener — let him react to your full situation first:
"I'm a PT student with 188K Instagram followers, an online coaching business already running, and a goal to open a cash pay clinic. If you were in my position — what would you be doing right now?"
2. What's the one thing that could slow me down or trip me up that I probably don't see coming?

4. What was your referral strategy early on — other practitioners, physicians, gyms, trainers?
5. Do you think an online audience converts to in-person clinic patients or are they a completely different customer?
6. How did you set your session rate and how has it evolved?
7. How do you handle the objection when patients compare your cash rate to an insurance copay?

12. Did you start in a dedicated space or rent by the hour — and what would you recommend for someone just starting out?
13. What's the one thing you wish someone had told you before you started?

Thirteen questions but the first one will eat 10 minutes on its own if he's generous with his answer. Let the conversation breathe and check them off naturally. What were the very first steps you took when you decided you wanted to go out on your own?
Did you have a business background going into it or did you figure it out as you went? Did you hire anyone or lean on people in your network who knew the business and financial side?
What did the financial side actually look like — how did you fund it and what were the real startup costs?
Why Encinitas specifically and how have you navigated operating in one of the more expensive markets in Southern California?
How did you get your first 10 paying cash patients with no existing audience?
What was your referral strategy early on — other practitioners, physicians, gyms, trainers?
How did you set your session rate and how has it evolved over time?
How do you handle the objection when patients compare your cash rate to an insurance copay?
Given everything I just told you about my situation — what would you be doing in my position right now?
Do you think an online audience converts to in-person clinic patients or are they a completely different customer?
What's the one thing you wish someone had told you before you started?

Hook (on screen, one word at a time):
"Most people are squatting wrong for their spine."

Spoken (voiceover over your b-roll footage):
"This is the first video in my compound lift series. Squat. Hinge. Push. Pull. Four movements. Everything you need.
And we're starting with the squat — but not the one you're thinking of.
We're doing the pendulum squat. And here's why.
When most people think compound squat movement they go straight to the barbell back squat. And look — it's a great exercise. But it comes with a tradeoff. You're loading a bar on your spine. That creates shear forces running down through your vertebrae. And for a lot of people — especially those training for longevity — that's unnecessary stress on your spine over time.
The pendulum squat eliminates that. You're fixed under the machine. No balancing act. No coordination tax. Just pure quad and lower body loading.
And here's what makes the pendulum specifically so effective. The foot plate is angled. That angle allows for greater dorsiflexion at the ankle — meaning your ankle can flex more freely. More dorsiflexion means more knee flexion. More knee flexion means deeper range of motion through the squat. Hook:
"The squat variation I'd recommend to almost anyone."

Spoken:
"This is the first video in my compound lift series. Squat. Hinge. Push. Pull. Four movements. Everything you need.
We're starting with the pendulum squat. And here's why I choose this over a barbell back squat for most people.
When you load a bar on your spine you introduce shear forces running down through your vertebrae. For people training for longevity — that's unnecessary stress over time. The pendulum squat eliminates that. You're fixed under the machine. No balancing act. No coordination tax. Just pure lower body loading.
And the foot plate on a pendulum is angled. That angle creates more dorsiflexion at the ankle — which allows for more knee flexion — which means a deeper range of motion and more muscle fiber recruitment. That's why I choose the pendulum over a standard leg press or hack squat.
Now the cues.
Foot placement shoulder width apart. Two contact points through the foot — the medial aspect of the heel and the first metatarsal head, the base of your big toe. Those two points stay in contact with the plate the entire rep.
Arms stay below the torso. Not grabbing the handles above. This keeps the spine neutral and eliminates the excessive lumbar extension you commonly see in squat patterns. Hands low, chest tall, spine neutral.
On the way down I'm taking a 2 to 4 second eccentric. Slow and controlled. And as I descend I'm thinking about shifting my weight from the medial heel through that first metatarsal head — almost replicating the gait cycle, like you're about to take a step.
Now depth.
You may have heard of the butt wink — that posterior pelvic tilt you see at the bottom of a squat where the lower back rounds. There's nothing inherently wrong with it. But when it happens you lose tension through the quads — and the quad is the primary muscle I'm targeting here.
So I descend to just before that point. For me that's roughly 100 degrees of knee flexion or more depending on the day.
But here's what makes the pendulum different. The way this machine is designed — with the angled foot plate and the arc of the movement — it actually allows me to go into relatively full knee flexion without triggering that butt wink. Even with my long femurs and long torso, which typically make deep squatting more challenging, I can get really deep on this machine without losing pelvic position.
So I go all the way down. I own that bottom position. I keep tension through the quads the entire time. And then I drive back up under control.
This is time under tension done properly. A high degree of mechanical tension on the quads throughout the entire range of motion. Your glutes are getting stimulus too — but make no mistake, this is a quad dominant movement.
Controlled down. Tension maintained. Drive up."

End card:
"I'm the Muscle SPT. Like and follow for more."

That's a complete, polished exercise demo script. Sounds like you talking — not like it was written. Clean up any words that don't feel natural when you say it out loud and record the voiceover in one or two takes. That I can repeat for every exercise demo videos a good hook that, like, draws people in.Mar 20Claude responded: Here are your options:Here are your options:

Strongest:
"Here's how I actually train."
Simple, personal, curiosity-driven. Makes people want to watch because it implies there's a gap between how most people train and how you do it.

Runner up:
"The way most people do this is costing them gains."
Slightly more provocative. Works for almost any exercise because there's always a common mistake to address.

Third option:
"This is what intelligent training actually looks like."
Positions every demo as the PT-informed version of training. Ties directly to your brand.

My pick is "Here's how I actually train."
It's personal, it's repeatable across every single exercise, and it doesn't overpromise. It just opens a door. The viewer's natural response is — okay, show me. And then you do.
Every demo starts the same way. Same hook, same end card, same format. That repetition is what turns a series into something people binge.You said: And make it tie into the whole sarcopenia muscle loss muscle gain narrative.And make it tie into the whole sarcopenia muscle loss muscle gain narrative. On how you should be doing this, blah blah blah. It tied to something like that or along the same line message, vision type thing.Mar 20Claude responded: Here are your options:Here are your options:

Strongest:

Ties directly to the mission. Every exercise demo becomes part of the longevity narrative without having to explain it every time. Simple, repeatable, on brand.

More explicit on the sarcopenia angle. Slightly longer but lands harder for the 35-60 demographic.

Third option:

Clean. Positions every demo as intentional, clinical, and long-term focused rather than just another gym video.

Fourth option:
"You're not just training. You're investing in the body you'll have at 60."
More emotional. Higher share potential. Slightly harder to repeat verbatim every video without it feeling formulaic.

My pick is "This is how you build muscle that lasts."
Seven words. Says everything. Works for the pendulum squat, works for a row, works for a hip hinge. Hook (on screen, one word at a time):
"How to squat to build muscle and prevent injury."

Spoken:
"This is the first video in my compound lift series. Squat. Hinge. Push. Pull. Four movements. Everything you need to build muscle and protect your body for life.
We're starting with the squat — but not the one you're thinking of.
We're doing the pendulum squat. And here's why.
When you load a barbell on your spine you introduce shear forces running down through your vertebrae. For anyone training for longevity that's unnecessary stress over time. The pendulum squat eliminates that. You're fixed under the machine. No balancing act. No coordination tax. Just pure lower body loading.
The foot plate on a pendulum is angled. That angle creates more dorsiflexion at the ankle — which allows for more knee flexion — which means a deeper range of motion and more muscle fiber recruitment. That's why I choose this over a standard leg press or hack squat.
Now the cues.
Foot placement shoulder width apart. Two contact points through the foot — the medial aspect of the heel and the first metatarsal head, the base of your big toe. Those two points stay in contact with the plate the entire rep.
Arms stay below the torso. Not grabbing the handles above. This keeps the spine neutral and eliminates the excessive lumbar extension you commonly see in squat patterns. Hands low, chest tall, spine neutral.
On the way down I'm taking a 2 to 4 second eccentric. Slow and controlled. As I descend I'm shifting my weight from the medial heel through that first metatarsal head — almost replicating the gait cycle, like you're about to take a step.
Now depth.
You may have heard of the butt wink — that posterior pelvic tilt at the bottom of a squat where the lower back rounds. There's nothing inherently wrong with it. But when it happens you lose tension through the quads — and the quad is the primary muscle I'm targeting here.
So I descend to just before that point.
But here's what makes the pendulum different. The way this machine is designed — with the angled foot plate and the arc of the movement — it allows me to go into relatively full knee flexion without triggering that butt wink. Even with my long femurs and long torso, which typically make deep squatting more challenging, I can get really deep on this machine without losing pelvic position.
So I go all the way down. I own that bottom position. I keep tension through the quads the entire time. And then I drive back up under control.
This is time under tension done properly. A high degree of mechanical tension on the quads throughout the full range of motion. Your glutes are getting stimulus too — but make no mistake, this is a quad dominant movement.
Controlled down. Tension maintained. Drive up.
That's how you squat to build muscle and prevent injury for life."

Hook (on screen, one word at a time):
