Spoken:
"This is the first video in my compound lift series. Squat. Hinge. Push. Pull. Four movements. Everything you need to build muscle and protect your body for life.
We're starting with the squat — but not the one you're thinking of.
We're doing the pendulum squat. And here's why.
When you load a barbell on your spine you introduce shear forces running down through your vertebrae. Over time that cumulative spinal compression can contribute to degenerative joint disease and accelerated wear on your discs and facet joints. The pendulum squat doesn't eliminate spinal loading entirely — but it significantly reduces it. You're fixed under the machine, the resistance profile is different, and you're not literally bearing a loaded bar on your back. That's a meaningful difference for anyone training hard over decades.
And for people focused on building muscle for life — not just for right now — that distinction matters.
The foot plate on a pendulum is angled. That angle creates more dorsiflexion at the ankle — which allows for more knee flexion — which means a deeper range of motion and more muscle fiber recruitment. That's why I choose this over a standard leg press or hack squat.
Now the cues.
Foot placement shoulder width apart. Two contact points through the foot — the medial aspect of the heel and the first metatarsal head, the base of your big toe. Those two points stay in contact with the plate the entire rep.
Arms stay below the torso. Not grabbing the handles above. This keeps the spine neutral and eliminates the excessive lumbar extension you commonly see in squat patterns. Hands low, chest tall, spine neutral.
On the way down I'm taking a 2 to 4 second eccentric. Slow and controlled. As I descend I'm shifting my weight from the medial heel through that first metatarsal head — almost replicating the gait cycle, like you're about to take a step.
Now depth.
You do not need to go into full knee flexion here. As you can see I don't. I'm going to roughly 100 to 120 degrees of knee flexion and stopping there intentionally.
The reason is the butt wink. That's the posterior pelvic tilt you see at the bottom of a squat where the lower back rounds under. And let me be clear — there is nothing inherently wrong with the butt wink. It's a natural movement pattern and going deeper is not going to hurt you.
But in this case I'm training for quad hypertrophy. And the moment that butt wink kicks in I start losing tension through the quads. So I stop just before it. I own that position. I keep maximum mechanical tension on the quad throughout the entire range of motion.
But here's what makes the pendulum different. The way this machine is designed — with the angled foot plate and the arc of the movement — it actually allows me to get really deep even with my long femurs and long torso, which typically make deep squatting more challenging. More depth than I'd get on a standard squat without the same pelvic compensation.
So I go to my end range. I own that bottom position. I keep tension through the quads the entire time. And then I drive back up under control.
This is time under tension done properly. A high degree of mechanical tension on the quads throughout the full range of motion. Your glutes are getting stimulus too — but make no mistake, this is a quad dominant movement.
Controlled down. Tension maintained. Drive up.
That's how you squat to build muscle and prevent injury for life."

Under a minute is too short to cover rationale, cues, and muscles properly. Over 2 minutes loses people on short form. 90 seconds is the target — comprehensive enough to be valuable, short enough to hold attention.
Here's the condensed script hitting every point:

Hook:
"How to squat to build muscle and prevent injury."

Spoken:
"First video in my compound lift series. Squat. Hinge. Push. Pull. Everything you need to build muscle and protect your body for life.
We're doing the pendulum squat. Here's the rationale.
A barbell back squat loads directly onto your spine — shear forces, spinal compression, cumulative wear over time. The pendulum squat significantly reduces that. You're fixed under the machine, the resistance profile is different, and you're not bearing a loaded bar on your back. For anyone training for longevity that's a meaningful tradeoff. It can also offset some of that long term degenerative joint disease risk at the spine.
The angled foot plate creates more dorsiflexion at the ankle — more knee flexion — deeper range of motion — more muscle recruitment. That's why I choose this over a leg press or hack squat.
Muscles being trained. Primary — quads. The four muscles on the front of your thigh. You'll get glute activation no matter what — the glutes contribute to almost every lower body movement. And there's some debate around proximal hamstring involvement at deeper ranges. But make no mistake — this is a quad dominant exercise.
Now the cues.
Shoulder width foot placement. Two contact points — medial heel and first metatarsal head, the base of your big toe. Keep those glued to the plate the whole rep.
Arms below the torso — not grabbing above. Keeps your spine neutral and eliminates excess lumbar movement through the squat.
Two to four second eccentric on the way down. As you descend think about shifting weight from your heel through that big toe — almost like replicating your gait cycle.
Depth. I go to about 100 to 120 degrees of knee flexion. You don't need full flexion. I stop just before the butt wink — that posterior pelvic tilt at the bottom where the lower back rounds under. Nothing inherently wrong with it. But I lose quad tension when it kicks in so I stop before it. Full quad tension. Entire range. Every rep.
Controlled down. Tension maintained. Drive up.
That's how you squat to build muscle and prevent injury for life."

End card:
"I'm the Muscle SPT. Like and follow for more."

That runs about 90 seconds at a natural pace. Comprehensive, clinical, educational — without being a lecture. Every demo in the series follows this exact structure: rationale, muscles, cues, depth, drive. Same format every time.You said: Say the resistance profile is smootherSay the resistance profile is smootherMar 20Claude responded: Here's the updated line — swap this into the rationale section:Here's the updated line — swap this into the rationale section:

"You're fixed under the machine, the resistance profile is smoother throughout the movement, and you're not bearing a loaded bar on your back."

Everything else stays the same. "Smoother resistance profile" is the right way to describe it — the pendulum arc distributes load more evenly through the range of motion compared to the abrupt loading of a barbell squat. Clean and accurate.You said: That, more deflection, add more muscle fiber recruitment, greater length tension on the on your quads.That, more deflection, add more muscle fiber recruitment, greater length tension on the on your quads. More potential for internal force production, which means more growthMar 20Claude responded: Here's the updated rationale section — swap this in:Here's the updated rationale section — swap this in:

"The angled foot plate creates more dorsiflexion at the ankle — more knee flexion — greater range of motion. And that greater range of motion means more muscle fiber recruitment, a more advantageous length tension relationship on the quads, and greater potential for internal force production. More force production means more mechanical tension. More mechanical tension means more growth. That's why I choose this over a leg press or hack squat."

That's the full physiological chain explained clearly — dorsiflexion to knee flexion to range of motion to fiber recruitment to length tension to force production to growth. Each step leads logically to the next. Sounds clinical without being inaccessible. Your audience will actually understand why the machine choice matters rather than just taking your word for it.You said: That last part's a little don't even know what the fuck.That last part's a little don't even know what the fuck. So anything that sounds like jargon, let's make it into more layman terms because I want them to be able to understand these conceptsMar 20Claude responded: Got it.Got it. Here's the full clean script — no jargon, no fragmented phrases, all real sentences:

Hook:
"How to squat to build muscle and prevent injury."

Spoken:
"This is the first video in my compound lift series. Squat. Hinge. Push. Pull. Four movements. Everything you need to build muscle and protect your body for life.
We're doing the pendulum squat and here's why I choose this over a barbell back squat for most people.
When you squat with a barbell on your back you're loading directly onto your spine. Over time that repeated compression can contribute to wear and tear on your discs and joints. The pendulum squat significantly reduces that. You're fixed under the machine, the resistance feels smoother throughout the movement, and you're not bearing a loaded bar on your back. For anyone training hard over the long term that's a meaningful difference.
Now here's what makes the pendulum especially effective for building muscle. The foot plate is angled. That angle lets your ankle bend more freely which allows your knee to bend deeper. The deeper you can go the more of your quad muscle you're actually using. More muscle used means more tension on the muscle. More tension means more growth. That's the whole idea.
The muscles you're training here. Your quads are the primary target — the four muscles on the front of your thigh. Your glutes will contribute to the movement no matter what, that's just how lower body mechanics work. And at deeper ranges there's some debate around whether the upper portion of your hamstring gets involved. But the focus here is the quad.
Now the cues.
Feet about shoulder width apart. I want two points of contact through your foot — your heel on the inside and the base of your big toe. Think of those two points as your foundation and keep them pressed into the plate the entire time.
Keep your arms below your body rather than reaching up to grab the handles above. This keeps your back in a neutral position and stops you from arching excessively through your lower back which is a really common pattern you see with squats.
On the way down take two to four seconds. Don't just drop into it. That slow controlled descent is where a lot of the muscle building actually happens. As you lower yourself think about pressing your weight from your heel toward your big toe — almost like the natural motion of taking a step.
For depth I go to around 100 to 120 degrees of knee bend and I stop there intentionally. You may have heard of the butt wink — that's when your hips tuck under at the bottom and your lower back rounds. There is nothing wrong with going that deep and the butt wink itself is not dangerous. But when it happens I start losing tension through my quads and the quad is what I'm here to train. So I stop just before that point and keep the tension where I want it.
Drive back up with control. Don't rush it. Keep that tension through your quads the whole way up.
That's how you squat to build muscle and prevent injury for life."

End card:
"I'm the Muscle SPT. Like and follow for more."You said: Again, I don't know, but I think the script might be slightly too long.Again, I don't know, but I think the script might be slightly too long. So take anything out that seems redundant, anything that is maybe too diluted or overcomplicated to the point where it's not really adding any benefit. Put yourself in the view of a in the shoes of a viewer who wants to learn. How to squat, why you should squat, you know, the muscles being trained, and then cues. Like, that should be the mainstay of these videos and then adding the visual component in me actually squatting. And then to and maybe, like, even intensity. Like, I'm training hard, and I'm gonna go close to fail at muscle failure. That's what you need that's what you need when you're training.Mar 20Claude responded: Here's the tightened script:Here's the tightened script:

Hook:
"How to squat to build muscle and prevent injury."

Spoken:
"First video in my compound lift series. Squat. Hinge. Push. Pull. Four movements. Everything you need.
We're doing the pendulum squat. Here's why.
A barbell back squat loads directly onto your spine. Over time that repeated compression adds up. The pendulum reduces that significantly. The resistance feels smoother throughout the movement and you're not bearing a loaded bar on your back. For anyone training hard over the long term that matters.
The angled foot plate lets your ankle bend more freely which allows your knee to bend deeper. The deeper you go the more quad muscle you're using. More muscle working means more tension. More tension means more growth.
Primary muscle here is the quad — the front of your thigh. Your glutes will contribute no matter what. That's just how lower body mechanics work.
Now the cues.
Feet shoulder width apart. Two points of contact — your inner heel and the base of your big toe. Keep those pressed into the plate the whole time.
Arms below your body, not grabbing above. Keeps your back neutral and stops you from over-arching through your lower back.
Two to four second descent. Slow and controlled. As you lower think about pressing from your heel toward your big toe.
I go to about 100 to 120 degrees of knee bend and stop just before my hips tuck under. That's where I start losing tension on the quad and the quad is what I'm here to train.
Then I drive back up. Controlled. Under tension the whole way.
And one more thing — intensity matters. I'm training close to failure on every set. That's what actually drives muscle growth. Controlled movement, full range, high effort. Every rep."

End card:
"I'm the Muscle SPT. Like and follow for more."

Tighter, cleaner, flows naturally. Runs about 75 to 90 seconds. Rationale, muscles, cues, intensity — all there, nothing wasted.You said: Deeper.Deeper. That's not necessarily true. You wanna go into deeper to active range of motion that you have access to. And not more attention means more growth. It's more active, high mechanical tension means more growth. Right?Mar 20Claude responded: Good catches — here are the corrected lines:Good catches — here are the corrected lines:
"Deeper" — swap this:
"The deeper you go the more quad muscle you're using. More muscle working means more tension. More tension means more growth."
For this:
"The deeper you can go within your available range of motion the more quad muscle you're actively using. More active muscle under high mechanical tension is what drives growth."

Both corrections matter for your credibility. "Deeper is better" is an oversimplification that could get you called out. "Within your available range of motion" is accurate and actually teaches people something — everyone's range is different and that's okay. And "high mechanical tension" is the correct mechanism, not just "more tension" which is vague and could mean anything.

Hook:
"How to squat to build muscle and prevent injury."

Spoken:
"First video in my compound lift series. Squat. Hinge. Push. Pull. Four movements. Everything you need to build muscle and protect your body for life.
We're doing the pendulum squat and here's why I choose this over a barbell back squat.
Loading a bar on your back puts direct compression through your spine. Over time that adds up. The pendulum reduces that significantly — the resistance is smoother throughout the movement and you're not bearing that load on your back. For anyone training hard over the long term that's worth paying attention to.
The angled foot plate allows your ankle to move more freely which drives your knee deeper into your active range of motion. The more active range of motion you can access the more quad muscle you're loading under high mechanical tension — and that's what actually drives growth. Your glutes are going to contribute as well because any squat pattern involves hip extension. But make no mistake — this is a quad dominant movement.
Now the cues.
Feet shoulder width apart. Two points of contact — your inner heel and the base of your big toe. Keep both pressed into the plate the entire time.
Arms below your body, not reaching up to grab above. This keeps your spine neutral and prevents that excessive lower back arch you commonly see in squat patterns.
Two to four second descent. As you lower yourself think about shifting your weight from your heel through to your big toe — replicating the gait cycle, the same way your foot loads when you walk.
I stop at about 100 to 120 degrees of knee bend — just before my hips tuck under. When that happens I lose tension on the quad so I stop before it, own that bottom position, and drive back up with control.
Intensity matters here. I'm training close to failure on every set because that's what actually stimulates growth. If you're leaving five or six reps in the tank every set you're leaving gains on the table.
That's how you squat to build muscle and prevent injury for life. I'll see you in the next one."

End card:
"I'm the Muscle SPT. Like and follow for more."You said: Make no mistake.Make no mistake. When the when this when this is performed correctly, this is a quad dominant movement. Quad dominant exercise.Mar 20Claude responded: Here's the updated line — swap this in:Here's the updated line — swap this in:

As a proxy for fall risk or longevity. Is there data on that?Mar 20Claude responded: Yes — there's solid data on this.Yes — there's solid data on this. Here's what's actually supported:

Sit-to-stand and fall risk:
The ability to get up from the floor without using your hands is one of the strongest predictors of all-cause mortality in middle-aged and older adults. A study published in the European Journal of Preventive Cardiology found that people who scored low on a sitting-rising test had a mortality risk up to 5 times higher than those who scored well. It went viral a few years ago for good reason — it's a compelling finding.
Leg strength and longevity:
Quadricep strength specifically is one of the most studied predictors of functional independence in older adults. Weaker quads correlate strongly with higher fall risk, reduced mobility, and loss of independence. Multiple large scale studies support this.
Squat performance as a proxy:
The squat pattern specifically — the ability to perform a deep bodyweight squat — is used clinically as a functional movement screen. Limitations in squat depth are associated with reduced ankle mobility, hip mobility, and overall functional capacity in aging populations.
Falls context:
Falls are the leading cause of injury-related death in adults over 65 in the United States. Leg strength, balance, and the ability to perform squat-pattern movements are directly tied to fall prevention in the literature.

How to use this in your content:
That sit-to-stand test is actually incredible content. You could film yourself doing it, explain the research behind it, and tie it directly back to why you train the squat pattern the way you do. That's a video that will get shared by people tagging their parents. Want me to write that script?You said: Yeah.Yeah. Let's let's write a script for sit to stand. Let's do the 5 times sit to stand and the 32nd to the stand. And then also add into my squat script, something about Squat patterns are inherently the most functional movement pattern you can do, and that doesn't necessarily mean it has to be under a barbell. But what it does mean is it's a strong indicator of large extremity strength. So add something to like that to the script to make it flow and it could you can replace something that's not as pertinent. And then, yes, write me a script for the 32nd 5 times to the stand, and that'll be, like, an education bit, I think.Mar 20Claude responded: Here's the updated squat script first with the functional movement addition, then the sit to stand script after:Here's the updated squat script first with the functional movement addition, then the sit to stand script after:

PENDULUM SQUAT — FULL SCRIPT:
Hook:
"How to squat to build muscle and prevent injury."

Spoken:
"First video in my compound lift series. Squat. Hinge. Push. Pull. Four movements. Everything you need to build muscle and protect your body for life.
The squat pattern is one of the most functional movements you can do. And that doesn't mean it has to be under a barbell. What it means is that your ability to squat is a direct indicator of lower body strength — and lower body strength is one of the strongest predictors of how well you move, how long you stay independent, and how well you age. We're going to talk more about that in a separate video. But that's why this movement matters.
We're doing the pendulum squat and here's why I choose this over a barbell back squat.
Loading a bar on your back puts direct compression through your spine. Over time that adds up. The pendulum reduces that significantly — the resistance is smoother throughout the movement and you're not bearing that load on your back. For anyone training hard over the long term that's worth paying attention to.
This is a more quad focused squat variation. The way the machine positions your torso relative to your hips and knees shifts the demand onto the quad. The angled foot plate allows your ankle to move more freely which drives your knee deeper into your active range of motion. The more active range of motion you can access the more quad muscle you're loading under high mechanical tension — and that's what actually drives growth. Your glutes are going to contribute as well because any squat pattern involves hip extension — but the primary target here is the quad.
Now the cues.
Feet shoulder width apart. Two points of contact — your inner heel and the base of your big toe. Keep both pressed into the plate the entire time.
Arms below your body, not reaching up to grab above. This keeps your spine neutral and prevents that excessive lower back arch you commonly see in squat patterns.
Two to four second eccentric — that's the lowering phase, where you lower the weight down. As you descend think about shifting your weight from your heel through to your big toe — replicating the gait cycle, the same way your foot loads when you walk.
I stop at about 100 to 120 degrees of knee bend — just before my hips tuck under. When that happens I lose tension on the quad so I stop before it, own that bottom position, and drive back up with control.
Intensity matters. I'm training close to failure on every set because that's what actually stimulates growth. If you're leaving five or six reps in the tank every set you're leaving gains on the table.
That's how you squat to build muscle and prevent injury for life. I'm the Muscle SPT. I'll see you in the next one."


SIT TO STAND — EDUCATION SCRIPT:
Hook:
"This simple test predicts how long you'll live."

Spoken:
"There are two tests I want you to know about. They take under a minute. And the research behind them is genuinely eye opening.
The first is the five times sit to stand test. You sit in a chair, arms crossed over your chest, and you stand up and sit back down five times as fast as you can. That's it. Research shows that how long it takes you to complete those five repetitions is directly correlated with fall risk, functional decline, and mortality in older adults. If it takes you more than 12 seconds you are considered at an elevated risk for falls. If it takes more than 15 seconds that risk increases significantly.
The second is the 30 second sit to stand test. Same setup — chair, arms crossed, no pushing off with your hands. This time you count how many times you can stand up and sit back down in 30 seconds. This one is used clinically to measure lower body strength and endurance in older adults. The lower your number the higher your risk of falls and loss of independence.
Now here's why this matters. Falls are the leading cause of injury related death in adults over 65 in the United States. And the ability to perform a squat pattern — standing up from a chair is literally a squat — is one of the most direct indicators of whether your legs are strong enough to keep you safe as you age.
This is not just about old people. The muscle you build right now in your 20s, 30s, and 40s is what determines how you perform on these tests at 60, 70, and beyond. Sarcopenia — the muscle loss that begins at 30 — is exactly what erodes that capacity over time.
So here's what I want you to do. Try both tests right now. Time yourself on the five rep version. Count your reps on the 30 second version. Write the numbers down. Then come back in 90 days after training your squat pattern consistently and test yourself again.
That number is going to change. And that change is going to matter for the rest of your life."

End card:
"I'm the Muscle SPT. Like and follow for more."You said: We’re going to talk more about that in a separate video.We’re going to talk more about that in a separate video. But that’s why this movement matters.take this outMar 20Claude responded: ---
Hook:
"How to squat to build muscle and prevent injury."

Spoken:
"First video in my compound lift series. Squat. Hinge. Push. Pull. Four movements. Everything you need to build muscle and protect your body for life.
The squat pattern is one of the most functional movements you can do. And that doesn't mean it has to be under a barbell. What it means is that your ability to squat is a direct indicator of lower body strength — and lower body strength is one of the strongest predictors of how well you move, how long you stay independent, and how well you age.
We're doing the pendulum squat and here's why I choose this over a barbell back squat.
Loading a bar on your back puts direct compression through your spine. Over time that adds up. The pendulum reduces that significantly — the resistance is smoother throughout the movement and you're not bearing that load on your back. For anyone training hard over the long term that's worth paying attention to.
This is a more quad focused squat variation. The way the machine positions your torso relative to your hips and knees shifts the demand onto the quad. The angled foot plate allows your ankle to move more freely which drives your knee deeper into your active range of motion. The more active range of motion you can access the more quad muscle you're loading under high mechanical tension — and that's what actually drives growth. Your glutes are going to contribute as well because any squat pattern involves hip extension — but the primary target here is the quad.
Now the cues.
Feet shoulder width apart. Two points of contact — your inner heel and the base of your big toe. Keep both pressed into the plate the entire time.
Arms below your body, not reaching up to grab above. This keeps your spine neutral and prevents that excessive lower back arch you commonly see in squat patterns.
Two to four second eccentric — that's the lowering phase, where you lower the weight down. As you descend think about shifting your weight from your heel through to your big toe — replicating the gait cycle, the same way your foot loads when you walk.
I stop at about 100 to 120 degrees of knee bend — just before my hips tuck under. When that happens I lose tension on the quad so I stop before it, own that bottom position, and drive back up with control.
Intensity matters. I'm training close to failure on every set because that's what actually stimulates growth. If you're leaving five or six reps in the tank every set you're leaving gains on the table.
That's how you squat to build muscle and prevent injury for life. I'm the Muscle SPT. I'll see you in the next one."You said: SIT TO STAND — EDUCATION SCRIPT:SIT TO STAND — EDUCATION SCRIPT:Hook:

That's a stronger hook. More specific, more actionable, and "at home" lowers the barrier immediately — anyone watching can do it right now. Here's the full updated script:

Hook:
"This simple test you can do at home predicts how long you'll live."

Spoken:
"There are two tests I want you to know about. They take under a minute. And the research behind them is genuinely eye opening.
The first is the five times sit to stand test. You sit in a chair, arms crossed over your chest, and you stand up and sit back down five times as fast as you can. That's it. Research shows that how long it takes you to complete those five repetitions is directly correlated with fall risk, functional decline, and mortality in older adults. If it takes you more than 12 seconds you are considered at an elevated risk for falls. If it takes more than 15 seconds that risk increases significantly.
The second is the 30 second sit to stand test. Same setup — chair, arms crossed, no pushing off with your hands. This time you count how many times you can stand up and sit back down in 30 seconds. This one is used clinically to measure lower body strength and endurance in older adults. The lower your number the higher your risk of falls and loss of independence.
Now here's why this matters. Falls are the leading cause of injury related death in adults over 65 in the United States. And the ability to perform a squat pattern — standing up from a chair is literally a squat — is one of the most direct indicators of whether your legs are strong enough to keep you safe as you age.
This is not just about old people. The muscle you build right now in your 20s, 30s, and 40s is what determines how you perform on these tests at 60, 70, and beyond. Sarcopenia — the muscle loss that begins at 30 — is exactly what erodes that capacity over time.
So here's what I want you to do. Try both tests right now. Time yourself on the five rep version. Count your reps on the 30 second version. Write the numbers down. Then come back in 90 days after training your squat pattern consistently and test yourself again.
That number is going to change. And that change is going to matter for the rest of your life."

End card:
"I'm the Muscle SPT. Like and follow for more."You said: Also, mention the cutoff values for the 5 times to the stand and the 30 seconds to the stand.Also, mention the cutoff values for the 5 times to the stand and the 30 seconds to the stand. I think you did the 5 times, but also the cutoff for the 30 second.Mar 20Claude responded: Here are the cutoff values for both tests — verify these before posting since you want to be clinically accurate:Here are the cutoff values for both tests — verify these before posting since you want to be clinically accurate:
5 Times Sit to Stand:

Under 12 seconds — normal, low fall risk
12 to 15 seconds — moderate risk, warrants attention
Over 15 seconds — elevated fall risk

30 Second Sit to Stand:
These are age and gender based norms. General benchmarks:

Ages 60–64: Men 14+ reps, Women 12+ reps considered normal
Ages 65–69: Men 12+ reps, Women 11+ reps
Ages 70–74: Men 12+ reps, Women 10+ reps
Ages 75–79: Men 11+ reps, Women 10+ reps
Ages 80+: Men 10+ reps, Women 9+ reps

Below these thresholds indicates below average lower body strength for that age group and elevated fall risk.

Want me to drop these into the full script cleanly? And I'd recommend adding a quick line that these are established clinical benchmarks used by physical therapists — it reinforces your authority and gives the numbers credibility without you having to cite a paper on camera.You said: Yes.Yes. In the script, Jesus Christ. It doesn't have to be all the values, but okay. Never mind. Just yeah, just give me I just want the script. Okay? It doesn't have to be super ultra specific like that.Mar 20Claude responded: ---
Hook:
"This simple test you can do at home predicts how long you'll live."

Spoken:
"There are two tests I want you to know about. They take under a minute. And the research behind them is genuinely eye opening.
The first is the five times sit to stand test. You sit in a chair, arms crossed over your chest, and you stand up and sit back down five times as fast as you can. Physical therapists use this clinically to assess fall risk and functional decline. If it takes you more than 12 seconds you're considered at an elevated risk for falls. Over 15 seconds that risk increases significantly.
The second is the 30 second sit to stand test. Same setup — chair, arms crossed, no pushing off with your hands. This time you count how many times you can stand up and sit back down in 30 seconds. The benchmark varies by age but generally speaking the fewer reps you can complete the greater your risk of falls and loss of independence down the line.
Now here's why this matters. Falls are the leading cause of injury related death in adults over 65 in the United States. And standing up from a chair is literally a squat. Your ability to do it repeatedly and efficiently is one of the most direct indicators of whether your legs are strong enough to keep you safe as you age.
This is not just about old people. The muscle you build right now in your 20s, 30s, and 40s is what determines how you perform on these tests at 60, 70, and beyond. Sarcopenia — the muscle loss that begins at 30 — is exactly what erodes that capacity over time.
So here's what I want you to do. Try both tests right now. Time yourself on the five rep version. Count your reps on the 30 second version. Write the numbers down. Then come back in 90 days after training your squat pattern consistently and test yourself again.
That number is going to change. And that change is going to matter for the rest of your life."

End card:
"I'm the Muscle SPT. Hook:
"This simple test you can do at home predicts how long you'll live."

Spoken:
"There are two tests I want you to know about. They take under a minute. And the research behind them is genuinely eye opening.
The first is the five times sit to stand test. You sit in a chair, arms crossed over your chest, and you stand up and sit back down five times as fast as you can. Physical therapists use this clinically to assess fall risk and functional decline. If it takes you more than 12 seconds you're considered at an elevated risk for falls. Over 15 seconds that risk increases significantly.
The second is the 30 second sit to stand test. Same setup — chair, arms crossed, no pushing off with your hands. This time you count how many times you can stand up and sit back down in 30 seconds. The benchmark varies by age but generally speaking the fewer reps you can complete the greater your risk of falls and loss of independence down the line.
Now here's why this matters. Falls are the leading cause of injury related death in adults over 65 in the United States. And standing up from a chair is literally a squat. Your ability to do it repeatedly and efficiently is one of the most direct indicators of whether your legs are strong enough to keep you safe as you age.
The muscle you build in your 20s, 30s, and 40s is what determines how you perform on these tests at 60 and 70. That's the prevention side of this. But if you're already in your 50s, 60s, or 70s — it is absolutely not too late. You can get better at squat patterns at any point in life. The research is clear that resistance training improves lower body strength and functional performance at every age. It's never too late to start building the strength that protects you.
So here's what I want you to do. Try both tests right now. Time yourself on the five rep version. Count your reps on the 30 second version. Write the numbers down. Then come back in 90 days after training your squat pattern consistently and test yourself again.
That number is going to change. And that change is going to matter for the rest of your life."

End card:
"I'm the Muscle SPT. Like and follow for more."You said: Okay.Okay. Now give me a script on how resistance training is under a dose. In older adults and, you know, for multiple reasons. Like, because we wanna we think they're, like, frail. We wanna we, you know, we worry about their safety Like, we don't think they can go we don't think we can push them, blah blah blah. Like, mention all the things. From a physical therapy context, but why we need to actually push them to some extent adaptation. Neuroplasticity, blah blah blah, intensities matters, blah blah blah.Mar 20Claude responded: ---
Hook:
"We are massively under dosing resistance training in older adults. And it's hurting them."

Spoken:
"This is something I think about a lot as a PT student. And honestly it's one of the bigger problems in how we approach exercise prescription for older adults.
The common assumption is that older adults are fragile. That we need to be conservative. That we should keep the weights light, the intensity low, and prioritize safety above everything else. And I understand where that comes from. But the research tells a very different story.
Here's the problem with under dosing. When you don't apply enough stimulus to the body — enough mechanical tension, enough intensity — you don't get adaptation. And adaptation is the entire point. Without sufficient stimulus the muscle doesn't grow. The nervous system doesn't adapt. Strength doesn't improve. And the person walks out of that session no better equipped to live independently than when they walked in.
There are a few reasons we under dose older adults and none of them are actually supported by the evidence.
The first is the fear of injury. We assume higher intensity means higher risk. But the research consistently shows that progressive resistance training at moderate to high intensities is safe and well tolerated in older adults — including those in their 70s and 80s. The injury risk from training is far lower than the injury risk from being weak and falling.
The second is the assumption that they can't handle it. That their bodies won't respond the same way. But older adults are absolutely capable of building muscle and gaining strength. The mechanism is the same. You need progressive overload. You need sufficient intensity. You need to get close enough to failure that the body has a reason to adapt.
The third is neuroplasticity. This is something people don't talk about enough. Resistance training doesn't just build muscle — it drives neurological adaptation. The nervous system gets better at recruiting muscle fibers, coordinating movement, and producing force. That has profound implications for fall prevention, balance, and functional independence. But you only get that adaptation if the intensity is high enough to challenge the system.
The bottom line is this. Older adults don't need less intensity. They need appropriate intensity — progressively applied, carefully monitored, and consistently delivered. The goal is adaptation. And adaptation requires a stimulus worth adapting to.
If you're working with older adults or you are an older adult — don't be afraid of the weight. Be afraid of what happens when you don't lift it."

End card:
"I'm the Muscle SPT. Like and follow for more."You said: Okay.Okay. These are the education scripts. They should be under a minute. Okay? Under a minute. This is not the fucking exercise demo.Mar 20Claude responded: You're right.You're right. Way too long. Here's the rule going forward:

"We are massively under dosing resistance training in older adults."

Spoken:
"The common assumption is that older adults are fragile. Keep the weights light. Keep the intensity low. Prioritize safety. I understand where that comes from — but the research doesn't support it.
When you don't apply enough stimulus you don't get adaptation. No adaptation means no muscle growth, no strength gains, and no improvement in the things that actually keep older adults independent and out of danger.
We under dose for a few reasons. Fear of injury. Assumption that they can't handle it. But progressive resistance training at moderate to high intensities is safe and well tolerated even into your 70s and 80s. The injury risk from training is far lower than the injury risk from being weak and falling.
And here's what people don't talk about enough — neuroplasticity. Resistance training drives neurological adaptation. Your nervous system gets better at recruiting muscle, coordinating movement, and producing force. But only if the intensity is high enough to challenge it.
Older adults don't need less intensity. They need appropriate intensity. Because adaptation requires a stimulus worth adapting to."

Like and follow for more."You said: Now do another script, education script, 4 think of muscle like a like a carved Think of think of carbohydrate like a muscle sponge or zinc and talk about insu…Now do another script, education script, 4 think of muscle like a like a carved Think of think of carbohydrate like a muscle sponge or zinc and talk about insulin sensitivity, talk about the more muscle you have, the more carbs you can eat, and it not over not overflow leading to insulin resistance. Because more muscle means you're more insulin sensitive, blah blah blah. Go down the rabbit hole of that post and, of course, tie it all into this whole why you've been muscle, blah blah blah, sarcopenia, blah blah blah blah.Mar 20Claude responded: ---
Hook:
"The more muscle you have the more carbs you can eat."

Spoken:
"Think of muscle like a sponge for carbohydrates. When you eat carbs your body breaks them down into glucose. That glucose needs somewhere to go. And muscle tissue is the primary storage site.
The more muscle you have the more storage capacity you have. Which means your body can handle a greater amount of carbohydrate without that glucose overflowing into the bloodstream and driving up insulin levels.
This is called insulin sensitivity. And muscle mass is one of the biggest drivers of it. More muscle means your body is better at shuttling glucose where it needs to go efficiently and effectively. Less muscle means that system becomes less efficient. Glucose builds up. Insulin spikes. Over time that can contribute to insulin resistance and type 2 diabetes.
Now connect this back to sarcopenia. As you lose muscle after 30 you're not just losing strength. You're losing metabolic capacity. Your body becomes less efficient at managing blood sugar. Less tolerant of carbohydrates. More prone to the downstream effects of poor glucose regulation.
This is why building and maintaining muscle isn't just about how you look or how strong you are. It's about how your entire metabolic system functions. Muscle is arguably your most important metabolic organ.
Build it early. Protect it always."

End card:
"I'm the Muscle SPT. Like and follow for more."You said: Let's do a couple of hot take reels.Let's do a couple of hot take reels. So let's do building muscle is the most functional thing you can do. And then another hot take real, peptides aren't the answer. Something like that.Mar 20Claude responded: ---
HOT TAKE 1:
Hook:
"Building muscle is the most functional thing you can do."

Spoken:
"Forget functional training classes. Forget the bosu ball. Forget the resistance band workouts your physio gave you. The most functional thing you can do for your body is build muscle.
Functional means your body can do what life demands of it. Carry groceries. Get off the floor. Climb stairs. Lift your kids. Survive a fall. All of those things require one thing — strength. And strength comes from muscle.
The fitness industry sold you functional training as some special category of exercise. But a heavy squat is functional. A loaded carry is functional. A Romanian deadlift is functional. Any movement that makes you stronger is functional.
The people who age the best aren't the ones who did the most mobility work or the most cardio. They're the ones who built and maintained the most muscle. That's what the research shows. That's what I see in clinic. And that's what I'm building my entire practice around.
Stop chasing functional. Start building muscle. That's the most functional decision you'll ever make."

End card:
"I'm the Muscle SPT. Like and follow for more."


HOT TAKE 2:
Hook:
"Peptides aren't the answer."

Spoken:
"BPC-157. TB-500. Every other peptide being sold to you as the solution to your recovery, your muscle building, your longevity. I get it. The idea that you can inject your way to better health is appealing. But let's be real about what's actually happening here.
Most people reaching for peptides haven't optimized the basics. They're not sleeping enough. Their protein intake is too low. Their training intensity isn't high enough. Their stress is through the roof. And instead of fixing those things they're adding a compound with limited human research, no FDA approval, and a price tag that could fund six months of a solid training program.
I'm not saying peptides have zero merit. The research on some of them is genuinely interesting. But interesting research and something you should be injecting into your body are two very different things.
You know what has decades of research behind it? Resistance training. Progressive overload. Adequate protein. Quality sleep. Those four things will do more for your muscle, your recovery, and your longevity than any peptide on the market.
Fix the foundation before you start optimizing the edges."

End card:
"I'm the Muscle SPT. Like and follow for more."You said: Give me the color pallets againGive me the color pallets againMar 20Claude responded: Primary:Primary:

Dark background, white text, yellow-green for the one word or stat you want them to remember on each slide.You said: For carousels, also that key word yellow is way too bright can’t read off the whiteFor carousels, also that key word yellow is way too bright can’t read off the whiteMar 20Claude responded: You're right — yellow on white is unreadable.You're right — yellow on white is unreadable. Two options:
Option 1 — Use yellow only on the dark background:
Keep #E8FF47 strictly for text on the dark #070707 background. Never yellow on white. If you need a light slide use white text on dark, not the other way around.
Option 2 — Swap yellow for a deeper accent:
If you want an accent that works on both light and dark backgrounds try:
#B8A800 — deeper golden yellow, readable on white and still on brand
Or go a completely different direction for the accent:
#FF8B64 — your warm orange. Readable on dark backgrounds, works as a highlight color, still within your palette.

Honestly the simplest fix is Option 1. Keep all your carousels dark background only. White text. Yellow-green accent. Never a light slide. Stays on brand, no readability issues, and looks sharp and consistent every time.You said: Or background I the black?Or background I the black?Mar 20Claude responded: Yes.Yes. #070707 for every slide. No light slides at all.
Dark background, white text, #E8FF47 for key words. That's your carousel format. Consistent, on brand, readable, and it looks premium. Stick to that every single time.You said: Oh that’s clean I like thatOh that’s clean I like thatMar 20Claude responded: It works really well for your brand specifically.It works really well for your brand specifically. Dark and clinical. Matches the yellow-green background light in your videos too so everything feels cohesive — your Reels and your carousels look like they belong to the same brand. That's what you want.You said: What about @themusclespt at the bottom yellow or whiteWhat about @themusclespt at the bottom yellow or whiteMar 20Claude responded: White.White.
Small, subtle, bottom corner. You don't want the handle competing with your accent color. Yellow is reserved for the key word on each slide — if your handle is also yellow it dilutes the hierarchy. White keeps it present but secondary. People see it without it pulling focus from the content.You said: Font sizes for title slide ie 5 signs you’re losing muscle and don’t know it.Font sizes for title slide ie 5 signs you’re losing muscle and don’t know it. Font size for muscles in broom and font size for each following slides. Also good fonts to use on brandMar 20Claude responded: Font sizes:Font sizes:
Title slide (hook slide) — 60 to 70pt. Big, bold, takes up most of the slide. This is your scroll stopper.
Sub-text on title slide if you have any — 24 to 28pt
Body slides (each point) — 36 to 44pt. Still large and readable. One idea per slide means you have room to go big.
Handle at the bottom — 16 to 18pt. Small and consistent every slide.

Fonts that fit your brand:
Display / Headers:

Bebas Neue — this is already in your brand system, bold, condensed, clinical and strong. Use this for your title slide and key words.
Montserrat Bold — clean, modern, slightly softer than Bebas. Good alternative.
Barlow Condensed Bold — similar energy to Bebas, slightly more refined.

Body / Supporting text:

DM Sans — already in your brand system, clean and readable at smaller sizes
Inter — neutral, highly readable, works well on dark backgrounds
Outfit — modern and clean, slightly more personality than Inter


My recommendation:
Bebas Neue for your title slide and any large headline text. DM Sans for your body slides. That's exactly the font pairing already in your brand identity. Keeps everything consistent across your videos, carousels, and documents.You said: How do I draw an arrow to right on title slide?How do I draw an arrow to right on title slide? Also the font I have right now is Radley. It’s kinda niceMar 20Claude responded: Arrow on Canva:Arrow on Canva:
Go to Elements → search "arrow" → filter by Lines & Shapes. Pick a simple clean arrow. Make it white or #E8FF47 depending on what you want it to point to. Resize and rotate it to point right.
Or even simpler — just type → as text. Clean, minimal, no element needed.

On Radley:
Radley is actually a solid choice for your brand. It's a serif font which gives it a clinical, authoritative feel — think medical journals, academic papers. That contrast between a serious serif font and your dark aggressive background works really well. It's unexpected and that's what makes it memorable.
If it feels right keep it. You don't need to match what I suggested exactly. The best font is the one that looks good to your eye and feels consistent to your brand. If Radley does that stick with it.
You could use Radley for your title slides and DM Sans or Inter for your body slides. Serif for the big statement, sans-serif for the readable body text. That's a classic editorial pairing and it would look really sharp on a dark background.You said: So title is 5 signs you’re losing muscle and don’t know itSo title is 5 signs you’re losing muscle and don’t know itMar 20Claude responded: For that specific title the font size depends on how you want it to sit on the slide.For that specific title the font size depends on how you want it to sit on the slide. A few options:
Option 1 — All on one or two lines, big and dominant:
Break it into two lines:
"5 Signs You're
Losing Muscle
And Don't Know It"
Around 60 to 65pt. Takes up most of the slide. Bold and aggressive.
Option 2 — Slightly smaller with more breathing room:
Same three line break at around 50 to 55pt. More negative space around it. Cleaner and more premium feeling.
Option 3 — Lead with the number:
Make "5 Signs" significantly larger — around 80 to 90pt in #E8FF47
Then "You're Losing Muscle And Don't Know It" below in white at around 36 to 40pt.
The number stops the scroll, the rest of the title explains it.

Option 3 is my pick for this specific title. The number 5 is the hook — make it the biggest thing on the slide. Yellow so it pops immediately. Then the explanation in white below. That hierarchy tells the eye exactly where to look first and what to read second.
Try all three and see which one feels right when you look at it. Your gut will tell you.You said: Font for thatFont for thatMar 20Claude responded: Radley for everything since that's what you're working with.Radley for everything since that's what you're working with.
"5 Signs" — Radley Bold, 80 to 90pt, #E8FF47
"You're Losing Muscle And Don't Know It" — Radley, 36 to 40pt, white
If Radley doesn't have a bold weight use the regular weight but increase the size slightly to compensate. Radley is already a strong enough serif that it doesn't need bold to command attention.You said: Give me other font optionsGive me other font optionsMar 20Claude responded: For your title/display text:For your title/display text:

Playfair Display — elegant serif, strong and authoritative, looks premium on dark backgrounds
Cormorant Garamond — refined and clinical, slightly more editorial than Radley
Libre Baskerville — clean classic serif, highly readable at large sizes
DM Serif Display — modern serif, pairs perfectly with DM Sans for body text
Freight Display — sharp and sophisticated, strong on dark backgrounds


For body slides:

DM Sans — already in your brand system, clean and minimal
Inter — extremely readable, neutral, works on everything
Outfit — modern with slight personality
Poppins — friendly and clean, slightly rounder
Karla — similar to DM Sans, slightly more condensed


Best pairings for your brand:

Playfair Display + DM Sans — editorial and clean
DM Serif Display + DM Sans — cohesive, modern, clinical
Cormorant Garamond + Inter — refined and authoritative
Libre Baskerville + Outfit — classic meets modern


My pick for your brand specifically is Playfair Display for titles and DM Sans for body. The white supporting text looks like it might still be Radley too. Switch that to DM Sans Medium or Bold. Right now both lines are in the same font which flattens the hierarchy. The contrast between Radley serif for the header and DM Sans sans-serif for the support text is what gives it that editorial premium look.
So:
"You're Getting Weaker." — Radley, yellow
"The weights that used to feel easy / feel heavier than they should." — DM Sans Medium or Bold, white

The serif vs sans-serif contrast is too jarring at those sizes side by side.
Two options:
Option 1 — Use Radley for both
Keep Radley throughout. Yellow header bold, white supporting text regular weight. Same font family, different weight and color creates the hierarchy without the clash.
Option 2 — Use DM Sans for both
Drop Radley entirely for body slides. DM Sans Bold for the yellow header, DM Sans Regular or Medium for the white text below. Clean, modern, cohesive.
My pick is Option 1 — Radley for everything on body slides. The serif has a clinical editorial quality that fits your brand and when it's the same font family at different weights it'll feel intentional rather than conflicting. Save DM Sans strictly for your handle at the bottom.You said: Is there another font different then dm sans tha works with the radleyIs there another font different then dm sans tha works with the radleyMar 20Claude responded: Yes.Yes. These pair well with Radley:
Best options:
Lato — clean, slightly warm, works beautifully with serif headers. Very readable at body sizes on dark backgrounds.
Nunito — rounded and approachable, softens the contrast with Radley without clashing. Good for the supporting text tone you want.
Raleway — elegant and thin, complements Radley's editorial feel. Use Medium or SemiBold weight so it doesn't disappear on dark background.
Josefin Sans — geometric and clean, creates a nice modern contrast with Radley's classical feel without being jarring.
Mulish — similar to Lato, slightly more neutral. Very clean on dark backgrounds.

My pick for your brand is Lato Bold or SemiBold for the white supporting text. It has enough weight to read well on dark backgrounds, feels warmer than DM Sans, and sits comfortably next to Radley without competing with it.
Try Lato first. If it doesn't feel right try Raleway SemiBold second.You said: Radley or title slide fontRadley or title slide fontMar 20Claude responded: Radley for everything.Radley for everything. Title slide and body slides.

Title slide: Radley, large, yellow for the number, white for the rest

Fill in the blanks each time:

EDUCATIONAL REELS
Line 1: State the problem or insight in one sentence. (What most people don't know)
Line 2-3: Expand with one key fact or stat that backs it up.
Line 4: The takeaway — what they should do with this information.
.
.
.

PERSONAL / STORY REELS
Line 1: The honest opener. One sentence that sets the scene.
Line 2-3: The context — what was happening, what you were feeling.
Line 4: The lesson or shift. What changed and why it matters to them.
.
.
.

.
.
.

.
.
.

#exercisedemo #strengthtraining #physicaltherapy #musclebuilding #injuryprevention #themusclespt

The rules that apply to every caption:

Keep it under 5 lines before the dots — people don't read long captions on mobile
The dots create the "more" break so the CTA is always visible without expanding
Same hashtag block every time for that content type — save them in your notes app and paste
Always end with Follow @themusclespt for more — every single post, no exceptions
Never use more than 10 to 15 hashtags — Instagram has confirmed more doesn't help and can hurt reach
You said: Okay.Okay. Give me one for the first reel we made about, sarcopenia.The the muscle loss, say, after age 30, that one. Me a caption for that.Mar 20Claude responded: ---
Most people don't find out they're losing muscle until it's already been happening for a decade.
After 30 you lose 3 to 8 percent of your muscle every ten years. By 80 that number can reach up to 50 percent in inactive adults. And it's the number one predictor of fall risk and loss of independence as you age.
The window to prevent it is open right now. Most people sleep through it.
.
.
.
Follow @themusclespt for more.
#muscleloss #sarcopenia #physicaltherapy #strengthtraining #musclebuilding #longevity #fitnesseducation #themusclesptYou said: Okay.Okay. Now give me caption for every reel we've put together and then every post we put together, every carousel, the educational one, the everything we I've you've told me, like, that I've made this far.Mar 20Claude responded: Here are captions for everything we've built:Here are captions for everything we've built:

REEL 1 — Sarcopenia Intro
Most people don't find out they're losing muscle until it's already been happening for a decade.
After 30 you lose 3 to 8 percent of your muscle every ten years. By 80 that number can reach up to 50 percent in inactive adults. And it's the number one predictor of fall risk and loss of independence as you age.
The window to prevent it is open right now. Most people sleep through it.
.
.
.
Follow @themusclespt for more.
#muscleloss #sarcopenia #physicaltherapy #strengthtraining #musclebuilding #longevity #fitnesseducation #themusclespt

REEL 2 — The Fitness Industry Is Lying To You
The fitness industry is built on making you believe you need more. More days. More supplements. More programs.
The research says otherwise. Two resistance training sessions a week is enough to significantly slow muscle loss. The minimum effective dose is way lower than they want you to think.
Follow the science. Not the industry.
.
.
.
Follow @themusclespt for more.
#fitnessmyths #muscleloss #strengthtraining #physicaltherapy #sarcopenia #themusclespt

REEL 3 — The Two Workouts A Week That Stop Muscle Loss
You don't need to live in the gym to build muscle and prevent the decline that comes with age.
Two full body sessions a week. Compound movements. Getting stronger over time. That's the whole framework. Simple, sustainable, and backed by research.
Stop overcomplicating it.
.
.
.
Follow @themusclespt for more.
#musclebuilding #strengthtraining #sarcopenia #physicaltherapy #longevity #minimumeffectivedose #themusclespt

REEL 4 — You're Not Eating Enough Protein
You can train perfectly and still lose muscle if your protein is too low.
At least 0.7 grams of protein per pound of bodyweight per day to preserve and build muscle. Most people are getting half that. And older adults actually need more protein than younger people to get the same muscle building response. Not less. More.
Fix your protein before you fix anything else.
.
.
.
Follow @themusclespt for more.
#protein #musclebuilding #nutrition #sarcopenia #strengthtraining #physicaltherapy #themusclespt

HOT TAKE 1 — Your Doctor Never Told You This
Your doctor checks your blood pressure, your cholesterol, your weight. Nobody checks your muscle mass. Nobody mentions sarcopenia. Nobody tells you that two resistance training sessions a week and adequate protein could change the entire trajectory of how you age.
The medical system is built to treat. Not prevent. By the time they treat it it's already too late.
That's why I'm building this.
.
.
.
Follow @themusclespt for more.
#sarcopenia #physicaltherapy #muscleloss #longevity #preventivehealth #themusclespt

HOT TAKE 2 — Squatting Is Not Bad For Your Knees
Avoiding squats because your knees hurt is making your knees worse. Not better.
The muscles around your knee are what protect the joint. When those muscles are weak the knee takes all the load. Squatting builds exactly those muscles. Weakness is not the answer. Strength is.
Agree or disagree? Drop it below.
.
.
.
Follow @themusclespt for more.
#kneehealth #squats #physicaltherapy #strengthtraining #injuryprevention #themusclespt

HOT TAKE 3 — Building Muscle Is The Most Functional Thing You Can Do
Forget the bosu ball. Forget the resistance band circuit your physio gave you. The most functional thing you can do is build muscle.
Functional means your body can do what life demands of it. And all of those things require one thing — strength. The research is clear. The people who age the best are the ones who built and maintained the most muscle.
Stop chasing functional. Start building muscle.
.
.
.
Follow @themusclespt for more.
#functionaltraining #musclebuilding #strengthtraining #physicaltherapy #longevity #themusclespt

HOT TAKE 4 — Peptides Aren't The Answer
Most people reaching for peptides haven't optimized the basics. Sleep. Protein. Training intensity. Stress management.
Decades of research support resistance training, progressive overload, adequate protein, and quality sleep. Those four things will do more for your muscle, your recovery, and your longevity than any peptide on the market.
Fix the foundation before you optimize the edges.
.
.
.
Follow @themusclespt for more.
#peptides #musclebuilding #strengthtraining #physicaltherapy #longevity #fitnessmyths #themusclespt

EXERCISE DEMO — Pendulum Squat
Compound lift series. Squat edition.
The pendulum squat is one of the most quad dominant squat variations you can do. The angled foot plate drives your knee deeper into your active range of motion, the resistance is smoother throughout the movement, and you're not loading directly onto your spine the way a barbell does. For anyone training hard over the long term that distinction matters.
Cues — shoulder width stance, two contact points through the foot, arms below the torso, 2 to 4 second eccentric, stop just before the hips tuck under. Train close to failure. Every set.
Save this for your next leg day.
.
.
.
Follow @themusclespt for more.
#squats #legday #physicaltherapy #musclebuilding #strengthtraining #injuryprevention #exercisedemo #themusclespt

EDUCATION — Sit To Stand Test
This test takes under a minute and it tells you more about your long term health than most things your doctor checks.
Five times sit to stand — over 12 seconds means elevated fall risk. Over 15 seconds that risk increases significantly. Thirty second sit to stand — the fewer reps you complete the greater your risk of falls and loss of independence.
Standing up from a chair is a squat. Your ability to do it repeatedly is a direct measure of whether your legs are strong enough to keep you safe as you age. Try both tests right now and write the numbers down. Come back in 90 days.
.
.
.
Follow @themusclespt for more.
#fallprevention #physicaltherapy #muscleloss #sarcopenia #longevity #strengthtraining #themusclespt

EDUCATION — Under Dosing Resistance Training In Older Adults
We are under dosing resistance training in older adults and it is actively hurting them.
The assumption is that older adults are fragile. Keep the weights light. Keep the intensity low. But the research consistently shows that progressive resistance training at moderate to high intensities is safe and well tolerated even into your 70s and 80s. The injury risk from training is far lower than the injury risk from being weak and falling.
Older adults don't need less intensity. They need appropriate intensity. Because adaptation requires a stimulus worth adapting to.
.
.
.
Follow @themusclespt for more.
#physicaltherapy #olderadults #strengthtraining #sarcopenia #muscleloss #longevity #themusclespt

EDUCATION — Muscle As A Carbohydrate Sponge
The more muscle you have the more carbs you can eat without consequences.
Muscle tissue is the primary storage site for glucose. More muscle means more storage capacity means better insulin sensitivity means your body handles carbohydrates more efficiently. Less muscle means that system breaks down. Glucose builds up. Insulin spikes. Over time that contributes to insulin resistance and type 2 diabetes.
Muscle is not just about how you look. It is your most important metabolic organ. Build it early. Protect it always.
.
.
.
Follow @themusclespt for more.
#musclebuilding #insulinsensitivity #nutrition #sarcopenia #metabolichealth #physicaltherapy #themusclespt

CAROUSEL — 5 Signs You're Losing Muscle
Most people don't realize they're losing muscle until the signs are impossible to ignore.
Getting weaker. Losing definition. Always tired. Getting injured more. Losing grip strength. These aren't just signs of aging. They're signs of sarcopenia — the muscle loss that begins at 30 and accelerates every decade after.
Save this and share it with someone who needs to see it.
.
.
.
Follow @themusclespt for more.
#muscleloss #sarcopenia #physicaltherapy #strengthtraining #longevity #musclebuilding #themusclespt

PERSONAL 1 — The Injuries
Five injuries in four years. Broken leg. Rolled ankle. Scapular dyskinesia. Tennis elbow. Then a herniated disc as an EMT.
I kept thinking it was bad luck. It wasn't. It was a pattern I didn't know how to read yet. Every injury was my body telling me something I didn't have the knowledge to understand at the time.
PT school changed that. I'm not building this despite my injuries. I'm building it because of them.
.
.
.
Follow @themusclespt for more.
#ptstudent #physicaltherapy #injuryprevention #athletejourney #themusclespt

PERSONAL 2 — The Dream
I was seven years old sitting on the couch with my mom watching Grey's Anatomy telling her I was going to be a doctor.
No hesitation. No doubt. That was the plan. She told me I could do anything I set my mind to. I believed her. But I also carried self doubt from as far back as I can remember. Shy. Introverted. Insecure. My own worst critic.
That hasn't fully gone away. Even now. Even building this. Some days I wonder if I'm good enough. And then I think about that seven year old on the couch. Completely certain. I'm not going to let him down.
.
.
.
Follow @themusclespt for more.

#ptstudent #physicaltherapy #motivation #selfdoubt #themusclesptYou said: Whole captions plzWhole captions plzMar 20Claude responded: ---
REEL 1 — Sarcopenia Intro
Most people don't find out they're losing muscle until it's already been happening for a decade.
After 30 you lose 3 to 8 percent of your muscle every ten years. By 80 that number can reach up to 50 percent in inactive adults. And it's the number one predictor of fall risk and loss of independence as you age.
The window to prevent it is open right now. Most people sleep through it.
.
.
.
Follow @themusclespt for more.
#sarcopenia #muscleloss #physicaltherapy #longevity #themusclespt

REEL 2 — Fitness Industry
The fitness industry is built on making you believe you need more. More days. More supplements. More programs.
The research says otherwise. Two resistance training sessions a week is enough to significantly slow muscle loss. The minimum effective dose is way lower than they want you to think.
Follow the science. Not the industry.
.
.
.
Follow @themusclespt for more.
#sarcopenia #strengthtraining #physicaltherapy #fitnessmyths #themusclespt

REEL 3 — Two Workouts A Week
You don't need to live in the gym to build muscle and prevent the decline that comes with age.
Two full body sessions a week. Compound movements. Getting stronger over time. That's the whole framework. Simple, sustainable, and backed by research.
Stop overcomplicating it.
.
.
.
Follow @themusclespt for more.
#musclebuilding #strengthtraining #sarcopenia #longevity #themusclespt

REEL 4 — Protein
You can train perfectly and still lose muscle if your protein is too low.
At least 0.7 grams of protein per pound of bodyweight per day to preserve and build muscle. Most people are getting half that. And older adults actually need more protein than younger people to get the same muscle building response. Not less. More.
Fix your protein before you fix anything else.
.
.
.
Follow @themusclespt for more.
#protein #musclebuilding #sarcopenia #strengthtraining #themusclespt

HOT TAKE 1 — Your Doctor
Your doctor checks your blood pressure, your cholesterol, your weight. Nobody checks your muscle mass. Nobody mentions sarcopenia. Nobody tells you that two resistance training sessions a week and adequate protein could change the entire trajectory of how you age.
The medical system is built to treat. Not prevent. By the time they treat it it's already too late.
That's why I'm building this.
.
.
.
Follow @themusclespt for more.
#sarcopenia #physicaltherapy #longevity #preventivehealth #themusclespt

HOT TAKE 2 — Squats
Avoiding squats because your knees hurt is making your knees worse. Not better.
The muscles around your knee are what protect the joint. When those muscles are weak the knee takes all the load. Squatting builds exactly those muscles. Weakness is not the answer. Strength is.
Agree or disagree? Drop it below.
.
.
.
Follow @themusclespt for more.
#squats #physicaltherapy #kneehealth #strengthtraining #themusclespt

HOT TAKE 3 — Functional Training
Forget the bosu ball. Forget the resistance band circuit your physio gave you. The most functional thing you can do is build muscle.
Functional means your body can do what life demands of it. And all of those things require one thing — strength. The research is clear. The people who age the best are the ones who built and maintained the most muscle.
Stop chasing functional. Start building muscle.
.
.
.
Follow @themusclespt for more.
#musclebuilding #strengthtraining #physicaltherapy #longevity #themusclespt

So I'm gonna keep my back flat against pad. I wanna think as if I'm producing counterforce against the pad as I push pushing out and away. I'm also queuing driving through my first met head. Metacarpal head. And then I'm controlling back through my pinkies. And then I'm keeping my foot contact with the floor just like I did with the pendulum Quite I'm taking a deep, exhale on the eccentric. And then an inhale on the concentric. So I'm controlling my breathing throughout this. I'm not arching my back. I'm not retracting my scapula. I'm letting my scapula move freely in space. Just to keep all attention on my on my chest, you can see I'm my my arm path is lower. Or you can see my arm path kinda goes from low to high. You can see my shoulders are depressed. So they're not shrugging up. And this was I this is gonna get this movement is gonna get, you know, predominantly that mid chest. So those external fibers. But it's also gonna get a little bit of the the clog lumicular You can see there's a little bit of a shoulder flexion component. And the way the machine is designed, it has a bit of a ascending, It has a descending resist profile, so it's gonna be hardest at the bottom. And because it's Because I'm sitting upright versus lying down, I don't have to worry about gravity. Reason why I like I mean, I really saw a machine press like this. Is I don't have to worry about stability. I don't have to worry about the coordination component. I can target my chest much better than using a barbell or some even dumbbells.Show moreMar 21Claude responded: Here's your full script:Here's your full script:

Hook:
"How to press to build muscle and prevent injury."

Spoken:
"Second video in my compound lift series. Squat. Hinge. Push. Pull. Four movements. Everything you need to build muscle and protect your body for life.
We're doing the flat machine chest press. And here's why I choose this over a barbell bench press for most people.
When you're on a barbell you have to manage stability, coordinate the bar path, and balance the load simultaneously. That coordination tax takes attention away from actually loading the chest. The machine eliminates all of that. You're fixed in the movement pattern. No stability demand. No balance component. Just pure chest loading.
The resistance profile on this machine is descending — meaning it's hardest at the bottom and gets slightly easier as you press out. And because I'm seated upright rather than lying flat I don't have to fight gravity the same way I would on a bench. That combination lets me keep full focus on the target muscle the entire rep.
This movement primarily targets the mid chest — those external pec fibers. Because of the arm path there's also a slight shoulder flexion component which means you'll get some clavicular head involvement as well. Your anterior shoulder will contribute to some degree. That's just how horizontal pressing mechanics work.
Now the cues.
Back flat against the pad the entire time. I'm not arching away from it. I'm actually thinking about producing counterforce — pressing my back into the pad as I push out. That keeps my spine neutral and my chest in the right position throughout.
Same foot contact as the squat. Two points through the floor — inner heel and first metatarsal head. Grounded the whole time.
On the press I'm thinking about driving through my first metacarpal head — the base of my index finger. That's where I initiate the force. On the way back I'm controlling through my pinkies — that external rotation cue keeps tension in the chest and stops the shoulder from taking over.
Shoulders stay depressed throughout. Not shrugging up. Not retracting the scapula. I'm letting my scapula move freely in space — that protects the shoulder joint and keeps all the tension where it belongs — on the chest.
Arm path goes from low to high as I press. That's intentional. It follows the natural fiber orientation of the pec.
Breathing. Deep exhale on the eccentric — that's the way back as you lower the weight. Inhale on the concentric as you press out. Controlled breathing throughout. Never holding your breath.
Two to four second eccentric. Own the stretch at the bottom. Drive out with intent.
Train close to failure. That's what stimulates growth.
That's how you press to build muscle and prevent injury for life. I'm the Muscle SPT. I'll see you in the next one."

Same voiceover over b-roll format as the squat. Let me know if anything needs adjusting.You said: CaptionCaptionMar 21Claude responded: Flat Machine Chest Press — Compound Lift Series.Flat Machine Chest Press — Compound Lift Series. Push Edition.
No barbell needed to build a strong chest. The machine eliminates the stability and coordination demand so you can focus entirely on loading the pec. Descending resistance profile, free scapular movement, controlled breathing, and a low to high arm path that follows the natural fiber orientation of the chest.
Cues — back flat into the pad, counterforce through the back, drive through the first metacarpal, control back through the pinkies, shoulders depressed, scapula free, exhale on the way back, inhale on the press. Train close to failure. Every set.
Save this for your next push day.
.
.
.
Follow @themusclespt for more.
