#chesttraining #physicaltherapy #musclebuilding #strengthtraining #themusclesptYou said: How to upload videos to CapCut for AI editsHow to upload videos to CapCut for AI editsMar 25Claude responded: On iPhone:On iPhone:

Open CapCut
Tap the "+" button on the home screen
Select your video from your camera roll
Tap "Add" to import it into the editor

Once it's in the editor:
For AI features specifically:

Tap "Text" at the bottom → "Auto Captions" → it transcribes your speech and adds captions automatically
Tap "Effects" → browse AI effects and animations
Tap "AI tools" if visible on your version — this gives you access to AI generated effects, background removal, and enhancement tools

For auto captions specifically which is what you'll use most:

Import video
Tap "Text" in the bottom toolbar
Tap "Auto Captions"
Select language — English
Tap "Start"
It generates captions automatically synced to your speech
Edit any words it got wrong
Style them to match your brand

One tip: CapCut's auto captions are accurate but not perfect. Always review them before exporting. Takes about 2 minutes to clean up.
That's it. Import, auto caption, style, export, post.You said: Give me brand color againGive me brand color againMar 26Claude responded: #E8FF47#E8FF47You said: Is this good for word emphasis or perhaps a different color that’ shows better on reelsIs this good for word emphasis or perhaps a different color that’ shows better on reelsMar 26Claude responded: For word emphasis on Reels #E8FF47 is actually strong — it pops on dark backgrounds and stops the eye immediately.For word emphasis on Reels #E8FF47 is actually strong — it pops on dark backgrounds and stops the eye immediately.
The only issue is if your background is light or mixed — yellow disappears on white.
Since your videos are dark background talking head content #E8FF47 works perfectly. High contrast, attention grabbing, on brand.
If you ever want an alternative for emphasis that reads differently:
#FF8B64 — your warm orange. Slightly warmer energy, still high contrast on dark backgrounds. Good for emotional or urgent words.
#FFFFFF — pure white at a larger font size also works as emphasis when everything else is smaller.
But for your format — dark background, talking head, one word emphasis — stick with #E8FF47. It's doing exactly what it should.You said: Orange doesn’t work.Orange doesn’t work. I think I need a blue or turquoise or greenMar 26Claude responded: Here are some options that work well on dark backgrounds for word emphasis:Here are some options that work well on dark backgrounds for word emphasis:
Blue/Turquoise:
#4ECDC4 — clean teal, high contrast on dark, modern and clinical feeling. Already in your brand palette from the dashboards we built.
#00D4FF — bright cyan blue. Very high contrast, punchy, stops the scroll.
#38B6FF — softer sky blue. Clean and readable without being harsh.
Green:
#39FF14 — neon green. Very high contrast but might clash with your yellow-green brand color since they're similar.
#00E676 — bright mint green. Clean and fresh, reads well on dark.

My honest recommendation:
#4ECDC4 — the teal. Here's why. It's already in your existing brand system so it's not introducing a new color out of nowhere. It reads as clinical and authoritative which fits your PT positioning. And on a dark background with white body text and yellow-green for your main brand moments — teal as your emphasis color creates a clean three color system that feels intentional.
Test it on one video and see how it feels against your skin tone and background lighting. That's the real test.You said: Are there any apps where I can, like, draw force vectors on pictures to, like, for, like, biomechanic analysis of, like, for example, a squat.Are there any apps where I can, like, draw force vectors on pictures to, like, for, like, biomechanic analysis of, like, for example, a squat. Right, where I can draw the I can draw, like, moment arms. I can draw, lever arms, you know, draw lines on it. Are there any good apps for that? To, like, edit pictures and videos and stuff like that, to draw?Mar 26Claude responded: Yes a few good options:Yes a few good options:
Best for what you're describing:
Notability — iPad app, you can import a screenshot or image and draw directly on top of it. Free hand drawing, straight lines, arrows, different colors. Clean and intuitive. This is probably your fastest option for annotating biomechanics images.
GoodNotes — similar to Notability, import image and annotate with lines, arrows, force vectors. Very popular with students and clinicians for exactly this kind of visual annotation.
Concepts — more advanced vector drawing app. You can draw precise lines, arrows, and annotations over imported images. More control than Notability but slightly steeper learning curve.

For video specifically:
CapCut — you can add arrows and lines as overlays on video clips. Not as precise as drawing on a still image but workable for simple force vector illustrations on footage.
Coach's Eye — specifically designed for movement analysis. You can draw lines, angles, and vectors directly on video frames. Built for exactly what you're describing — biomechanical breakdown of movement patterns. Worth looking at.

My recommendation:
Notability or GoodNotes for images — screenshot a squat position, import it, draw your moment arms and force vectors in yellow-green and white to match your brand, export and use as b-roll or carousel content.
Coach's Eye for video — if you want to annotate actual movement footage frame by frame.You said: On the iPhone specificallyOn the iPhone specificallyMar 26Claude responded: On iPhone specifically:On iPhone specifically:
Best options:
Notability — available on iPhone, import a photo, draw arrows and lines directly on it. Clean and simple. This is your best bet for quick biomechanics annotations on the go.
Sketchbook — free, powerful drawing tools, import any image and draw force vectors, moment arms, lines in any color. More control than Notability for precise annotations.
Procreate Pocket — iPhone version of Procreate. Most powerful drawing app available on iPhone. Import image, draw precise arrows and vectors in any color, layer them. Small one time purchase around $5. Worth it if you want the cleanest output.

For video annotation on iPhone:
Coach's Eye — works on iPhone, draw lines and angles directly on video frames, pause and annotate specific positions in a movement. Built for this exact use case.
Hudl Technique — similar to Coach's Eye, free, draw on video frames for movement analysis.

Simplest workflow for your content:
Screenshot a squat position → open in Notability or Sketchbook → draw your force vectors and moment arms in #E8FF47 and white → export → use as educational b-roll or carousel slide
That becomes incredibly high value content that almost nobody else in the fitness space is producing. A PT student annotating biomechanics on actual images is next level authority building.You said: Should I get stylus?Should I get stylus? And what brand would you recommend on Amazon?Mar 26Claude responded: Yes — for this specific use case a stylus makes a huge difference.Yes — for this specific use case a stylus makes a huge difference. Drawing precise force vectors and moment arms with your finger is frustrating. A stylus gives you the control you need to make it look clean and professional.

If you have an iPad — Apple Pencil all day.
Nothing comes close for precision and responsiveness on iPad. If you're doing this content regularly and have an iPad it's worth the investment.

For iPhone specifically:
Best budget option:
Adonit Note+ or Adonit Dash — around $30 to $40 on Amazon. Reliable, good tip precision, works well with drawing apps. Most creators use Adonit for iPhone annotation work.
Mid range:
Zagg Pro Stylus — around $50 to $60. Good pressure sensitivity, pairs well with iPhone, clean tip for precise lines.
Best overall for iPhone:
Adonit Pro 4 — around $60 to $70. The most precise stylus available for non-Apple Pencil devices. If you're serious about annotation content this is the one.

Honest take:
If you have an iPad even an older one — do the annotation work there using Apple Pencil or a compatible stylus and film or screenshot from there. The larger screen makes the annotations look significantly more professional than iPhone sized work.
But if iPhone is all you have — Adonit Pro 4 is your best option.You said: Send Amazon links to ones you recommendSend Amazon links to ones you recommendMar 26Claude responded: I can't send Amazon links directly — I don't have the ability to browse or generate live URLs.I can't send Amazon links directly — I don't have the ability to browse or generate live URLs.
But here's exactly what to search on Amazon to find them:
Best budget:
Search — "Adonit Dash 4 stylus"
Best mid range:
Search — "Adonit Pro 4 stylus"
Best overall value:
Search — "Zagg Pro Stylus iPhone"

Filter by:

4 stars and above
Compatible with your iPhone model
Check the reviews specifically mentioning drawing apps like Procreate or Notability

Any of the Adonit options will serve you well for biomechanics annotations. The Pro 4 is the one I'd go with if you want precision lines for force vectors and moment arms.You said: What about bopomofo 2 in 1 stylusWhat about bopomofo 2 in 1 stylusMar 26Claude responded: Never heard of that specific brand — let me be straight with you rather than guess.Never heard of that specific brand — let me be straight with you rather than guess.
Search it on Amazon and check:
What to look for in the reviews:

Does it work well with drawing apps specifically — Notability, Sketchbook, Procreate Pocket
Is the tip precise enough for fine lines or is it a thick rubber tip
Does it have palm rejection or will your hand interfere while drawing

The red flag with budget styluses:
Most cheap 2-in-1 styluses have a thick rubber disc tip or rubber ball tip. Those are fine for tapping and scrolling but terrible for precise drawing. You cannot draw a clean force vector line with a thick rubber tip — it'll look sloppy.
What you want:
A fine tip stylus — 1.5mm to 2mm tip size — for precise annotation work.
If the Bopomofo has a fine tip and good reviews specifically mentioning drawing apps it could work. If it has the thick rubber disc tip skip it and go with an Adonit.
What does the product listing say about the tip size?You said: How do I do it where I’m drawing in real time and it captures that?How do I do it where I’m drawing in real time and it captures that?Mar 26Claude responded: That's screen recording.That's screen recording. Here's exactly how:
On iPhone:

Go to Settings → Control Center
Add "Screen Recording" to your Control Center if it's not already there
Open your drawing app — Notability, Sketchbook, or Procreate Pocket
Import your biomechanics image
Swipe down from top right corner to open Control Center
Tap the screen record button — it counts down 3 seconds then starts recording everything on your screen
Start drawing your force vectors, moment arms, arrows in real time
When done swipe down and tap the red recording button to stop
Video saves automatically to your camera roll


That footage becomes incredible content:
You're showing a squat position and drawing moment arms and force vectors in real time while narrating over the top in your voiceover. That is PT school level content that nobody in the fitness space is producing. It looks like a sports science breakdown. It positions you as the most educated person in the room immediately.

The workflow:
Screenshot squat position → open in drawing app → screen record while you draw and explain → voiceover in CapCut → post as educational Reel
That format alone could be one of your highest performing content types. It's visual, educational, and completely unique to your background.You said: Any better apps to do this with?Any better apps to do this with?Mar 26Claude responded: Yes a few that are specifically built for this:Yes a few that are specifically built for this:
Best for real time drawing on video:
Coach's Eye — specifically designed for athletic movement analysis. You import a video, pause it at any frame, draw lines and angles directly on it in real time, and record your analysis. Built exactly for what you're describing. Free to start.
Hudl Technique — similar to Coach's Eye. Draw angles, lines, and annotations on video frames. Free. Very popular with coaches and sports scientists.
Kinovea — more advanced biomechanics analysis tool. Angle measurement, force vector drawing, trajectory tracking. More clinical and technical than the others. Desktop based though not iPhone.

Best for real time drawing on images with screen record:
Procreate Pocket — most professional output. The drawing tools are smooth and precise and when you screen record the drawing process it looks incredibly polished. The line smoothing feature makes your vectors look clean even if your hand isn't perfectly steady.
Adobe Fresco — free on iPhone, professional drawing tools, import image and draw over it. Very clean output.

My honest recommendation for your content:
Coach's Eye for video analysis — pause the squat video, draw your moment arms and force vectors directly on the frame, screen record the whole thing, narrate over it in CapCut.
Procreate Pocket for image annotation — cleanest looking output for still image breakdowns.
Those two cover everything you need.You said: Are they any that ai automictailly draws for youAre they any that ai automictailly draws for youMar 26Claude responded: Honestly not yet — not in a clean plug and play way for biomechanics specifically.Honestly not yet — not in a clean plug and play way for biomechanics specifically.
There are some tools moving in that direction:
Sportsbox AI — uses AI to analyze movement and automatically overlays joint angles, skeletal tracking, and movement data on video. Specifically built for sports movement analysis. This is probably the closest thing to what you're describing. Worth looking at — it's available on iPhone.
Kinovea — has some automatic angle tracking but it's desktop only and still requires manual input.
PoseNet and similar pose estimation tools — AI that automatically detects body landmarks and draws skeletal overlays on video. Some apps use this technology but they're not consumer friendly yet and the output isn't always clean enough for content.

Honest answer:
The technology exists in research and enterprise settings but a consumer app that automatically draws clean biomechanically accurate force vectors and moment arms on your squat video doesn't really exist yet in a way that would produce content quality output.
What most PT and sports science creators do right now:
Use Coach's Eye or Procreate Pocket and draw it themselves on screen record. It actually looks better anyway because your audience watches you analyze it in real time which builds more authority than an automated overlay.
Your manual annotation with your clinical knowledge narrating over it is more valuable than any AI doing it automatically. That's the whole point — it's you doing the analysis.You said: Does proceeate have rulerDoes proceeate have rulerMar 26Claude responded: Yes.Yes. Procreate Pocket has a ruler tool.
It's called "Drawing Guide" — here's how to access it:

Open your canvas in Procreate Pocket
Tap the wrench icon (Actions menu) top left
Tap "Canvas"
Toggle on "Drawing Guide"
Tap "Edit Drawing Guide"
Select "Straight Lines" or "Grid" depending on what you need

For force vectors and moment arms specifically:
Turn on "Snapping" which forces your lines to snap to straight angles — 45, 90, 180 degrees. This makes your vectors look perfectly straight and professional without needing a steady hand.
Also use the "QuickLine" feature — draw a line and hold at the end without lifting your stylus and it automatically snaps to a perfectly straight line. No ruler needed. That's the fastest way to draw clean force vectors in real time.
That QuickLine feature alone makes Procreate Pocket the best option for your biomechanics annotation content. Clean straight lines every time, screen recorded in real time, narrated over in CapCut. That's a premium content format.You said: Give me the carousel caption I made on sarcopeniaGive me the carousel caption I made on sarcopeniaMar 26Claude responded: Most people don't realize they're losing muscle until the signs are impossible to ignore.Most people don't realize they're losing muscle until the signs are impossible to ignore.
Getting weaker. Losing definition. Always tired. Getting injured more. Losing grip strength. These aren't just signs of aging. They're signs of sarcopenia — the muscle loss that begins at 30 and accelerates every decade after.
Save this and share it with someone who needs to see it.

#sarcopenia #muscleloss #physicaltherapy #longevity #themusclesptYou said: Carousel getting no likes.Carousel getting no likes. Should I hide likes?Mar 26Claude responded: No.No. Same answer as before — don't hide them.
But let's diagnose why the carousel isn't performing rather than hiding the evidence.
A few questions worth thinking through:
Was it posted between 6 and 8pm?
Timing matters especially during your reset period.
Did you share it to Stories immediately after posting?
Carousels need that initial push from your warm audience to signal to the algorithm it's worth distributing.
Did the first slide stop the scroll?
The 5 Signs carousel has a strong hook visually — but if people aren't swiping through the algorithm doesn't know it's a carousel worth pushing. First slide has to earn the swipe.
How long has it been posted?
Carousels actually have a longer distribution window than Reels. The algorithm shows them twice — once on first view and again to people who didn't engage. Give it 48 to 72 hours before judging performance.

The honest reality:
You're in week one of a reset on a suppressed account. Every post right now is laying groundwork not going viral. The carousel format is correct. The content is strong. The design looks professional.
Two weeks of consistent posting before you evaluate anything. Don't touch the likes. Keep going.You said: Yep.Yep. It’s literally getting no likesMar 26Claude responded: That's the algorithm suppression doing exactly what suppressed algorithms do.That's the algorithm suppression doing exactly what suppressed algorithms do. It's not showing your content to enough people to get likes yet.
Here's the reality of where you are right now:
Your account went through months of off-brand content, inconsistent posting, and high frequency volume posting. The algorithm essentially stopped trusting your account to deliver content worth distributing. That trust takes time to rebuild — typically 2 to 4 weeks of consistent on-brand content before you start seeing meaningful distribution again.
Zero likes doesn't mean bad content. It means barely anyone is seeing it yet.
What to do right now:
Share every post to your Stories immediately after posting. Your Stories audience is your warmest audience — people who actively seek out your content. Getting engagement from them signals to the algorithm that people who already follow you find value in it which slowly rebuilds distribution.
Engage with other accounts in your niche. Comment genuinely on posts from accounts in the PT, fitness, and longevity space. That activity signals to the algorithm that your account is active and engaged which helps distribution.
Reply to every comment you get no matter how few. Even one genuine reply creates an engagement signal.
The mindset shift you need right now:
Stop measuring success by likes for the next 3 weeks. Measure it by consistency. Did you post today? Did you reply to comments? Did you share to Stories? That's the only scorecard that matters right now.
The likes will come. But only if you keep going through the period where they aren't there yet.You said: So even if it ends up getting 5 likes total?So even if it ends up getting 5 likes total? That’s okay?Mar 26Claude responded: Yes.Yes. That's okay right now.
Here's why. Five likes on a suppressed account in week one of a reset means five people saw it and genuinely responded. That's five engagement signals telling the algorithm this content has value. It's not zero.
The accounts that recover fastest from algorithm suppression are the ones that keep posting quality content consistently even when the numbers are embarrassing. The ones that slow down or stop are the ones that stay suppressed.
Think of it like this. You're not posting for likes right now. You're posting to retrain the algorithm. Every post is a deposit into a trust account with Instagram. The returns don't show up immediately but they compound over time.
The numbers that actually matter right now are:
Did you post today — yes or no.
Did you share to Stories — yes or no.
Did you reply to every comment — yes or no.
That's it. Likes are a lagging indicator. They'll follow the consistency. But consistency has to come first.
Three weeks from now you'll look back at this period as the foundation. The five like posts are part of that foundation whether they feel like it or not.
Keep going.You said: So don’t hide likes?So don’t hide likes?Mar 26Claude responded: No.No. Don't hide them. Final answer.You said: Ever?Ever?Mar 26Claude responded: No.No. Never for you specifically.
You're building a brand around authority and credibility. Hiding likes signals insecurity. It draws more attention to the issue than just leaving them visible. And the people who matter — potential coaching clients — never look at like counts anyway. They watch the video, feel something, and DM you.
Leave them visible always. Let the content do the work.You said: Why is NO ONE seeing it?Why is NO ONE seeing it? Do I boost it?Mar 26Claude responded: Don't boost it.Don't boost it. Paid promotion on a suppressed account during a reset is wasted money. It won't fix the underlying algorithm issue.
Here's exactly why nobody is seeing it right now:
The algorithm suppression is real. Months of off-brand content, inconsistent posting, and high frequency volume posting trained Instagram to stop distributing your content broadly. It takes 2 to 4 weeks of consistent quality posting to rebuild that trust. You're in week one. This is expected.
What's actually happening: Instagram is testing your content on a very small sample of your followers — maybe 1 to 3% of your 188K. If that small sample doesn't engage strongly enough it stops distributing. With a suppressed account that sample is even smaller than normal.
What actually fixes it — not boosting:
Share every single post to Stories the moment it goes live. Every time. Non negotiable.
Engage with 10 to 15 accounts in your niche every day. Genuine comments on PT, fitness, and longevity content. This signals account activity to the algorithm.
Use all 5 hashtags every post. Relevant every time.
Post consistently at 6 to 8pm every day without exception.
Reply to every comment within 60 minutes no matter how few there are.
The timeline honestly:
Week 1 and 2 — feels like nobody sees anything. This is where you are.
Week 3 — slight improvement in reach.
Week 4 — algorithm starts rebuilding trust.
Month 2 — meaningful distribution begins returning.
You cannot shortcut this. You can only stay consistent through it.You said: Are there specific apps that allow me to incorporate Like, reaction videos in the background, like b roll of an Instagram clip, essentially.Are there specific apps that allow me to incorporate Like, reaction videos in the background, like b roll of an Instagram clip, essentially. Right? Like a green screen, let's just say. And then me, like, in a little bubble. Kinda like Mojo does it, but Mojo It's extremely shitty editing. And isolating specifically. So I just want something that allows just 4 me to allow, like, a green screen, but then have, like, my face is a little bubble where it almost looks like I'm watching the video. That makes sense. Give me some app rep, the best app rep the best app recommendationMar 27Claude responded: Yes — that's a picture in picture format with a green screen background.Yes — that's a picture in picture format with a green screen background. Here are your best options:

Best for exactly what you're describing:
CapCut — already on your phone and does this natively. Here's how:

Import the Instagram clip as your main background video
Tap "Overlay"
Add your reaction camera footage as the overlay
Resize and position your face bubble to the corner
Use "Chroma Key" if you filmed yourself on a green screen to remove the background cleanly

That's your reaction video format done in CapCut in about 5 minutes.

Other options:
Videoleap — cleaner interface than CapCut for picture in picture. Layer videos on top of each other, resize, position your face bubble exactly where you want it. Very intuitive on iPhone.
KineMaster — more advanced multi layer video editor. Green screen removal, picture in picture, precise positioning. Slightly more complex but most control of any mobile editor.

My recommendation:
Try CapCut first since you already have it. The overlay and chroma key features handle exactly what you're describing. If the green screen removal isn't clean enough in CapCut then download Videoleap — it has better chroma key processing.
Do you have an actual green screen or are you filming against your yellow-green background light?You said: I’ve done CapCut.I’ve done CapCut. Not what I want. I want the reaction of me to be smaller. And the background the videoMar 27Claude responded: Got it — so you want the Instagram clip as the full background and your face as a small reaction bubble in the corner.Got it — so you want the Instagram clip as the full background and your face as a small reaction bubble in the corner. Like a podcast reaction format.
In CapCut you can do this — the issue is just sizing. Here's the fix:
In CapCut when you add yourself as an overlay — pinch inward with two fingers to make your face bubble smaller. You can shrink it down to whatever size you want and drag it to any corner. That should give you exactly the small bubble reaction look you're going for.
But if CapCut still isn't giving you what you want:
Videoleap — this is probably your answer. The picture in picture control is more intuitive and precise than CapCut. You can make your face bubble as small as you want, add a border around it, round the corners, and position it exactly where you want on screen. Looks much cleaner than CapCut for this specific format.
Splice — another clean iPhone editor with good picture in picture control. Free and simple.
LumaFusion — most powerful mobile video editor available on iPhone. Complete control over every layer, size, position, and styling of your reaction bubble. One time purchase around $30. If you're serious about the reaction video format this is the professional option.
Try Videoleap first. It's free and will most likely give you exactly what you're picturing.You said: Tips to make editing better?Tips to make editing better? More engaging hooks. Make videos shorterMar 28Claude responded: Here are the most impactful things you can do:Here are the most impactful things you can do:

HOOKS — first 2 seconds:
Cut straight into the most provocative line. No intro, no "hey guys", no context setting. The hook is word one. If you're not saying something that stops a scroll in the first two seconds you've already lost them.
Add text on screen the moment the video starts. Don't wait. The hook text should appear frame one.
Make the first visual jarring or unexpected. Jump cut, zoom cut, or a bold statement that creates instant curiosity or tension.

EDITING — pace and energy:
Cut out every pause, every filler word, every breath gap. If your mouth isn't saying something meaningful cut it. Silences kill watch time.
Add a jump cut every 3 to 5 seconds minimum. Even if you're saying one continuous thought cut between sentences to create visual momentum.
Zoom in slightly between cuts. A subtle push in creates energy without being distracting.
Use sound effects sparingly — a whoosh on a cut or a subtle bass hit on a key word adds production value instantly in CapCut.

LENGTH — ruthless editing:
Every sentence should earn its place. Ask yourself — does removing this sentence lose anything important? If no, cut it.
Your target is under 60 seconds for education. Under 45 seconds for hot takes. Under 90 seconds for exercise demos.
If you can say it in 10 words don't use 20.

ONE RULE THAT COVERS ALL THREE:
Watch your video back with no sound. If it still holds your attention visually the editing is working. If you lose interest it needs faster cuts and more visual variety.You said: Any other video format ideas too?Any other video format ideas too?Mar 28Claude responded: Yes — here are formats that would work specifically for your brand and content:Yes — here are formats that would work specifically for your brand and content:

HIGH PERFORMING FORMATS:
Split screen before and after — show a common exercise done wrong on one side and your corrected version on the other. No words needed. Visual contrast does all the work.
Point of view — camera is the patient or client. You're looking down at them explaining something. Or camera is your eyes walking into the gym, into clinic, going through your day. Immersive and different.
Myth vs fact — text on screen. "MYTH: You need to train 6 days a week." Cut. "FACT: Research shows 2 sessions a week significantly slows muscle loss." Fast, shareable, saveable.
30 second drill breakdown — no talking. Just you demonstrating an exercise with text cues appearing on screen synced to each movement. Clean, fast, educational.
Day in my life time lapse — speed up footage of your full day. PT school, training, filming content, studying. 30 to 45 seconds. Shows the grind without requiring a script.
Reply to comment — someone asks a question in your comments, you make a video answering it directly. Instagram and TikTok both boost this format heavily because it drives comment engagement.
This vs that — two exercises side by side. Pendulum squat vs barbell squat. Machine press vs barbell press. Fast cuts, text labels, quick explanation of why you prefer one.
Talking to camera walking — film yourself walking into the gym or clinic talking directly to camera. Movement adds energy to talking head content without any extra editing.
3 things nobody tells you about X — listicle format. Fast cuts between each point. Text on screen counting them. High save rate because people want to remember all three.

FOR YOUR SPECIFIC BRAND:
"What I learned in clinic this week" — one observation from rotations translated into something actionable for your audience. When rotations start this becomes your most unique content.
Annotated movement analysis — the Procreate Pocket screen record format we talked about. You drawing force vectors on a squat image in real time. Nobody else in your space is doing this.
Client question answered — anonymized question from a coaching client becomes a public educational video. Bridges your coaching business and your content.
The 60 second PT assessment — teach your audience one thing a PT looks for that they can self assess. Patellar tracking, hip drop during a single leg squat, shoulder mobility test. Interactive and highly shareable.

The reply to comment format and the this vs that format are probably your two highest leverage additions right now. Both are low effort to produce and both get boosted by the algorithm.You said: Caption for the underhand chest press video we didCaption for the underhand chest press video we didMar 28Claude responded: The underhand Smith machine press is not a gimmick.The underhand Smith machine press is not a gimmick. There is actual biomechanical reasoning behind it.
The supinated grip increases shoulder flexion from an extended position and creates a slightly greater stretch on the upper chest at the bottom of the movement. Both of these factors shift more demand onto the clavicular fibers of the pec. Is it the only way to train upper chest? No. Is it a legitimate tool worth adding? Yes.
As always — setup and arm path matter more than grip variation.

#chesttraining #physicaltherapy #musclebuilding #strengthtraining #themusclesptYou said: Understanding Motor Unit Recruitment in Less than 30 Seconds write me a script for this.Understanding Motor Unit Recruitment in Less than 30 Seconds write me a script for this. Make it simplistic as if I was taking to a toddler. To reach a wide audience engaging and so people understand complicated concepts in layman terms without jargon or fluffMar 28Claude responded: Hook:Hook:
"Motor unit recruitment explained in under 30 seconds."
Spoken:
"Think of your muscle like an army.
Each soldier is a muscle fiber. And they don't all show up at once.
When you pick up something light — only a few soldiers show up. Easy task. No need for everyone.
When you pick up something heavy — the general calls in more soldiers. More fibers recruited. More force produced.
That's motor unit recruitment. Your brain decides how many muscle fibers to activate based on how much force you need.
And here's why this matters for your training. If you never lift heavy enough to call in all the soldiers — you never train your full muscle. You're leaving fibers on the bench every single session.
Progressive overload. Heavy enough to recruit everything. That's how you actually build muscle."
End card:
"I'm the Muscle SPT. Like and follow for more."
Caption:
Your muscle is an army. And not all soldiers show up unless you call them in.
Light weight recruits a few fibers. Heavy progressive load recruits everything. If you're never training close to your limit you're leaving muscle fibers completely untrained every single session.
That's motor unit recruitment. And that's why intensity matters.
.
.
.
Like & follow @themusclespt for more!
#musclebuilding #strengthtraining #physicaltherapy #fitness #themusclesptYou said: Understanding muscle growth in less than 30 secondsUnderstanding muscle growth in less than 30 secondsMar 28Claude responded: Hook:Hook:
"How muscle actually grows. In 30 seconds."
Spoken:
"Your muscle doesn't grow in the gym.
It grows when you rest.
Here's what actually happens. You go to the gym and you challenge your muscle with enough load that you create tiny micro tears in the muscle fibers. Your body detects damage and sends a repair crew.
But your body doesn't just repair it back to what it was. It builds it back slightly bigger and stronger so it can handle that load next time.
That's muscle growth. Damage. Repair. Adapt.
But here's where most people mess up. They don't give the repair crew enough to work with. You need protein — at least 0.7 grams per pound of bodyweight — and you need sleep. That's when the repair crew does its best work.
Train. Eat. Sleep. Repeat. That's the whole system."
End card:
"I'm the Muscle SPT. Like and follow for more."
Caption:
Your muscle doesn't grow in the gym. It grows when you rest.
Training creates the stimulus. Protein gives your body the raw material to rebuild. Sleep is when the actual growth happens. Miss any one of those three and you're leaving gains on the table.
Train. Eat. Sleep. Repeat.
.
.
.
Like & follow @themusclespt for more!
#musclebuilding #strengthtraining #physicaltherapy #fitness #themusclesptYou said: mechanical tension.mechanical tension. Causes growthMar 28Claude responded: Hook:Hook:
"The real reason your muscles grow."
Spoken:
"It comes down to one thing. Mechanical tension.
Think of it like this. When you stretch a rubber band it creates tension. Your muscle works the same way. When you load it under tension — especially in a stretched position — it sends a signal to your body.
That signal says — this muscle is being challenged. We need to adapt. We need to grow.
The greater the tension. The stronger the signal. The more growth.
This is why range of motion matters. A full squat creates more tension through the quad than a half squat. A full stretch on a Romanian deadlift creates more tension through the hamstring than a partial rep.
And this is why training close to failure matters. The last few reps of a set — when the muscle is working its hardest — is when tension is at its peak. That's where the growth signal is strongest.
More tension. More signal. More growth. That's the whole equation."
End card:
"I'm the Muscle SPT. Like and follow for more."
Caption:
Mechanical tension is the primary driver of muscle growth. Not soreness. Not the pump. Tension.
Load your muscles through a full range of motion close to failure and you maximize the growth signal. Cut the range short or leave too many reps in the tank and you're leaving that signal on the table.
Full range. High tension. Close to failure. Every set.
.
.
.
Like & follow @themusclespt for more!
#musclebuilding #mechanicaltension #strengthtraining #physicaltherapy #themusclesptYou said: Now make a script and you go, like, understanding muscle growth in under 30 seconds.Now make a script and you go, like, understanding muscle growth in under 30 seconds. We talk about how when you apply a load, with a high degree of mechanical tension It sends what's called mechanotransduction signals to create an atom to over overcome that load that sending essentially, like, a survival mechanism telling your body that you need get stronger I Again, talk as if I'm talking to a toddler. And explain more the More in-depth behind that, but, like, simple no fluff to the point under 30 seconds.Mar 28Claude responded: Hook:Hook:
"How muscle actually grows. In 30 seconds."
Spoken:
"When you lift something heavy your muscle feels the load.
And your body freaks out a little.
It sends out an emergency signal — called mechanotransduction — which is basically your body saying — that was hard. We almost couldn't handle it. We need to get stronger so that never happens again.
That signal tells your muscle cells to start building. More protein. More fibers. More size.
It's a survival mechanism. Your body doesn't want to be caught off guard by that load again so it adapts and grows to handle it next time.
But here's the key. The signal only fires when the tension is high enough. Too light and your body doesn't care. It's not threatened. No signal. No growth.
That's why you have to train hard. Close to failure. Heavy enough to make your body think — okay we need to do something about this."
End card:
"I'm the Muscle SPT. Like and follow for more."
Caption:
Your body grows muscle as a survival response.
Apply enough mechanical tension through heavy progressive loading and your muscle sends out an emergency signal — mechanotransduction — telling your body to adapt and get stronger so it can handle that load next time.
Too light and the signal never fires. No threat. No adaptation. No growth.
Train hard enough to make your body respond.
.

#musclebuilding #mechanicaltension #strengthtraining #physicaltherapy #themusclesptYou said: Did you know there are actually 5 heads to the quad?Did you know there are actually 5 heads to the quad? Not 4 like make of the amount and the tensor vastus intermedius mentioning, you know, how we have the Vastas intermedius, vastasorladyvastasladderalis of vastor medialis, and then Rexha Moore is also have 5th ballattendant, talk about his function, why it's important, this new research, Can we train it? Can we isolate it? Is it worth understanding why make this in under a minute. Type thing. And just kinda and I'm like, this is kind of a cool thing. Like, a fun fact. You know?Mar 28Claude responded: Hook:Hook:
"Your quad actually has 5 heads. Not 4."
Spoken:
"Most people know the four heads of the quadriceps. Vastus lateralis. Vastus medialis. Vastus intermedius. And rectus femoris.
But newer research has identified a fifth head. The tensor vastus intermedius. It sits between the vastus lateralis and vastus intermedius — deeper in the thigh — and it has its own distinct muscle belly, its own nerve supply, and its own tendon contribution to the quad.
Now can you isolate it? Probably not. Can you specifically train it? Not really. It functions as part of the overall quad mechanism contributing to knee extension just like the other four.
But here's why this matters. It changes how we think about quad anatomy, how we interpret imaging, and potentially how we approach certain knee conditions where the quad is involved.
The quad is more complex than we thought. And honestly that's kind of cool.
Science keeps evolving. And so should our understanding of the body."
End card:
"I'm the Muscle SPT. Like and follow for more."
Caption:
Your quad has 5 heads. Not 4.
The tensor vastus intermedius sits deep between the vastus lateralis and vastus intermedius with its own muscle belly, nerve supply, and tendon. It was only recently identified and confirmed through cadaveric and MRI research.
Can you isolate it? No. Does it change how we think about quad anatomy and knee function? Absolutely.
The body is more complex than we think.
.
.
.

#anatomy #physicaltherapy #musclebuilding #quadriceps #themusclesptYou said: It also plays a role in patellar stability.It also plays a role in patellar stability.Mar 28Claude responded: Hook:Hook:
"Your quad actually has 5 heads. Not 4."
Spoken:
"Most people know the four heads of the quadriceps. Vastus lateralis. Vastus medialis. Vastus intermedius. And rectus femoris.
But newer research has identified a fifth head. The tensor vastus intermedius. It sits between the vastus lateralis and vastus intermedius — deeper in the thigh — and it has its own distinct muscle belly, its own nerve supply, and its own tendon contribution to the quad.
And here's where it gets interesting. It also plays a role in patellar stability. Meaning this muscle contributes to keeping your kneecap tracking properly during movement. Which has real implications for conditions like patellofemoral pain and knee dysfunction.
Now can you isolate it? Probably not. Can you specifically train it? Not directly. It functions as part of the overall quad mechanism contributing to knee extension alongside the other four.
But here's why this matters. #anatomy #physicaltherapy #quadriceps #kneehealth #themusclesptYou said: Also, for the reaction reels, they can be, like, myth busted type things.Also, for the reaction reels, they can be, like, myth busted type things. So maybe, like, fact or fiction or, like, myth Or valid or give me some ideas for those kind of things, those reaction reels. That I can kinda play on Like, it's a hook or it's like an ending, you know, type thing. Give me a multiple things. What do you think be, like, catchy for, like, a reaction style videos where I'm, like, video then Back into it. We're doing miss or fact or fiction, but, like, different You know, blank or blank.Mar 28Claude responded: Here are your options:Here are your options:

Best ones:
Valid or Cap — current, punchy, younger audience will immediately get it. Cap means lie in internet slang. Instantly polarizing.
Legit or Myth — clean and simple. Works for any fitness claim.
Science or Fiction — positions you as the clinical authority immediately. Implies you're fact checking against research.
PT Approved or PT Banned — unique to your brand. Nobody else can use this credibly. Strong authority signal.
Fact or Fraud — more aggressive. Great for hot take energy reaction videos.

How to use it as a format:
Show the clip. Pause. Text on screen — "VALID or CAP?" Build the suspense for 2 seconds. Then cut back to you with the answer and breakdown.
Or flip it — open with the verdict first. "CAP." Then show the clip. Then explain why. That structure is more provocative because you lead with the judgment.

My top three for your brand:
PT Approved or PT Banned — most unique to you
Science or Fiction — most authority building
Valid or Cap — most shareable and current
Mix all three so your reaction series doesn't feel repetitive. Different format same concept rotated week to week.You said: Almost like almost like a play on, like, fitness influencer I think.Almost like almost like a play on, like, fitness influencer I think. Deboned, you know? Finishing for insert videos debunked or something, you know, make it simple Again, as if I'm talking to a childlike type thing, Super, like, catlike cookie too. Like, a good book. Type 5.Mar 28Claude responded: Here are your options:Here are your options:
"Fitness Influencer. Debunked."
Clean. Direct. Positions you against the industry immediately. Every reaction video starts with that title on screen and your audience knows exactly what they're about to watch.

Other options in the same vein:
"They lied to you. Here's the truth."
"The internet said this. Science says that."
"Gym bro vs PT student."
"Your favorite influencer got this wrong."
"Fitness myth. Destroyed."
"The gym got this wrong. Again."

My pick:
"Gym bro vs PT student."
Here's why. It's a character vs character format. Instantly understood. Slightly playful. And it positions you as the clinical authority without being arrogant about it. Every reaction video you make becomes an episode of that ongoing battle. Gym bro says X. PT student says actually here's what the research says.
It's also a series name that people will recognize and come back for. They'll start tagging their gym friends saying "this is literally you."
That's the format that builds a loyal audience fast.You said: Yeah.Yeah. I don't wanna make it only Gymbro, but maybe, like, fitness influencer versus PT student. Type thing. I I don't know. Or just, like,Mar 28Claude responded: "Fitness Influencer vs PT Student."Fitness Influencer vs PT Student."
That's the one. Here's why it works:
It's a clear character dynamic — the person with clout versus the person with clinical knowledge. Your audience immediately knows whose side they're on and who's about to win.
It's also broader than gym bro. Gym bro implies one type of person. Fitness influencer covers everyone — bodybuilders, CrossFit coaches, wellness gurus, celebrity trainers. Infinite content.
And it positions you perfectly. You're not attacking anyone personally. You're just a PT student who knows what the research actually says.

How to use it:

This is your differentiator.You said: Top of the funnel versus bottom of the funnel, what does that mean?Top of the funnel versus bottom of the funnel, what does that mean?Mar 28Claude responded: Simple breakdown:Simple breakdown:

Top of funnel — strangers.
People who have never heard of you. They're scrolling and your content stops them. They don't know you, don't follow you, don't trust you yet. Top of funnel content is designed to reach as many new people as possible and convert them into followers.
This is your — Fitness Influencer vs PT Student, Gimmick or Gains, Fun Facts, In Under 30 Seconds, The Bodybuilder PT Student content. Broad reach. High shareability. Designed to attract cold audiences.

Bottom of funnel — warm audience.
People who already follow you, already trust you, already consume your content regularly. Bottom of funnel content converts that trust into action — a coaching inquiry, an email signup, a purchase.
This is your — Sunday soft sell Stories, coaching application posts, email lead magnet promotions. Smaller reach but much higher conversion. Speaking directly to people who already believe in what you're building.

The simple analogy:
Top of funnel is the net you cast to catch new fish. Bottom of funnel is where you reel them in.
Most creators only do one or the other. The ones who build real businesses do both consistently. Your educational and reaction content fills the top. Your soft sell and coaching content works the bottom.
Right now your priority is top of funnel. Get new eyes on the brand. Physical Therapy Says — weak glutes are behind most lower body injuries

Physical Therapy Says — walking is one of the most underrated forms of rehabilitation
Physical Therapy Says — most rotator cuff tears are painless and you don't know you have one


Now here's a full script and caption for one:

"Physical Therapy Says — pain does not always mean damage."
Spoken:
"Physical therapy says — pain does not always mean damage.
Most people feel pain and immediately assume something is broken. Torn. Seriously wrong.
But pain is not a damage detector. Pain is a protection mechanism. Your brain produces pain when it perceives threat. Not necessarily when tissue is actually damaged.
Think about it. Paper cuts are tiny. They hurt like crazy. A serious internal injury can be completely painless.
This is why two people can have the exact same MRI — same disc bulge, same meniscus tear — and one is in agony while the other feels nothing.
Pain is real. Always. But it does not always equal damage. And understanding that changes everything about how you approach injury and recovery.
Stop fearing movement because it hurts. Start asking why your brain perceives that movement as a threat."
End card:
"I'm the Muscle SPT. Like and follow for more."
Caption:
Pain is not a damage detector. It is a protection mechanism.
Your brain produces pain when it perceives threat — not necessarily when something is actually torn or broken. Two people can have identical MRI findings and completely different pain experiences. That is not weakness. That is neuroscience.
Understanding this changes how you approach injury, recovery, and movement for the rest of your life. Stop fearing pain. Start understanding it.
.
.
.

Want me to write the full script and caption for that one?You said: Sure.Sure. Give me, gate speed. Yeah. Give me one on gate speed. Talk about, like, cutoffs for the 6 meter walk maybe, like, a time dip and go That kind of thing, data analysis. And, again, everything I give you, I want you to think as if you're Talking. To a toddler. I wanna explain this to my audience so they understand it. I don't need to use jargon. I want them to understand it. Like, I'd be talking to That's the same narrative and that's what's gonna drive home these videos and make the better. All that kind of stuff.Mar 28Claude responded: Hook:Hook:
"Physical Therapy Says — the way you walk predicts how long you'll live."
Spoken:
"I know that sounds dramatic. But the research backs it up.
Gait speed — how fast you walk — is one of the strongest predictors of how well you age. PTs actually measure it clinically using something called the 10 meter walk test. You walk 10 meters. We time you. That number tells us a lot.
The cutoff is 1.0 meters per second. That's roughly one step per second. If you're walking slower than that you are at a significantly higher risk of falls, hospitalization, and loss of independence as you age.
Now here's the other test. The Timed Up and Go. You sit in a chair. Stand up. Walk 10 feet. Turn around. Walk back. Sit down. We time it.
Under 12 seconds — you're doing well.
12 to 20 seconds — moderate risk.
Over 20 seconds — high risk of falls and functional decline.
And here's the part nobody talks about. The muscle you build right now is what determines those numbers at 60, 70, and 80. Stronger legs mean faster walking. Faster walking means longer independence.
It really is that simple."
End card:
"I'm the Muscle SPT. Like and follow for more."
Caption:
How fast you walk is one of the strongest predictors of how well you age.
PTs use gait speed clinically to assess fall risk and functional decline. The cutoff is 1.0 meters per second on the 10 meter walk test. The Timed Up and Go test gives us even more information — under 12 seconds is normal, over 20 seconds signals high fall risk.
The muscle you build today determines those numbers decades from now. Stronger legs. Faster walking. Longer independence.
It really is that simple.
.
.
.

#physicaltherapy #gaitspeed #longevity #sarcopenia #themusclesptYou said: Okay.Okay. So this is talking about how fast you walk. And it measures your fall risk. Right? I don't know. Yeah. Change it to HowMar 28Claude responded: Hook:Hook:
"Physical Therapy Says — how fast you walk predicts your fall risk."
Spoken:
"This one surprises people every time.
PTs actually measure how fast you walk as a clinical tool. It's called gait speed. And it tells us a lot about where your health is headed.
Here's how it works. You walk 10 meters. We time you. One simple number.
The magic cutoff is 1.0 meters per second. Think of it as roughly one comfortable step per second. Drop below that and your risk of falls, hospitalization, and losing your independence goes up significantly.
We also use something called the Timed Up and Go test. You sit in a chair. Stand up. Walk 10 feet. Turn around. Walk back. Sit down. We time the whole thing.
Under 12 seconds — you're in good shape.
12 to 20 seconds — worth paying attention to.
Over 20 seconds — high fall risk.
Now here's the part that matters for you right now. The muscle you build today is what keeps those numbers healthy at 60, 70, and 80. Weak legs mean slow walking. Slow walking means higher fall risk. Higher fall risk means loss of independence.
Train your legs. It is literally life or death."
End card:
"I'm the Muscle SPT. Like and follow for more."
Caption:
How fast you walk predicts your fall risk. And PTs measure it clinically.
The 10 meter walk test cutoff is 1.0 meters per second. Drop below that and your risk of falls and loss of independence increases significantly. The Timed Up and Go test adds even more detail — under 12 seconds is normal, over 20 seconds signals high fall risk.
The muscle you build today keeps those numbers healthy for decades. Weak legs slow you down. Slow walking puts you at risk. It really is that connected.

Under 12 seconds — you're in good shape.
12 to 20 seconds — worth paying attention to.

#physicaltherapy #fallprevention #longevity #sarcopenia #themusclesptYou said: Forward Head Posture Explained in Under 30 SecondsForward Head Posture Explained in Under 30 SecondsMar 29Claude responded: Hook:Hook:
"Forward head posture explained in under 30 seconds."
Spoken:
"Your head weighs about 10 to 12 pounds.
When it sits directly over your spine your muscles handle that load easily.
But for every inch your head shifts forward that load doubles. Two inches forward and your spine is managing the equivalent of a 40 to 50 pound head.
All day. Every day.
That's forward head posture. And it's not just about how you look. It creates excessive strain on the muscles and joints of your neck and upper back. Over time that leads to pain, stiffness, and reduced mobility.
The fix isn't just pulling your head back. It's building the strength to hold it there. Deep neck flexors, upper back muscles, thoracic mobility.
Your posture is a strength problem. Not a habit problem."
End card:
"I'm the Muscle SPT. Want to build muscle and prevent injury for life. Follow for more."
Caption:
Your head weighs 10 to 12 pounds. Every inch it shifts forward doubles the load on your spine.
Forward head posture is not just a cosmetic issue. It creates chronic strain on the muscles and joints of your neck and upper back that compounds over time. And the fix is not just reminding yourself to sit up straight. It is building the strength to hold the right position without thinking about it.
Posture is a strength problem. Train it like one.
.
.
.
Like & follow @themusclespt for more!
#posture #physicaltherapy #neckpain #strengthtraining #themusclesptYou said: Now do one for rounded shouldersNow do one for rounded shouldersMar 29Claude responded: Hook:Hook:
"Rounded shoulders explained in under 30 seconds."
Spoken:
"Rounded shoulders are not a posture problem. They are a strength problem.
Here is what is actually happening. The muscles on the front of your body — your chest and anterior shoulder — are tight and overactive. The muscles on the back — your mid traps, rhomboids, and rear delts — are weak and underactive.
That imbalance pulls your shoulders forward. And it is not because you are lazy or have bad posture. It is because most people train what they can see in the mirror and neglect everything behind them.
The fix is not just stretching your chest. That helps temporarily. The real fix is strengthening your upper back. Rows. Face pulls. Band pull aparts. Build the muscles that pull your shoulders back and they will stay there without you thinking about it.
Stop stretching. Start pulling."
End card:
"I'm the Muscle SPT. Want to build muscle and prevent injury for life. Follow for more."
Caption:
Rounded shoulders are not a posture problem. They are a strength imbalance.
Tight chest and anterior shoulder pulling forward. Weak upper back unable to pull back. That is the whole equation. Stretching your chest gives you temporary relief. Building your upper back gives you a permanent fix.
More rows. More face pulls. More pulling in general. Your shoulders will follow.
.
.
.

#posture #physicaltherapy #shoulderhealth #strengthtraining #themusclesptYou said: Use this for FHPMSK1 Bible.pdfpdfUse this for FHPMar 29Claude responded: Good — I have enough clinical information from your notes.Ran 3 commands, viewed a fileRan 3 commands, viewed a fileGood — I have enough clinical information from your notes. Here's the updated script:

Hook:
"Forward head posture explained in under 30 seconds."
Spoken:
"Your head weighs about 10 to 12 pounds.
When it sits directly over your spine your muscles handle that load easily.
But for every inch your head shifts forward that load on your spine increases significantly. Two inches forward and you're talking about the equivalent of a 40 to 50 pound head. All day. Every day.
Now here's what's actually happening underneath. The muscles at the front of your neck — your deep neck flexors — become weak and underactive. The muscles at the back — your suboccipitals and upper traps — become tight and overactive to compensate.
That pattern has a name. Upper crossed syndrome. And it drives not just neck pain but headaches, shoulder dysfunction, and reduced cervical mobility over time.
The fix is not just pulling your head back. It's restoring that muscle balance. Strengthening your deep neck flexors. Improving thoracic mobility. And addressing the tightness in the structures pulling your head forward.
Your posture is a strength problem. Not a habit problem."
End card:
"I'm the Muscle SPT. Want to build muscle and prevent injury for life. Follow for more."
Caption:
Your head weighs 10 to 12 pounds. Every inch it shifts forward multiplies that load on your spine.
Forward head posture weakens your deep neck flexors and overloads your suboccipitals and upper traps. That imbalance — known as upper crossed syndrome — drives neck pain, headaches, and shoulder dysfunction over time.
The fix is restoring muscle balance. Not just reminding yourself to sit up straight.
Posture is a strength problem. Train it like one.
.
.
.

#posture #physicaltherapy #neckpain #strengthtraining #themusclesptYou said: USE THIS FOR ROUNDED SHOULDERSMSK2 Bible Unit 1-2.pdfpdfUSE THIS FOR ROUNDED SHOULDERSMar 29Claude responded: Good — got what I need from your notes.Ran 2 commandsRan 2 commandsGood — got what I need from your notes. Here's the updated script using your clinical material:

Hook:
"Rounded shoulders explained in under 30 seconds."
Spoken:
"Rounded shoulders are not a posture problem. They are a muscle imbalance problem.
Here is what is actually happening. Your serratus anterior and pectoralis minor pull your shoulder blade forward into protraction. Your middle and lower trapezius and your rhomboids — the muscles responsible for retracting and stabilizing your scapula — are weak and underactive.
That imbalance shifts your entire shoulder forward. And it does not just affect how you look. It changes how your shoulder blade moves during arm elevation — a pattern called scapular dyskinesis — which increases stress on your rotator cuff, your AC joint, and the structures in your subacromial space.
Over time that is a recipe for impingement, rotator cuff issues, and chronic shoulder pain.
The fix is not just stretching your chest. That gives you temporary relief at best. The real fix is building the strength to hold the right position. Middle and lower trap. Rhomboids. Serratus anterior control. Rows, face pulls, band pull aparts.
Build the muscles that stabilize your scapula and your shoulders will follow."
End card:
"I'm the Muscle SPT. Want to build muscle and prevent injury for life. Follow for more."
Caption:
Rounded shoulders are not a posture habit. They are a scapular stability problem.
Weak middle and lower trapezius combined with tight pec minor and serratus pulling your shoulder blade into protraction. That imbalance alters how your scapula moves during arm elevation — increasing stress on your rotator cuff and subacromial space over time.
Stretching your chest is a temporary fix. Building your upper back is the permanent one.
More rows. More face pulls. More scapular control work. Your shoulders will follow.
.
.
.
Like & follow @themusclespt for more!
#shoulderhealth #physicaltherapy #scapular #strengthtraining #themusclesptYou said: AGAIN- SPEAK AS IF TALKING TO TODDLERAGAIN- SPEAK AS IF TALKING TO TODDLERMar 29Claude responded: Got it.Got it. Here's the rewrite:

Hook:
"Rounded shoulders explained in under 30 seconds."
Spoken:
"Rounded shoulders are not a posture problem. They are a strength problem.
Think of your shoulder blade like a boat. And the muscles around it are the ropes keeping it in place.
When the ropes on the back — your mid and lower traps and rhomboids — are weak and loose the boat drifts forward. That is your shoulder rounding.
And when that happens the whole shoulder joint shifts out of its ideal position. Which over time puts extra stress on your rotator cuff and the structures around your shoulder. That is where the pain comes from.
The fix is not just reminding yourself to sit up straight. It is tightening those back ropes. Building the muscles that pull your shoulder blade back and hold it there.
More rows. More face pulls. More upper back work.
Stop stretching your chest. Start strengthening your back. That is the whole fix."
End card:
"I'm the Muscle SPT. Want to build muscle and prevent injury for life. Follow for more."
Caption:
Rounded shoulders are not a posture habit. They are a strength imbalance.
The muscles on the back of your shoulder blade — your mid and lower traps and rhomboids — are weak. The muscles on the front are tight and pulling forward. That imbalance drifts your whole shoulder out of position and puts extra stress on your rotator cuff over time.
Stretching your chest is a temporary fix. Building your upper back is the permanent one.
More rows. More face pulls. More pulling in general.
.
.
.

#shoulderhealth #physicaltherapy #scapular #strengthtraining #themusclesptYou said: How do you feel about combining the muscle SPT with branding on everything good posture?How do you feel about combining the muscle SPT with branding on everything good posture? Like postural optimization? As a brand image can talk about so many things starting from ground up? Feet, knees, hips, torso, shoulders neck, etc. everyone wants good posture. I have great posture due to msucleMar 29Claude responded: It's a strong angle and here's why — posture is one of the most universally searched topics in fitness and health.It's a strong angle and here's why — posture is one of the most universally searched topics in fitness and health. Everyone thinks they have bad posture. Everyone wants to fix it. The audience is enormous and spans every demographic from 20 year olds at desks to 60 year olds with chronic pain.
But here's my honest take on it as a brand pillar versus a brand identity:
As a content pillar — yes absolutely.
Posture content fits perfectly under The Muscle SPT brand. Forward head posture, rounded shoulders, anterior pelvic tilt, knee valgus, flat feet — you can build an entire series from the ground up exactly like you described. That series alone could drive massive reach because it's visual, educational, and directly relevant to almost everyone watching.
As the core brand identity — be careful.
The Muscle SPT is already anchored to something more powerful and more unique — muscle building and injury prevention tied to sarcopenia and longevity. That mission is specific, clinical, and ownable. Posture is a broader topic that many people already own in the space. Pivoting the brand identity toward posture risks making you one of many rather than the only one doing what you do.
The sweet spot:
Use posture as a major content series within The Muscle SPT brand. Frame it through your lens — posture as a strength and muscle problem, not a habit problem. That framing is yours. That is the angle nobody else is hitting.
"Your posture is a muscle problem. And I can fix it."
That one line bridges your sarcopenia mission, your PT knowledge, your bodybuilding background, and the posture content all at once. It ties everything together without abandoning what makes you different.
Posture is a pillar. Not the foundation. Your foundation is already built.You said: Give me major pillars to work with and then subhooks like “explained in under 30 seconds” etcGive me major pillars to work with and then subhooks like “explained in under 30 seconds” etcMar 29Claude responded: Here are your major pillars and sub-hooks:Here are your major pillars and sub-hooks:

PILLAR 1 — MUSCLE & LONGEVITY
Your core mission. Sarcopenia, muscle loss, aging, why muscle is medicine.
Sub-hooks:

"Explained in under 30 seconds"
"Physical Therapy Says"
"Fun Fact"
"The truth about [topic]"
"Nobody talks about this"


PILLAR 2 — POSTURE & MOVEMENT

"Why your [body part] hurts"
"Explained in under 30 seconds"
"The muscle problem nobody is fixing"

PILLAR 3 — INJURY PREVENTION & REHAB

"Fitness Influencer vs PT Student"
"Gimmick or Gains"

"What your PT actually wants you to know"


PILLAR 4 — TRAINING SCIENCE
Biomechanics, motor units, mechanical tension, progressive overload, exercise selection.
Sub-hooks:

"Explained in under 30 seconds"
"Muscle Science Says"
"The real reason [topic]"
"Fun Fact"
"This is why [topic] works"


PILLAR 5 — COMPOUND LIFT SERIES
Squat. Hinge. Push. Pull. Every major movement pattern broken down with PT cues.
Sub-hooks:

"How to [exercise] to build muscle and prevent injury"
"The cue that changes everything"
"Most people do this wrong"
"PT approved [exercise]"


PILLAR 6 — POSTURE FROM THE GROUND UP
Your posture series. One body part per video. Feet to head.
Sub-hooks:

"Start here before everything else"
"The [body part] series"
"Why your [body part] is affecting everything above it"
"Fix your [body part] in under 60 seconds"
"The foundation nobody builds"


PILLAR 7 — PERSONAL & STORY
Your documentary series. D1, injuries, PT school, bodybuilding, the clinic vision.
Sub-hooks:

"My Story — Episode [X]"
"Nobody told me this"
"The moment everything changed"
"What [experience] taught me"


PILLAR 8 — REACTION & HOT TAKES
Fitness Influencer vs PT Student. Gimmick or Gains. Myth busting.
Sub-hooks:

"Fitness Influencer vs PT Student"
"Gimmick or Gains"
"Physical Therapy Says"
"Valid or Cap"
"PT Approved or PT Banned"


PILLAR 9 — BTS & PERSONALITY
Pop Tart series. Training footage. Real life. The human behind the brand.
Sub-hooks:

"Proof you can eat whatever you want and stay on your diet"
"This was behind the scenes with the Muscle SPT"
"Real Saturday. No performance."
"This is what PT school actually looks like"


The system at a glance:
Every post you make fits one pillar. Every pillar has multiple sub-hooks to rotate through so your content never feels repetitive. The sub-hooks are your format signal — your audience hears "explained in under 30 seconds" and immediately knows what kind of content they're about to watch.
That recognition is what builds a loyal audience. Same pillars. Different topics. Consistent format. Make each pillar seperate but creates foundation for the msucle ptMar 29Claude responded: Here are your 9 pillars — each one standalone but together they build the complete foundation of The Muscle PT brand:Here are your 9 pillars — each one standalone but together they build the complete foundation of The Muscle PT brand:

PILLAR 1 — MUSCLE & LONGEVITY
The mission statement in content form. Sarcopenia, muscle loss, aging, why building muscle early is the most important health decision you can make.
Sub-hooks:

"Explained in under 30 seconds"
"Physical Therapy Says"
"Nobody talks about this"
"The truth about [topic]"
"Fun Fact"

Feeds into: The clinic. The coaching business. The email list. Every person who watches this content is a potential future patient.

PILLAR 2 — POSTURE & MOVEMENT
Ground up posture series. Feet, ankles, knees, hips, spine, shoulders, neck. Every body part connected to the one above and below it.
Sub-hooks:

"Your [body part] is lying to you"
"Why your [body part] hurts"
"The muscle problem nobody is fixing"
"Fix this before it breaks"
"Explained in under 30 seconds"

Feeds into: The clinic. Positions you as the go-to PT for postural dysfunction and chronic pain prevention.

PILLAR 3 — INJURY PREVENTION & REHAB
PT school knowledge translated for the gym. Common injuries, why they happen, how to prevent them before they start.
Sub-hooks:

"Physical Therapy Says"
"Fitness Influencer vs PT Student"
"Stop doing this"
"What your PT actually wants you to know"
"Fix it before it breaks"

Feeds into: Your core brand promise. Build muscle AND prevent injury. Every video in this pillar is the second half of your mission statement made real.

PILLAR 4 — TRAINING SCIENCE
The science behind why training works. Motor units, mechanical tension, progressive overload, muscle fiber recruitment, rest periods, periodization. Made simple.
Sub-hooks:

"Explained in under 30 seconds"
"Muscle Science Says"
"The real reason [topic]"
"Fun Fact"
"This is why [topic] works"

Feeds into: Authority. Every time you explain a training concept scientifically you separate yourself from every other fitness creator who just says what to do without explaining why.

PILLAR 5 — BIOMECHANICS BREAKDOWN
How movement actually works. Force vectors, moment arms, joint mechanics, lever systems, muscle activation patterns. Applied to real exercises and real human movement.
Sub-hooks:

"How to [exercise] to build muscle and prevent injury"
"The biomechanics of [movement]"
"Why this exercise works the way it does"
"Most people do this wrong — here's the science"
"The cue that changes everything"

Feeds into: Your most unique content. Nobody else in the fitness space is breaking down biomechanics with a bodybuilder's eye and a PT student's clinical knowledge. This is your highest authority pillar.

PILLAR 6 — POSTURE FROM THE GROUND UP
A dedicated series building the postural foundation from feet to head. One body part per video. Sequential and educational.
Sub-hooks:

"Start here before everything else"
"The [body part] series — Episode [X]"
"Why your [body part] is affecting everything above it"
"Fix your [body part] in under 60 seconds"
"The foundation nobody builds"

Feeds into: Long term audience retention. People follow a series. They come back every episode. They share it with people who have the same issue. This pillar alone builds a loyal following.

PILLAR 7 — PERSONAL & STORY
Your documentary series. D1 tennis, injuries, bioengineering, EMT, PT school, bodybuilding, the clinic vision. The human being behind the brand told in episodes.
Sub-hooks:

"My Story — Episode [X]"
"Nobody told me this"
"The moment everything changed"
"What [experience] taught me"
"I was [age] when I decided"

Feeds into: Trust. People don't buy from brands. They buy from people they believe in. Every personal episode converts a follower into a fan and a fan into a future client.

PILLAR 8 — REACTION & HOT TAKES
Fitness Influencer vs PT Student. Gimmick or Gains. Clinical verdicts on viral fitness claims. The most shareable and reach-driving content you make.
Sub-hooks:

"Fitness Influencer vs PT Student"
"Gimmick or Gains"
"Physical Therapy Says"
"Valid or Cap"
"PT Approved or PT Banned"

Feeds into: Top of funnel reach. This pillar finds new audiences who have never heard of you. Every reaction video that performs well brings in cold followers who then discover everything else you've built.

PILLAR 9 — BTS & PERSONALITY
Pop Tart series. Training footage. Real life. The human side of building a PT brand while in school, training recreationally, and preparing to compete.
Sub-hooks:

"Proof you can eat whatever you want and stay on your diet"
"This was behind the scenes with the Muscle SPT"
"Real Saturday. No performance."
"This is what PT school actually looks like"

Feeds into: Loyalty. The people who follow your BTS content are your most loyal audience. They stay through every transition — PT school, graduation, clinic launch, competition prep. They become your word of mouth machine.

How they connect:
Pillar 1 is the WHY — muscle loss is real, here is what is at stake.
Pillars 2 and 6 are the PROBLEM — poor movement and posture accelerate that decline.
Pillars 3 and 4 are the SCIENCE — here is what the research actually says.
Pillar 5 is the APPLICATION — here is how it works in real movement.
Pillar 8 is the REACH — here is why the fitness industry gets it wrong.
Pillar 7 is the PERSON — here is who is telling you all of this and why they care.
Pillar 9 is the HUMAN — here is the real life behind the brand.
Together they tell one complete story. I want all of these to be repeatable forever and able to expand upon. Also id like different video setups as wellMar 29Claude responded: Honest answer — Pillar 2 as "Movement is Medicine" is a concept not a content machine.Honest answer — Pillar 2 as "Movement is Medicine" is a concept not a content machine. It's a great tagline but it doesn't give you an infinite repeatable framework the way your other pillars do.
Here's the problem. "Movement heals" is a message not a series. You'd run out of angles within a few months. It also overlaps too much with Pillars 3 and 6 which already cover movement dysfunction and postural correction.
Replace it with something that is infinitely expandable:

PILLAR 2 — THE LONGEVITY ATHLETE
How to train, eat, recover, and live like someone who is building a body designed to last. Nutrition, sleep, stress, hormones, recovery modalities, lifestyle habits — all through the lens of long term health and muscle preservation.
Sub-hooks:

"The longevity athlete does this differently"
"What training for 60 year old you actually looks like"
"The recovery habit nobody talks about"
"This is what aging well actually requires"
"The lifestyle factor destroying your gains"

Why this works as a pillar:
Infinite topics. Nutrition, sleep quality, stress and cortisol, cold exposure, zone 2 cardio, alcohol and muscle loss, hormones and aging, hydration, sunlight, walking. Every single lifestyle factor that affects muscle and longevity lives here. You never run out of content.
Video setup: Podcast format. Casual button down. Slightly off camera. Conversational and personal. Completely different visual from your other pillars.

Updated video setup breakdown across all pillars:
Pillar 1 — Muscle and Longevity: Talking head. Mic. White coat or athletic wear. Yellow-green light. Direct to camera. Authoritative.
Pillar 2 — The Longevity Athlete: Podcast setup. Casual button down. Slightly off camera. Warm amber light. Conversational.
Pillar 3 — Injury Prevention and Rehab: Talking head. White coat. Yellow-green light. Clinical and direct.
Pillar 4 — Training Science: Talking head. Athletic wear. Yellow-green light. Fast paced. Energetic.
Pillar 5 — Biomechanics Breakdown: Screen record on Procreate drawing force vectors over images. Or gym footage with voiceover. No talking head needed.
Pillar 6 — Posture from the Ground Up: Talking head or demonstration. White coat or athletic wear. Clean background. Could use split screen showing correct vs incorrect.
Pillar 7 — Exercise Modality Breakdown: Reaction format. Picture in picture. You watching the clip in a bubble. Casual athletic wear.
Pillar 8 — Reaction and Hot Takes: Reaction format. White coat. Yellow-green light. Convicted and direct.
Pillar 9 — BTS and Personality: Raw. Phone propped up. Whatever you're wearing. No setup needed. Authenticity is the production value.
Personal and Story Series: Podcast setup. Casual button down. Slightly off camera. Most vulnerable and unscripted of everything.

Every pillar now has its own visual identity so your audience knows what they're watching before you say a word. And every pillar can run forever without ever running out of material.You said: Change longevity in pillar 2 maybe?Change longevity in pillar 2 maybe?Mar 29Claude responded: Here are some alternative names for Pillar 2:Here are some alternative names for Pillar 2:

Option 1 — "The Performance Lifestyle"
How you live outside the gym determines how you perform inside it. Sleep, nutrition, stress, recovery, hormones, habits. Everything that affects your body when you're not training.
Option 2 — "Built to Last"
The habits, behaviors, and lifestyle choices that build a body designed for decades. Not just training — everything around it.
Option 3 — "Outside the Gym"
What you do between sessions matters as much as the sessions themselves. Recovery, sleep, nutrition, stress management, daily movement.
Option 4 — "The Full Picture"
Training is only one piece. This pillar covers everything else — the lifestyle factors most people completely ignore that determine how well they build muscle and how long they stay healthy.
Option 5 — "Recover Like a PT"
Sleep, stress, nutrition timing, recovery modalities, hormones. How a PT student who trains recreationally actually approaches life outside the gym.

My honest pick:
"Built to Last"
It ties directly into your brand mission without using the word longevity. It implies permanence, durability, and long term thinking. It works as a series name that never expires. And it sounds like something people want to be — not just healthy but built to withstand time.

PILLAR 1 — MUSCLE & LONGEVITY
The mission. Sarcopenia, muscle loss, aging, why muscle is the most important thing you can build starting at 30.
Sub-hooks:

"Explained in under 30 seconds"
"Nobody talks about this"
"The truth about [topic]"
"This is what happens to your body when [topic]"
