Slide 2
The problem in one sentence. White text. Plain language.
Slide 3
The surprising truth or the analogy. One to two sentences maximum.
Slide 4
The actionable takeaway. What do they do with this right now.
Slide 5
Follow @themusclespt. Build Muscle. Prevent Injury. For Life.
Carousel Caption
Same format as reel caption. Body text three dots like and follow hashtags.
STATIC POST
One powerful statement. Maximum 15 words. White text on black background. Stands alone. Makes people stop and think.
SOCIAL PROOF AND CURIOSITY PHRASES
Five short phrases. Maximum 10 words each. Perfect for on screen text overlays. Make people feel like they are missing something important.
LITTLE KNOWN TIPS
Five practical tips. Maximum 12 words each. Clear and easy to apply immediately. So useful it is impossible not to save.
Tip format:
Tip in 12 words or less
Why it works — one sentence
Which pillar
CTAS
Ten short creative direct CTAs. No generic phrases. Each one triggers curiosity or immediate action. Mix of save this, comment triggers, tag someone, and engagement hooks.
PAIN POINTS AND HOOKS
Ten pain points your target audience feels about this topic. Turn each one into a hook of maximum 10 words. Ready to use in future reels on the same concept.
Pain point format:
Pain point — what they feel or fear
Hook — maximum 10 words direct and provocative
MASTER RULES
Speak as if talking to a toddler — no jargon no fluff
Use analogies whenever explaining a complex concept — make it visual
Every script must be filmable in under 90 seconds ideally under 60
Every caption follows the locked in format — body text three dots like and follow hashtags
Five hashtags maximum always ending with themusclespt
Every video gets tagged before scripting — pillar sub-category format hook format write off setup lighting tone length
Exercise demos always include — primary mover secondary movers resistance profile stability demand stimulus to fatigue ratio
Hooks are always repeatable and testable — rotate 8 hook formats and track which performs
Write offs always rotate — use all 8 and track which drives the most follows and saves
Every script is written so visuals and b-roll can be added in edit — add bracket notes for editor
Caption CTA rotates between standard like and follow and soft sell coaching invitation — maximum two soft sells per week
YouTube title format for exercise demos — Exercise Name: The PT Cue That Changes Everything
HOW TO USE THIS EVERY SESSION
Step 1 — pick a topic from the content library
Step 2 — tag it with pillar sub-category format hook format write off setup lighting tone
Step 3 — drop the topic into this framework
Step 4 — get the full content package — hooks, reel script, caption, 7 second reel, carousel, static post, social proof phrases, tips, CTAs, pain points
Step 5 — film edit post between 6 and 8pm
Step 6 — share to Stories immediately after posting
Step 7 — reply to every comment within 60 minutes
Step 8 — track which hook format and write off drove the most engagement
Step 9 — rotate the same concept across different formats throughout the week
Step 10 — double down on what works## Repeatable Hook FormatsTest these across every video and track which ones perform best:
Hook Format 1 — The Question:
“[Topic]. Do you actually know what this means?”
Hook Format 2 — The Myth Buster:
“Everyone thinks [common belief]. They’re wrong.”
Hook Format 3 — The Time Hook:
“[Topic] explained in under 30 seconds.”
Hook Format 4 — The Surprise:
“[Surprising fact]. Here’s why that matters for you.”
Hook Format 5 — The Direct Challenge:
“If you don’t know this about [topic] you need to watch this.”
Hook Format 6 — The Simple Promise:
“[Topic]. Here’s everything you need to know.”
Hook Format 7 — The Relatability Hook:
“Nobody explained [topic] to me like this. So I’m doing it for you.”
Hook Format 8 — The Stakes Hook:
“[Topic] is affecting your body right now. Here’s what’s actually happening.”## Repeatable Write Off OptionsPick two or three and rotate them. Test which ones drive the most follows and saves:
Write Off 1 — The Rating:
“I’m giving [topic] a [X] out of 10 on the must know scale. Drop yours in the comments.”
Write Off 2 — The Test:
“Now you know. Go test this today and tell me what you find.”
Write Off 3 — The Challenge:
“If this changed how you think about [topic] — share it with one person who needs to hear it.”
Write Off 4 — The Cliffhanger:
“That’s [topic] simplified. Next video I’m breaking down [related topic] and it gets even more interesting. Follow so you don’t miss it.”
Write Off 5 — The Call Out:
“Most people watching this will keep doing what they’ve always done. The ones who save this and apply it are the ones who actually change. Be that person.”
Write Off 6 — The Credibility Close:
“I’m the Muscle SPT — a PT student, former D1 athlete, and bodybuilder. This is what I study so you don’t have to. Follow for more.”
Write Off 7 — The Save Hook:
“Save this video. You will want to come back to it.”
Write Off 8 — The Simple Close:
“I’m the Muscle SPT. Want to build muscle and prevent injury for life. Follow for more.”## Master Script FrameworkEvery video going forward follows this exact structure:
[HOOK] — One punchy line. Visual on screen. Stops the scroll.
[THE SIMPLE SETUP] — One or two sentences. What are we talking about and why does it matter to the viewer right now. No jargon. Toddler level.
[THE ANALOGY] — One comparison to something everyone understands. This is where complex concepts become memorable. Make it visual so the editor can add b-roll or animation here.
[THE BREAKDOWN] — Two to four key points. Short sentences. One idea per sentence. Pause between each one for emphasis. Think out loud like you’re explaining to a friend not presenting to a class.
[THE SO WHAT] — One or two sentences. Why does this actually matter for the viewer’s life right now. Make it personal and actionable.
[THE WRITE OFF] — Rotate between your write off options. End every video with one of these before your end card.
[END CARD] — “I’m the Muscle SPT. Want to build muscle and prevent injury for life. Follow for more.”## Example Script FormatHook:
“How much volume do you need to build muscle? It depends. Here’s how to figure it out.”
Simple Setup:
“Volume is just the total amount of work you do for a muscle in a week. And the question of how much you need does not have one answer. It has several.”
Analogy:
“Think of your muscle like a sponge. A dry sponge absorbs water instantly. A saturated sponge cannot take any more no matter how much you pour on it. Your muscle works the same way. The right amount of volume soaks in and produces adaptation. Too much and it just spills over without doing anything useful.”
Breakdown:
“If you are newer to training your sponge is dry. You do not need much volume to see results. Ten sets per muscle group per week is enough to drive real growth.
As you get more advanced the sponge gets harder to saturate. You need more work to keep progressing. Somewhere between 10 and 20 working sets per muscle group per week covers most people in most situations.
But here is the key. Volume you cannot recover from does nothing for you. Sleep, stress, nutrition — these determine how much your body can actually absorb and adapt to.
And quality beats quantity every time. Ten hard sets taken close to failure will always beat twenty easy sets with too much left in the tank.”
The So What:
“Start on the lower end. Add volume gradually as long as you are recovering well and getting stronger. That is the whole system.”
Write Off:
“Save this video. Most people are either doing too much or too little and never figure out why they are stuck. Now you know the answer. I’m the Muscle SPT. Want to build muscle and prevent injury for life. Follow for more.“## **LITTLE KNOWN TIPS — HOW TO USE THEM IN CONTENT**Three ways to turn every tip into its own piece of content:
FORMAT 1 — STANDALONE TIP REEL
Each tip becomes its own 15 to 30 second reel. Hook is the tip itself stated as a surprising fact. Body is the one sentence explanation of why it works. Write off is a save CTA. This is your fastest content to film — one setup, one tip, done in two minutes. Post these on days when you do not have a full educational reel ready. They fill the calendar and drive saves.
Example from BFR tips:
Hook — “BFR sets are not 8 to 12 reps. They are 30 then three sets of 15. Here is why.”
Body — “Low load requires higher reps to accumulate enough time under tension to produce the same growth signal as heavy training. The protocol is different because the mechanism is different.”
Write off — “Save this before your next BFR session.”
FORMAT 2 — CAROUSEL SLIDE
Every tip list becomes a carousel automatically. Title slide is the hook. Each subsequent slide is one tip. Final slide is your brand close. This is your Thursday carousel slot. One topic. Five tips. Five slides. Done.
FORMAT 3 — CAPTION ADD ON
At the bottom of any reel caption add one tip as a bonus. Label it clearly.
Example:
“Bonus tip — BFR cuffs go on the most proximal part of the limb. Upper arm for upper body. Upper thigh for lower body. Placement matters.”
This rewards people who read the full caption and drives saves because they do not want to lose the bonus information.
FORMAT 4 — STORIES SERIES
Post one tip per day across five consecutive Stories. Day 1 through 5 each get one tip. Simple text slide. Yellow on black. Brand colors. No filming required. Drives profile visits as people come back each day for the next one.
FORMAT 5 — COMMUNITY CONTENT
Post the tip list as a question. Ask your audience which one surprised them most. Comment bait that also educates. Works as a standalone post or as a Stories poll.
THE RULE FOR TIPS
Every tip should be so specific and immediately actionable that someone can use it at their next session. If it is too vague it will not get saved. The more specific the tip the higher the save rate.## **7 SECOND REELS — BACKGROUND VIDEO GUIDE**Yes the background should match the modality or topic. Here is the rule for every category:
MUSCLE AND LONGEVITY TOPICS
Background — anatomy animation of muscle fiber, stock footage of someone training in a gym, slow motion weight being loaded onto a bar, aging adult performing resistance training. The visual should make the viewer feel the stakes of the topic immediately.
INJURY PREVENTION AND REHAB TOPICS
Background — stock footage of a knee or shoulder being examined, someone performing rehab exercises, a PT working with a patient, joint anatomy animation. Clinical and visual at the same time.
POSTURE TOPICS
Background — side profile of someone with forward head posture, spine anatomy animation, before and after posture footage, someone sitting at a desk with poor posture. The visual contrast between bad and good posture is your most powerful tool here.
TRAINING SCIENCE TOPICS
Background — slow motion footage of someone performing a compound lift, muscle fiber animation, neural activation graphic, barbell loading. The visual should feel scientific and powerful simultaneously.
BIOMECHANICS TOPICS
Background — your Procreate Pocket screen record drawing force vectors, slow motion footage of a squat or deadlift from the side angle, split screen correct vs incorrect. This is where your annotation content lives.
BFR SPECIFICALLY
Background — footage of BFR cuffs being applied to an arm or thigh, someone performing low load leg press or curls with a cuff visible, clinical rehab setting footage. The visual of the cuff is essential — most people have never seen BFR in action and seeing the cuff immediately creates curiosity and stops the scroll.
EXERCISE MODALITY TOPICS
Background — footage of the actual modality being performed. Pilates video for Pilates content. Spin bike footage for spin content. CrossFit footage for CrossFit content. Always show the modality so the viewer immediately knows what you are reacting to or breaking down.
BEYOND THE GYM TOPICS
Background — someone sleeping, meal prep footage, someone stressed at a desk, supplement bottles on a counter, morning routine footage. Lifestyle visuals that feel relatable and real.
HOT TAKES AND REACTIONS
Background — the clip you are reacting to plays in the background or as a split screen. Your face as a small bubble in the corner reacting to it. The original content is the background. You are the overlay.
BTS AND PERSONALITY
Background — your actual life. Gym footage, kitchen footage, PT school footage, training footage. No stock needed. Real is the format.
THE SIMPLE RULE FOR 7 SECOND REELS
The background video should answer the question — what am I looking at — in the first half second. If the viewer needs context to understand the visual it is the wrong background. The text on screen provides the information. The background provides the emotion and the context. They work together. Never use a generic background that could belong to any fitness account. Every background should be specific enough that it could only belong to your content.
FOR BFR SPECIFICALLY YOUR 7 SECOND REEL BACKGROUND
Use stock footage of a BFR cuff being applied to a thigh or arm. Search Pixabay or Pexels for blood flow restriction or occlusion training. If you cannot find clean stock footage use a close up of a resistance band wrapped tightly around an arm as a visual approximation while the text does the explaining. The visual of restriction is what creates the curiosity that makes people stop scrolling.## **VISUAL SYSTEM FOR WHITE INFOGRAPHIC CAROUSELS**Background — pure white or very light gray F8F8F8
Primary text — near black 111111
Accent color — your brand yellow E8FF47 used sparingly for highlights callouts and data points
Secondary accent — dark charcoal 2A2A2A for headers and subheadings
Data visualization color — E8FF47 for bars and highlights 111111 for labels
Dividing lines — thin light gray DDDDDD
WHAT GOES ON EACH SLIDE
Title slide — clean white background. Bold black header. One yellow accent element. Your handle in black bottom center. Could include a simple clinical icon or anatomy line drawing.
Data slides — clean chart or graph showing the research data. Bar chart. Progress bar. Simple percentage visualization. Label everything clearly. Source cited small at the bottom.
Explanation slides — clean white. Black header. Body text in dark gray. One yellow highlight on the key word or number. No clutter.
Pull quote slide — white background. Large bold black quote. Yellow quotation marks or accent line. Clean and editorial.
Close slide — white background. Your logo or handle in black. Yellow tagline accent. Clean.
TYPES OF INFOGRAPHICS THAT WORK FOR YOUR CONTENT
Bar charts — muscle loss percentage per decade. VO2 max decline by age. BFR strength increase vs low intensity training.
Progress bars — gait speed cutoffs shown as a horizontal bar from red to green. Fall risk increasing as speed decreases.
Body diagrams — simple anatomy line drawing with labeled arrows showing muscle groups, posture compensations, or joint positions.
Timeline graphics — muscle loss from age 20 to 80 shown as a declining line. Peak bone mass timeline. Hormonal decline by decade.
Comparison tables — BFR vs heavy load vs low intensity. Flexibility vs mobility vs stability. Modifiable vs non-modifiable osteoporosis risk factors.
Stat callout boxes — large bold number with context. 40 percent strength increase. 3 to 8 percent muscle loss per decade. 1 in 3 adults over 50 affected by sarcopenia.
SPECIFIC CANVA ELEMENTS TO USE
Charts — Canva has a built in chart tool. Go to elements, search chart, select bar or progress bar, input your data directly. It generates the visual automatically.
Icons — search anatomy, muscle, bone, heart, brain in Canva elements. Use line style icons only. No filled colorful icons. Line drawings keep it clinical and clean.
Tables — Canva table tool. White background. Dark gray header row. Alternating white and light gray rows. Yellow accent on key data points.
Divider lines — thin horizontal lines in light gray between sections. Creates structure without visual noise.
THE HYBRID APPROACH
Here is what I actually recommend for your brand. Use both systems and let the content type determine the format.
White infographic carousels — for research data, clinical statistics, comparison content, and anything with numbers. These are your most saveable and most shareable posts because they look authoritative and professional.
Dark black carousels with bold numbers and pull quotes — for motivational content, hot takes, personal story, and mission driven content. These are your most emotionally impactful posts.
The contrast between the two formats also helps your audience quickly identify what type of content they are about to consume. White means data and research. Dark means conviction and mission. Both are on brand. Both serve different purposes.
EXAMPLES OF WHITE INFOGRAPHIC CAROUSELS FOR YOUR CONTENT
Muscle loss percentage per decade — bar chart declining from age 30 to 80
Gait speed and mortality risk — horizontal progress bar with color coded zones
BFR vs conventional training — side by side comparison table
The four nutrients for sarcopenia — icon for each with one line description
Sarcopenia prevalence by age — bar chart showing 1 in 3 over 50
The six vital signs — clean numbered list with icons
Five times sit to stand scoring — color coded results table green yellow red
Osteoporosis risk factors — two column table modifiable vs non-modifiable
VO2 max decline by decade — declining line graph with age on x axis
The minimum effective dose — simple visual showing 2 sessions per week protocol
TOOLS BEYOND CANVA
If you want more sophisticated infographics than Canva allows:
Piktochart — specifically designed for infographics. More templates and chart types than Canva. Free tier available.
Venngage — research and data focused infographic tool. Medical and scientific templates built in. Free tier available.
Adobe Express — cleaner output than Canva for data visualization. Free with an Adobe account.
Datawrapper — specifically for charts and graphs. Input your data and it generates a publication quality chart. Free. Used by major news organizations for data journalism.
The honest answer is Canva handles 90 percent of what you need right now. When your audience grows and your content gets more sophisticated you can layer in the other tools. Start with Canva white infographic templates and build from there. 8 REPEATABLE HOOK FORMATS
Hook 1 — The Question — [Topic]. Do you actually know what this means?

Hook 3 — The Time Hook — [Topic] explained in under 30 seconds.
Hook 4 — The Surprise — [Surprising fact]. Here is why that matters for you.

8 REPEATABLE WRITE OFFS
Write Off 1 — The Rating — I am giving [topic] a [X] out of 10 on the must know scale. Drop yours in the comments.
Write Off 2 — The Test — Now you know. Go test this today and tell me what you find.
Write Off 3 — The Challenge — If this changed how you think about [topic] — share it with one person who needs to hear it.
Write Off 4 — The Cliffhanger — That is [topic] simplified. Next video I am breaking down [related topic] and it gets even more interesting. Follow so you do not miss it.
Write Off 5 — The Call Out — Most people watching this will keep doing what they have always done. The ones who save this and apply it are the ones who actually change. Be that person.
Write Off 6 — The Credibility Close — I am the Muscle SPT — a PT student, former D1 athlete, and bodybuilder. This is what I study so you do not have to. Follow for more.
Write Off 7 — The Save Hook — Save this video. You will want to come back to it.
Write Off 8 — The Simple Close — I am the Muscle SPT. Want to build muscle and prevent injury for life. Follow for more.
WRITE OFF ROTATION TRACKER

MASTER SCRIPT STRUCTURE — EVERY VIDEO
Hook — One punchy line. Maximum 10 words. Visual on screen. Stops the scroll.
Simple Setup — One to two sentences. What are we talking about and why does it matter right now. No jargon. Toddler level.
Analogy — One comparison to something everyone understands. Visual and memorable. Make it something the editor can put b-roll or animation behind.
Breakdown — Two to four key points. Short sentences. One idea per sentence. Pause between each one for emphasis.
So What — One to two sentences. Why does this matter for the viewer right now. Personal and actionable.
Write Off — Rotate between all 8. Never repeat the same one twice in a row. Track it.
End Card — I am the Muscle SPT. Want to build muscle and prevent injury for life. Follow for more.

BACKGROUND VIDEO GUIDE FOR EVERY TOPIC CATEGORY
Muscle and Longevity — anatomy animation of muscle fiber, slow motion weight being loaded, aging adult performing resistance training, declining muscle mass visualization
Injury Prevention and Rehab — knee or shoulder being examined, someone performing rehab exercises, PT working with a patient, joint anatomy animation
Posture — side profile forward head posture, spine anatomy animation, before and after posture contrast, someone at a desk
Training Science — slow motion compound lift, muscle fiber animation, neural activation graphic, barbell loading
Biomechanics — Procreate Pocket screen record drawing force vectors, slow motion squat or deadlift from side angle, split screen correct vs incorrect
BFR — BFR cuff being applied to arm or thigh, low load leg press with cuff visible, clinical rehab setting
Exercise Modalities — footage of the actual modality. Pilates for Pilates. Spin bike for spin. CrossFit for CrossFit. Always show what you are reacting to.
Beyond the Gym — someone sleeping, meal prep, someone stressed at a desk, supplement bottles, morning routine
Hot Takes and Reactions — the clip you are reacting to as background or split screen. Your face as small bubble in corner.
BTS and Personality — your actual life. Gym kitchen PT school training footage. No stock needed. Real is the format.
The simple rule — the background answers what am I looking at in the first half second. Text provides information. Background provides emotion and context. Never generic. Always specific to your brand.
LITTLE KNOWN TIPS — HOW TO USE EVERY TIP LIST
Format 1 — Standalone tip reel. Hook is the tip stated as a surprising fact. Body is the one sentence why it works. Write off is Save CTA. Fastest content to film. Fills calendar gaps.
Format 2 — Carousel slides. Title slide is the hook. Each slide is one tip. Final slide is brand close. Thursday carousel slot automatically done.
Format 3 — Caption add on. One tip added at the bottom of any reel caption labeled Bonus tip. Rewards people who read the full caption. Drives saves.
Format 4 — Stories series. One tip per day across five consecutive Stories. Simple text slide yellow on black. No filming. Drives daily profile visits.
Format 5 — Community content. Post the tip list as a question. Ask which one surprised them most. Comment bait that educates. Works as standalone post or Stories poll.
The rule for every tip — specific enough that someone can use it at their next session. The more specific the tip the higher the save rate.
WEEKLY CONTENT SCHEDULE
Monday — 60 to 90 second deep dive reel — Write off tracked
Tuesday — 10 to 15 second pure visual reel — same topic as Monday compressed — Write off tracked
Wednesday — White infographic carousel — complementary data on same topic — Write off tracked
Thursday — 30 second Explained In Under 30 Seconds — Write off 4 default — Write off tracked
Friday — Hot take or reaction reel — Fitness Influencer vs PT Student or Gimmick or Gains — Write off tracked
Saturday — BTS or personality — Pop Tart series gym footage real life — Write off tracked
Sunday — Soft sell Stories only — no feed post — one coaching invitation or lead magnet push
CONTENT MAXIMIZERS — DO THESE EVERY WEEK
Pin your best performing post from each topic to the top of your profile. New visitors always see your strongest content first.
Share every feed post to Stories immediately after posting with a poll sticker related to the topic. Drives engagement within the first 60 minutes.
Take the pull quote slide from every carousel and post it as a standalone static post two weeks later. Same content. New audience. Doubles the lifespan of every carousel.
Use the 10 to 15 second reel as a Stories teaser the day before the full 60 to 90 second version drops. Builds anticipation. Drives people to your feed.
Post one little known tip per day as a simple text Stories slide across five consecutive days from each topic. Drives daily profile visits.
Reply to every comment on the first post of each topic with a teaser for the next format on the same topic. Drives profile visits and increases session time on your page.
At the end of every carousel add a final slide — want the full breakdown watch my reel on this link in bio. Connects carousel audience to your video content.
FULL CONTENT PACKAGE — DELIVERED FOR EVERY TOPIC
8 hooks — different angles on the same concept for testing
60 to 90 second reel script with full text on screen notes for editor
30 second Explained In Under 30 Seconds script
10 to 15 second pure visual reel script with background video direction
White infographic carousel — all 7 slides with full copy and visual direction
Dark conviction carousel — all 6 slides with full copy
Static post — one powerful statement maximum 15 words
5 social proof and curiosity phrases for on screen overlays
5 little known tips with explanations and pillar tags
10 creative CTAs — mix of save comment tag and engagement triggers
10 pain points turned into 10 hooks for future posts on the same concept
Caption for every format — unique to each post not recycled
Write off assigned and tracked for every piece of content

The Muscle SPT — Dr. Zach Ermert

Topic —

Hook format used —
Write off used —
WHEN TO USE
Monday educational slot. Highest reach post of the week. Pillar 1, 2, 3, or 4. Your deepest and most thorough format.
SCRIPT STRUCTURE
Hook
One line. Maximum 10 words. Stops the scroll.
[TEXT ON SCREEN]
Simple Setup
One to two sentences. Why this matters right now. Toddler language. No jargon.
[TEXT ON SCREEN]
Analogy
One visual comparison everyone understands immediately. Make it something the editor can put b-roll behind.
[TEXT ON SCREEN]
Breakdown
Two to four points. One idea per sentence. Short and punchy. Text on screen after each point.
[TEXT ON SCREEN after each point]
So What
One to two sentences. What does the viewer do with this right now. Personal and actionable.
[TEXT ON SCREEN]
Write Off
Rotate through all 8. Never repeat the same one twice in a row. Track what you used.
Write Off 1 — The Rating — I am giving [topic] a [X] out of 10 on the must know scale. Drop yours in the comments.
Write Off 2 — The Test — Now you know. Go test this today and tell me what you find.
Write Off 3 — The Challenge — If this changed how you think about [topic] share it with one person who needs to hear it.
Write Off 4 — The Cliffhanger — That is [topic] simplified. Next video I am breaking down [related topic] and it gets even more interesting. Follow so you do not miss it.
Write Off 5 — The Call Out — Most people watching this will keep doing what they have always done. The ones who save this and apply it are the ones who actually change. Be that person.
Write Off 6 — The Credibility Close — I am the Muscle SPT — a PT student, former D1 athlete, and bodybuilder. This is what I study so you do not have to. Follow for more.
Write Off 7 — The Save Hook — Save this video. You will want to come back to it.
Write Off 8 — The Simple Close — I am the Muscle SPT. Want to build muscle and prevent injury for life. Follow for more.
End Card
I am the Muscle SPT. Want to build muscle and prevent injury for life. Follow for more.
CAPTION FORMAT
Three to five lines. Plain language. Problem. Insight. Takeaway. No jargon. Rotate soft sell coaching CTA maximum twice per week.

PAGE 4 — CAROUSEL
The Muscle SPT — Dr. Zach Ermert
TOPIC TAG
Topic —
Pillar —
Sub-category —
Carousel type — White infographic research and data / Dark conviction and mission
Write off used —
WHEN TO USE
Wednesday slot. Complements Monday's deep dive with a different visual format on the same or related topic. Most saveable post of the week. Drives saves more than any other format.
CHOOSE YOUR CAROUSEL TYPE
White infographic — for research data, clinical statistics, comparison content, anything with numbers. Looks authoritative and professional. Signals research and credibility before anyone reads a word.
Dark conviction — for motivational content, hot takes, personal story, mission driven content. Highest emotional impact. Signals conviction and authority.
WHITE INFOGRAPHIC CAROUSEL
Visual System
Background — white F8F8F8
Primary text — near black 111111
Accent — brand yellow E8FF47 used sparingly for highlights and data points
Data visualization — yellow bars white background black labels
Source cited — small gray bottom of relevant slide
Font — clean sans serif. Bold for headers. Regular for body.
Slide 1 — Title Slide
Clean white. Bold black header hook maximum 8 words. One yellow accent element or clinical anatomy line drawing icon. Handle bottom center black.
Slide 2 — The Problem or Context
Clean white. Dark gray header. Body text near black. Key stat or word in yellow callout box. Two to three sentences. Toddler simple.
Slide 3 — The Data or Research
Chart or graph. Bar chart, progress bar, comparison table, or large stat callout. Yellow and black visualization. Source cited small gray bottom.
Slide 4 — The Breakdown or Prescription
Numbered list or comparison table. Clean and scannable. Each point its own row. Key numbers in yellow.
Slide 5 — Pull Quote Slide
White background. One bold statement. Nothing else. Maximum 10 words. Most shareable slide. Screenshot bait.
Slide 6 — The Takeaway or Action Step
Clean white. One clear action. Simple and direct. Yellow CTA element.
Slide 7 — Brand Close
White background. Handle bold black centered. Tagline yellow below.
Follow @themusclespt [yellow bold]
Build Muscle. Prevent Injury. For Life. [black]
Canva tools — built in chart tool for bar charts and progress bars. Line style anatomy icons only. Table tool with alternating white and light gray rows. Thin light gray DDDDDD divider lines.
DARK CONVICTION CAROUSEL
Visual System
Background — near black 070707
Primary text — white
Key word emphasis — brand yellow E8FF47
Handle — white small bottom center every slide
Title slide font — Playfair Display or similar serif
Body slide header — Radley yellow large
Body slide text — Radley white smaller
Slide numbers — DM Sans 30 to 36pt yellow top left corner
Arrow — white right pointing same position every slide except last
Last slide — no arrow. Follow @themusclespt yellow. Tagline white below.
Slide 1 — Hook slide. Bold. Yellow on black. Same as the reel hook.
Slide 2 — The problem in one sentence. White text. Plain language.
Slide 3 — The surprising truth or the analogy. One to two sentences.
Slide 4 — The actionable takeaway. What do they do right now.
Slide 5 — Pull quote. One bold statement. Maximum 10 words. Most shareable slide.
Slide 6 — Brand close. Follow @themusclespt yellow. Tagline white.
CAPTION FORMAT
Three to five lines. Lead with data point or research finding for white infographic. Lead with conviction or mission statement for dark carousel. Close with actionable takeaway. Rotate soft sell coaching CTA maximum twice per week.

INFOGRAPHIC TYPES FOR YOUR CONTENT
Bar charts — muscle loss per decade, VO2 max decline by age, BFR strength increase vs low intensity
Progress bars — gait speed cutoffs color coded from red to green
Body diagrams — anatomy line drawing with labeled arrows for muscle groups posture or joint positions
Timeline graphics — muscle loss from 20 to 80, peak bone mass, hormonal decline by decade
Comparison tables — BFR vs heavy load vs low intensity, flexibility vs mobility vs stability, modifiable vs non-modifiable risk factors
Stat callout boxes — large bold number with context. 40 percent strength increase. 3 to 8 percent muscle loss per decade. 1 in 3 adults over 50 with sarcopenia.
TOOLS
Canva — handles 90 percent of what you need. Start here.
Piktochart — more chart types and infographic templates than Canva. Free tier.
Datawrapper — specifically for charts and graphs. Publication quality. Free. Used by major news organizations.
Adobe Express — cleaner data visualization output. Free with Adobe account.
MASTER RULES
White means data and research. Dark means conviction and mission. Never mix the two on the same carousel.
Pull quote slide is your most shareable slide — always include one in the middle
Every slide has your handle at the bottom in the same position
Source every data point — small gray text at the bottom of data slides
Five hashtags maximum always ending with themusclespt

Add a final slide — want the full breakdown watch my reel link in bio — connects carousel audience to your video content

Want me to try different variations — stacked layout, horizontal layout, icon only version, or a version with a graphic element like a muscle or lightning bolt?Themusclept logoImage · SVG DownloadYou said: What was caption for reel on overload principleWhat was caption for reel on overload principleApr 4Claude responded: Your body is not broken.Your body is not broken. It is just comfortable.
The overload principle is the reason most people train for years and look exactly the same. Your body only adapts to demands you actually place on it. When the weight never goes up and the reps never increase your body has zero reason to change. Same stimulus. Same result. That is not a motivation problem. That is biology.
Add a rep. Slow the eccentric. Add a little weight. Do something different from last week. That is the whole system.

#musclebuilding #progressiveoverload #strengthtraining #physicaltherapy #themusclesptYou said: Best app or platform for generating thumbnails for Instagram reelsBest app or platform for generating thumbnails for Instagram reelsApr 4You said: Canva?Canva?Apr 4Claude responded: Yes.Yes. Canva is your best option and here is why specifically for your brand.
It already has your brand colors saved if you set up a brand kit. You can build a thumbnail template once and duplicate it for every reel. Consistent look across every post without starting from scratch each time.
For Instagram Reel thumbnails the specs are 1080 x 1920 pixels — same as your Reel itself. In Canva set a custom canvas to that size and build your template there.
What makes a strong Reel thumbnail for your brand specifically:
Your face taking up at least 60 percent of the frame. Instagram favors faces. People scroll past graphics. They stop for faces.
Bold text overlay at the top or bottom. Maximum 4 to 6 words. Your hook as the thumbnail text. Large enough to read on a phone screen without zooming.
Brand yellow E8FF47 as the text color or accent element. Creates immediate visual consistency across your entire grid.
High contrast between your face and the background. Your yellow green light setup already does this naturally.
No clutter. One face. One text line. One color accent. Done.
The template in Canva would be — your photo as the background layer, a subtle dark gradient overlay at the bottom 30 percent of the frame so text is readable, your hook text in yellow bold at the bottom, your handle in small white at the very bottom edge.
Build that template once. Swap the photo and the text for every new reel. Takes two minutes per thumbnail once the template exists.You said: Font?Font? And how to go about thumbnails. And are they necessaryApr 4Claude responded: Thumbnails on Instagram Reels are not as critical as they are on YouTube.Thumbnails on Instagram Reels are not as critical as they are on YouTube. Here is the honest breakdown:
ARE THEY NECESSARY
For Reels — not strictly necessary. Instagram auto selects a frame from your video as the cover. If your first frame is strong — good lighting, face visible, text on screen — that works fine as a thumbnail without any extra work.
Where thumbnails matter on Instagram is your grid. When someone lands on your profile they see a grid of covers. If your auto selected frames are inconsistent, blurry, or mid-expression your grid looks messy and unpolished. A clean consistent thumbnail grid signals professionalism and makes new visitors more likely to follow.
So the answer is — not necessary for individual reel performance but important for profile first impressions.
WHEN TO PRIORITIZE THUMBNAILS
When you start posting consistently enough that your grid has 9 plus posts visible. Before that it does not matter enough to spend time on.
When you start getting profile visits from viral or high performing reels. That is when people are landing on your grid and making a follow decision based on what they see.
FONT RECOMMENDATION FOR CANVA THUMBNAILS
Primary — Montserrat Bold or Montserrat Extra Bold. Clean, modern, highly legible at small sizes on mobile. Works perfectly with your brand aesthetic.
Alternative — Bebas Neue. All caps only. Very bold and punchy. Great for single line hook text overlays.
For your brand specifically I would use Montserrat Extra Bold in E8FF47 yellow for the main hook text and keep it to one line maximum four to six words. Clean and instantly readable on a phone screen at thumbnail size.
SIMPLE THUMBNAIL WORKFLOW IN CANVA
Step 1 — Create a 1080 x 1920 canvas in Canva
Step 2 — Upload your best frame from the reel as the background — a clear well lit shot of your face
Step 3 — Add a dark gradient overlay rectangle at the bottom 30 percent of the frame — opacity 60 to 70 percent — so text is readable over the image
Step 4 — Add your hook text in Montserrat Extra Bold yellow at the bottom over the gradient
Step 5 — Add your handle in small white at the very bottom edge
Step 6 — Save as template. Duplicate for every new reel. Swap photo and text. Done in two minutes.
The whole system takes 30 minutes to build once and two minutes per reel after that.You said: What, are there any apps that I can, like, tell it to make, like, videos?What, are there any apps that I can, like, tell it to make, like, videos? Using AI. That it can, like, input. Like, for example, if I wanted, like, a video of showing rib cage expansion or some sort of anatomy, is there an app where I can, like, type in what I want or tell what I want? I can make a video AI into that so that I can save that video and then upload it. As a clip into my caption for capco.Apr 4Claude responded: Yes.Yes. Here are your best options for exactly that use case:
BEST FOR ANATOMY AND CLINICAL CONTENT
Sora — OpenAI's video generation tool. You type a text prompt and it generates a realistic video clip. Most advanced quality available right now. Type something like ribcage expanding during diaphragmatic breathing slow motion anatomy view and it generates the clip. Access through ChatGPT Plus subscription.
Runway ML — type a text prompt or upload an image and it generates a video from it. Very popular with content creators. Good for anatomy style visuals and clinical concepts. Has a free tier with limited credits. runwayml.com
Pika — similar to Runway. Text to video. Good for clean simple anatomy style animations. Free tier available. pika.art
Kling — one of the highest quality text to video generators available right now. Very realistic output. Good for body movement and anatomical concepts. kling.ai
Hailuo — fast and clean text to video. Good for short clips under 10 seconds which is exactly what you need for b-roll inserts. hailuoai.com
FOR ANATOMY SPECIFICALLY
The prompts that work best for your content type:
Slow motion ribcage expanding during deep breath from anterior view
Human muscle fiber contracting under load close up
Spine compressing and decompressing during movement lateral view
Foot arch collapsing and engaging during walking gait cycle
Knee joint loading and unloading during squat movement
Blood flow restriction cuff on thigh during leg exercise
HONEST ASSESSMENT
The quality varies. Sora and Kling produce the most realistic output right now. Runway and Pika are more accessible and have free tiers. None of them are perfect for precise anatomy — they are best for impressionistic clinical visuals that communicate the concept rather than medically accurate diagrams.
For precise anatomy animations your best alternative is still searching Pixabay or Pexels for anatomy animation and using existing stock clips. But for custom concepts that do not exist in stock libraries — Runway or Pika are your fastest options.
Start with Runway. Free tier. Type your prompt. Download the clip. Drop it into CapCut as your b-roll layer. That workflow takes about five minutes once you have your prompt ready.You said: I forgot to post today and now it’s 9pm.I forgot to post today and now it’s 9pm. Should I post still tonight or wait to tomorrowApr 4Claude responded: Post tonight.Post tonight. Here is why.
Nine pm is not ideal but it is not dead either. Your Stories audience — your warmest followers — are still active. Post now, share to Stories immediately, reply to every comment tonight before you sleep.
The bigger mistake is breaking your posting streak. Consistency signals to the algorithm that your account is active. Missing a full day during your reset period is more damaging than posting at a slightly off time.
Post it tonight. Get back on schedule tomorrow at 6 to 8pm. Do not let one late night turn into two missed days.You said: Can I use kahunas with Cronomter rather than superset with CronomterCan I use kahunas with Cronomter rather than superset with CronomterApr 5Claude responded: Yes you can use Kahunas with Cronometer instead of Superset.Yes you can use Kahunas with Cronometer instead of Superset. The workflow would be essentially the same — Kahunas handles the coaching and programming side, Cronometer handles the nutrition tracking side, and you connect them through shared resources and links the same way you would with Superset.
The honest comparison:
Kahunas strengths — cleaner client facing interface, better habit tracking built in, good check in system, more customizable coaching experience overall. Popular with online coaches who want a more premium client experience.
Superset strengths — exercise library and video demonstration integration is stronger, better for program delivery specifically, slightly more fitness coaching focused out of the box.
For your brand and coaching model the decision comes down to one question — what do your clients interact with most. If the program and exercise demos are the core of your coaching Superset is stronger there. If the check ins, habits, and overall coaching relationship are the core Kahunas gives you a better client experience.
The Cronometer integration works either way since you would be sharing the Cronometer link or directing clients to track there independently regardless of which platform you use for the coaching logistics side.
Have you tried the free trial of Kahunas yet to see how the interface feels compared to Superset. That hands on experience will tell you more than any comparison I can give you.You said: Bio SPTBio SPT 
I help men & women build muscle, fix posture, & prevent injury for life
@hd.muscle code: THEMUSCLEPT
1:1 Coaching Application ↓Apr 5Claude responded: Clean and strong.Clean and strong. Here is the full bio:
Dr. Zach Ermert, SPT
I help men & women build muscle, fix posture, & prevent injury for life
@hd.muscle code: THEMUSCLEPT
1:1 Coaching Application ↓
[Stan Store link]
That works really well. Here is why:
Line 1 — your name and credential. Instant authority.
Line 2 — your mission statement as a direct value proposition. Tells exactly who you help and what you do for them in one line.
Line 3 — affiliation and discount code. Revenue and partnership visible upfront.
Line 4 — clear CTA with the arrow pulling the eye down to the link.
The only thing to watch is character count. Instagram bios cap at 150 characters. This sits right around that limit so check it in the app when you paste it in and adjust spacing if needed.
One small suggestion — consider adding the emoji before the code line to create visual separation and make the discount code pop:
Dr. Zach Ermert, SPT
I help men & women build muscle, fix posture, & prevent injury for life
🏷 @hd.muscle — THEMUSCLEPT
1:1 Coaching Application ↓
[Stan Store link]
Either version works. The Muscle SPT — Dr. Zach Ermert
Build Muscle. Fix Posture. Prevent Injury. For Life.
HOW TO USE THIS EVERY SESSION
Step 1 — Pick a topic from the content library
Step 2 — Fill in the topic tag at the top
Step 3 — Run the framework top to bottom
Step 4 — Get your full week of content from one topic
Step 5 — Film edit post between 6 and 8pm
Step 6 — Share to Stories immediately after posting
Step 7 — Reply to every comment within 60 minutes
Step 8 — Track which hook and write off drove the most engagement
Step 9 — Rotate the same topic across different formats throughout the week
Step 10 — Double down on what works
TOPIC TAG — FILL THIS IN FIRST
Topic —
Pillar —
Sub-category —
Setup — White coat / Athletic wear / Casual button down / Raw
Lighting — Yellow green / Warm amber / Deep blue / Natural gym

Hook format used —
Write off used —

Supporting citations — Author Year Journal PMID
CITATION SYSTEM
Every piece of content built from this framework includes at minimum one peer reviewed citation. Here is how citations are used across each format:
In the script — the research is referenced conversationally in plain language. Never read out a citation on camera. Instead say the research shows or studies consistently show or a meta-analysis of X studies found. The PMID backs it up but never appears on screen in the video itself.
In the caption — one citation line at the bottom of every caption above the hashtags. Format is clean and simple.
Caption citation format:
Source: [Author et al., Year] — PMID [number]
In the carousel — one slide dedicated to the source. Small gray text on white infographic carousels. Small white text on dark carousels. Bottom of the data slide on any slide containing research numbers.
Carousel citation format:
[Author et al., Year]. [Journal Name].
In Stories — not required. Stories are conversational and ephemeral. Citation in the linked post is sufficient.
On screen — optional for the 60 to 90 second reel only. If you want to add credibility visually add a small text overlay in the bottom corner on the data point slide that reads Source: Author Year PMID. Keep it small. It is a credibility signal not the focus.
CITATION RULE
If you state a specific number, statistic, or clinical claim it must have a PMID attached. If the claim is general knowledge in the field and not a specific stat you do not need a citation. The goal is to be the most credible creator in the space — not to turn every video into a research paper. Use citations strategically on your strongest claims.
8 REPEATABLE HOOK FORMATS
Hook 1 — The Question — [Topic]. Do you actually know what this means?
Hook 2 — The Myth Buster — Everyone thinks [common belief]. They are wrong.
Hook 3 — The Time Hook — [Topic] explained in under 30 seconds.
Hook 4 — The Surprise — [Surprising fact]. Here is why that matters for you.
Hook 5 — The Direct Challenge — If you do not know this about [topic] you need to watch this.
Hook 6 — The Simple Promise — [Topic]. Here is everything you need to know.
Hook 7 — The Relatability Hook — Nobody explained [topic] to me like this. So I am doing it for you.
Hook 8 — The Stakes Hook — [Topic] is affecting your body right now. Here is what is actually happening.
8 REPEATABLE WRITE OFFS
Write Off 1 — The Rating — I am giving [topic] a [X] out of 10 on the must know scale. Drop yours in the comments.
Write Off 2 — The Test — Now you know. Go test this today and tell me what you find.
Write Off 3 — The Challenge — If this changed how you think about [topic] share it with one person who needs to hear it.
Write Off 4 — The Cliffhanger — That is [topic] simplified. Next video I am breaking down [related topic] and it gets even more interesting. Follow so you do not miss it.
Write Off 5 — The Call Out — Most people watching this will keep doing what they have always done. The ones who save this and apply it are the ones who actually change. Be that person.
Write Off 6 — The Credibility Close — I am the Muscle SPT — a PT student, former D1 athlete, and bodybuilder. This is what I study so you do not have to. Follow for more.
Write Off 7 — The Save Hook — Save this video. You will want to come back to it.
Write Off 8 — The Simple Close — I am the Muscle SPT. Want to build muscle and prevent injury for life. Follow for more.
WRITE OFF ROTATION TRACKER
Week 1 — Monday 1 Tuesday 7 Wednesday 5 Thursday 4 Friday 2 Saturday 8
Week 2 — Monday 3 Tuesday 6 Wednesday 2 Thursday 1 Friday 7 Saturday 4
Week 3 — Monday 5 Tuesday 4 Wednesday 8 Thursday 3 Friday 6 Saturday 2
Week 4 — Monday 7 Tuesday 2 Wednesday 3 Thursday 6 Friday 1 Saturday 5
MASTER SCRIPT STRUCTURE — EVERY VIDEO
Hook — One punchy line. Maximum 10 words. Visual on screen. Stops the scroll.
Simple Setup — One to two sentences. What are we talking about and why does it matter right now. No jargon. Toddler level.
Analogy — One comparison to something everyone understands. Visual and memorable. Make it something the editor can put b-roll or animation behind.
Breakdown — Two to four key points. Short sentences. One idea per sentence. Pause between each one for emphasis.
So What — One to two sentences. Why does this matter for the viewer right now. Personal and actionable.
Research Note — One conversational line referencing the research without reading a citation. The research shows. Studies consistently find. A meta-analysis of X studies found.
Write Off — Rotate between all 8. Never repeat the same one twice in a row. Track it.
End Card — I am the Muscle SPT. Want to build muscle and prevent injury for life. Follow for more.
MASTER RULES FOR EVERY PIECE OF CONTENT
Speak as if talking to a toddler — no jargon no fluff
Use analogies for every complex concept — make it visual and memorable
Every script filmable in under 90 seconds ideally under 60
Every caption — body text three dots like and follow citation hashtags
Five hashtags maximum always ending with themusclespt
Every video tagged before scripting — pillar sub-category format hook write off setup lighting tone length citation PMID
Exercise demos always include — primary mover secondary movers resistance profile stability demand stimulus to fatigue ratio
Hooks always repeatable and testable — rotate all 8 formats and track which performs best
Write offs always rotate — track which drives the most follows and saves
Every script written so visuals and b-roll can be added in edit — bracket notes for editor on every key point
Caption CTA rotates between standard like and follow and soft sell coaching invitation — maximum two soft sells per week
Every specific stat or clinical claim has a PMID attached — referenced conversationally in video, cited formally in caption
YouTube title format for exercise demos — Exercise Name: The PT Cue That Changes Everything

LITTLE KNOWN TIPS — HOW TO USE EVERY TIP LIST
Format 1 — Standalone tip reel. Hook is the tip as a surprising fact. Body is the one sentence why it works. Write off is Save CTA. Fastest content to film.
Format 2 — Carousel slides. Title is the hook. Each slide is one tip. Final slide is brand close.
Format 3 — Caption add on. One bonus tip at the bottom of any reel caption labeled Bonus tip.
Format 4 — Stories series. One tip per day across five consecutive Stories. Text slide yellow on black. No filming required.
Format 5 — Community content. Post the tip list as a question. Ask which one surprised them most.
The rule — every tip specific enough to use at the next session. The more specific the higher the save rate. Cite the source on any tip derived from a specific study.
WEEKLY CONTENT SCHEDULE
Monday — 60 to 90 second deep dive reel — Write off tracked — PMID in caption
Tuesday — 10 to 15 second pure visual reel — same topic compressed — Write off tracked — PMID if stat used on screen
Wednesday — White infographic carousel — complementary data — Write off tracked — PMID on data slide and caption
Thursday — 30 second Explained In Under 30 Seconds — Write off 4 default — PMID in caption
Friday — Hot take or reaction reel — Write off tracked — PMID if specific claim made
Saturday — BTS or personality — Write off tracked — no citation required
Sunday — Soft sell Stories only — no feed post — no citation required
CONTENT MAXIMIZERS
Pin your best performing post from each topic to the top of your profile
Share every feed post to Stories immediately after posting with a poll sticker
Take the pull quote slide from every carousel and post as a standalone static post two weeks later
Use the 10 to 15 second reel as a Stories teaser the day before the full 60 to 90 second drops
Post one little known tip per day as a Stories text slide across five consecutive days
Reply to every comment on the first post of each topic with a teaser for the next format
Add a final carousel slide — want the full breakdown watch my reel link in bio

CITATION FORMAT RULES
Standard citation — Source: Author et al., Year — PMID number
Multiple citations — Source: Author et al., Year — PMID number / Author et al., Year — PMID number
No PMID available — Source: Author et al., Year. Journal Name.
General knowledge claim — no citation needed. Only cite specific stats and clinical claims.
Hot takes — always cite. Your credibility in this format depends entirely on the research backing you up.
BTS and personal — never cite. These are human moments not clinical claims.
WHERE THE CITATION SITS
Always above the three dots. Never below the hashtags. Keeps it visible without cluttering the CTA and hashtag block.
Line 1 — hook
Line 2 — insight
Line 3 — takeaway
Line 4 — optional bonus tip or soft sell

The rule — every caption should be readable in under 15 seconds on a phone screen. If it takes longer cut it. The caption supports the video. It does not replace it.You said: CAROUSELCAROUSEL
Slide 1
No pill exists for muscle loss. But something more powerful does.
Slide 2
Sarcopenia — the muscle loss that begins at 30 — has zero FDA approved drug treatments. But the research is extremely clear on what actually works. And it does not come from a pharmacy.
Slide 3
Dr. Nathan LeBrasseur from the research put it simply — exercise is the powerful magical silver bullet for muscle loss. Not a supplement. Not a medication. Exercise.
Slide 4
The protocol the evidence supports — resistance training two to three times per week. Large muscle groups. Eight to fifteen reps. Start at moderate intensity and progress to high intensity over six to twelve weeks.
Slide 5
Four nutrients the research supports alongside training — protein is the raw material your muscle rebuilds with. Vitamin D supports muscle function. Leucine directly triggers muscle protein synthesis. HMB reduces muscle breakdown.
Slide 6
The prescription exists. It just does not come from a pharmacy. Two to three sessions per week. Hit your protein. Support it with vitamin D and leucine rich foods. That is the protocol.
Slide 7
Follow @themusclespt. Build Muscle. Prevent Injury. For Life.
Carousel Caption
No FDA approved medication exists for sarcopenia. Zero. But the research is clear on what works.
Exercise is the silver bullet. Resistance training two to three times per week targeting large muscle groups at eight to fifteen reps progressing from moderate to high intensity over six to twelve weeks.
Four nutrients that enhance the impact of training — protein, vitamin D, leucine, and HMB. Each one plays a specific role in building and protecting your muscle.
The prescription exists. It just does not come from a pharmacy.

Here is your full infographic carousel with citations:
CAROUSEL — NO PILL EXISTS FOR MUSCLE LOSS
White Infographic Version with Research Data
Slide 1 — Title Slide
White background. Bold black header. Yellow accent line below header. Clinical icon optional — dumbbell or muscle line drawing.
NO PILL EXISTS FOR MUSCLE LOSS.
BUT THE RESEARCH FOUND SOMETHING BETTER.
@themusclespt [black small bottom center]
Slide 2 — The Problem
White background. Dark gray header. Key stat in yellow callout box.
HEADER — WHAT IS SARCOPENIA?
Sarcopenia is the progressive loss of muscle mass and strength that begins at age 30. It affects 1 in 3 adults over 50 and is the number one predictor of fall risk and loss of independence. And as of today there is zero FDA approved medication to treat it.
[YELLOW CALLOUT BOX — 0 FDA approved drugs for sarcopenia]
Source: Papadopoulou et al., 2023 — PMID 37291776
Slide 3 — The Data — Bar Chart
White background. Yellow and black bar chart. Three bars side by side.
HEADER — WHAT THE RESEARCH ACTUALLY SUPPORTS
Bar 1 — Resistance Training Alone — High quality evidence for muscle mass, strength, and physical performance
Bar 2 — Resistance Training + Nutrition — Greater effect on handgrip strength and muscle mass
Bar 3 — No Exercise — Continued muscle decline
[YELLOW highlight on Bar 1 and Bar 2]
Source: Beckwée et al., 2019 — PMID 31233069
Slide 4 — The Protocol — Numbered List
White background. Numbered list. Key numbers in yellow.
HEADER — THE EVIDENCE BASED PROTOCOL
01 — Resistance training 2 to 3 times per week
02 — Target large muscle groups — squat hinge push pull
03 — 8 to 15 reps per set
04 — Start moderate intensity 60 percent 1RM — progress to high intensity 80 percent plus 1RM
05 — Maintain for 6 to 12 weeks minimum to see meaningful change
[KEY NUMBERS in yellow — 2 to 3x, 8-15 reps, 60%, 80%+, 6-12 weeks]
Source: Beckwée et al., 2019 — PMID 31233069
Slide 5 — The Nutrients — Icon Grid
White background. Four icons in a two by two grid. One nutrient per box. Yellow accent borders on each box.
HEADER — 4 NUTRIENTS THE RESEARCH SUPPORTS
PROTEIN [icon] — Raw material for muscle rebuilding. 0.7 to 1g per pound of bodyweight daily.
VITAMIN D [icon] — Supports muscle function. Deficiency directly impairs muscle fiber size.
LEUCINE [icon] — The amino acid that most directly triggers muscle protein synthesis.
HMB [icon] — Metabolite of leucine. Reduces muscle protein breakdown.
Source: Beckwée et al., 2019 — PMID 31233069 / Papadopoulou et al., 2023 — PMID 37291776
Slide 6 — Pull Quote
White background. One bold statement. Nothing else. Maximum 10 words. Black text centered.
"Exercise is the powerful magical silver bullet for muscle loss."
— Dr. Nathan LeBrasseur, PhD
[Yellow quotation mark accent]
Slide 7 — The Takeaway
White background. Clean action steps. Yellow CTA element.
HEADER — THE PRESCRIPTION EXISTS. IT JUST IS NOT FROM A PHARMACY.
Resistance train 2 to 3 times per week.
Hit your protein target every single day.
Support it with vitamin D and leucine rich foods.
Start today. Track your progress every 6 weeks.
[YELLOW element — Save this protocol]
Slide 8 — Brand Close
White background. Handle bold black centered. Tagline yellow.
Follow @themusclespt [yellow bold]
Build Muscle. Fix Posture. Prevent Injury. For Life. [black]
CAPTION
No FDA approved medication exists for sarcopenia. Zero. But the research is extremely clear on what works.
Resistance training 2 to 3 times per week targeting large muscle groups at 8 to 15 reps progressing from moderate to high intensity. Combined with adequate protein, vitamin D, leucine, and HMB — this is the protocol the evidence supports.
The prescription exists. It just does not come from a pharmacy.
Source: Beckwée et al., 2019 — PMID 31233069

APPS FOR MAKING RESEARCH DATA LOOK GREAT
Datawrapper — best free tool for clean publication quality charts and graphs. Input your data directly. Generates bar charts, line graphs, and tables that look like major news publication graphics. Free. No design skills needed. datawrapper.de
Canva — built in chart tool handles bar charts, progress bars, and pie charts. Fastest option since you already use it. Go to elements, search chart, input your data. Done in two minutes.
Piktochart — specifically built for infographics. More template variety than Canva. Has medical and research focused templates. Free tier available. piktochart.com
Venngage — research and data infographic focused. Has clinical and scientific templates built in. Best option if you want something that looks like a medical journal figure. Free tier available. venngage.com
Infogram — similar to Datawrapper. Clean interactive charts. Good for bar charts and comparison visuals. Free tier available. infogram.com
MY HONEST RECOMMENDATION FOR YOUR WORKFLOW
Use Datawrapper for your charts and graphs — input the data, download the image, import into Canva. Then build the rest of the carousel slide around it in Canva. That combination gives you publication quality data visualization inside your brand visual system. Takes about 10 minutes per data slide once you learn the workflow.You said: Don’t see a data wrapper anything else on App StoreDon’t see a data wrapper anything else on App StoreApr 7Claude responded: Datawrapper is web based only — no app.Datawrapper is web based only — no app. You use it in a browser at datawrapper.de, build your chart, then download it as an image and bring it into Canva.
For App Store specifically here are your options:
CHART AND GRAPH APPS ON APP STORE
Infogram — available on App Store. Input data directly in the app. Clean chart output. Download as image and import into Canva. Free tier available.
Canva — you already have it and the built in chart tool covers 80 percent of what you need. Go to elements, search chart, tap it, input your data, and it generates a bar chart or progress bar automatically. Fastest option since you are already in the app.
Microsoft Excel or Google Sheets — both available on App Store. Build your chart in the spreadsheet, screenshot or export the chart, bring it into Canva. Not the prettiest output but fully functional for simple bar charts and line graphs.
Numbers — Apple's native spreadsheet app. Already on your iPhone. Clean chart output. Build the chart, screenshot it, import into Canva.
Piktochart — has a mobile friendly web version accessible from Safari on your phone. Not a native app but works well on mobile browser.
HONEST RECOMMENDATION
For your workflow right now — use Canva's built in chart tool for simple data visualizations and use Google Sheets on your phone for anything more complex. Both are already on your phone. Build the chart in Sheets, screenshot it, bring it into Canva as an image layer, design the slide around it. MACHINE ROW — COMPOUND LIFT SERIES. PULL EDITION.
Hook
"How to row to build muscle and prevent injury."
Spoken
"Third video in my compound lift series. Squat. Hinge. Push. Pull. Four movements. Everything you need.
I choose a machine row over a cable or free weight row for one reason — stability. When the machine handles the balance I can focus entirely on loading my back. No energy wasted on staying upright.
And here is why the row specifically is so valuable. You get shoulder extension from your lats. You get scapular retraction from your rhomboids and mid traps. Two movements. One exercise. Your entire back working together.
Now the cues.
Counterforce into the pad. Same concept as the chest press. Press your chest into the support and do not let it lift away as you pull. That keeps tension exactly where it belongs.
Eyes on the horizon. Not looking up. Not looking down. Neutral head position throughout.
On the pull — think about driving your pinky into the handle. That fifth finger cue creates the external rotation you want and keeps your elbows driving back instead of flaring out.
On the way back — let your index finger lead the return. That subtle internal rotation cue keeps tension on the muscle through the full eccentric.
Two to four second eccentric. Train close to failure. Every set."
End Card
"That is how you row to build muscle and prevent injury for life. I am the Muscle SPT. I will see you in the next one."
Caption
Compound lift series. Pull edition.
The machine row gives you shoulder extension from your lats and scapular retraction from your rhomboids and mid traps. Two movement patterns. One exercise. Your entire back working together.
Cues — counterforce into the pad, eyes on the horizon, drive your pinky into the handle on the pull, let your index finger lead the return on the eccentric. Two to four second eccentric. Train close to failure. Every set.
Save this for your next pull day.
.
.
.
Like & follow @themusclespt for more!
#backday #physicaltherapy #musclebuilding #strengthtraining #themusclespt
ROMANIAN DEADLIFT — COMPOUND LIFT SERIES. HINGE EDITION.
Hook
"How to hinge to build muscle and prevent injury."
Spoken
"Second video in my compound lift series. Squat. Hinge. Push. Pull. Four movements. Everything you need.
I am using a pit shark today. No bar on your back. No balance demand. Just pure hinge loading.
The hinge is the most functional movement pattern most people never train. Every time you pick something up off the floor your body is asking for this movement. Build it now.
Now the cues.
Feet neutral. Two contact points — heel and base of your big toe pressed into the floor.
As you lower think about your thighs very subtly rotating inward. Not a lot. Just a gentle shift. This keeps tension on the hamstrings through the full range of motion.
Shift your hips back like you are trying to touch the wall behind you. Keep a slight anterior pelvic tilt. If you find yourself excessively arching cue a slight posterior tilt to bring you back to neutral.
Eyes on the horizon. Head neutral. Not looking up. Not looking down.
Go as low as you can while keeping tension on your hamstrings and glutes. For most people that is around chin height. Your hamstring flexibility will determine your range.
Want more glutes — bend your knees slightly more.
Want more hamstrings — keep the legs straighter.
Want more erectors — allow a slight controlled spinal flexion at the bottom.
Own the stretch at the bottom. Drive through your heels back to the top."
End Card
"That is how you hinge to build muscle and prevent injury for life. I am the Muscle SPT. I will see you in the next one."
Caption
Compound lift series. Hinge edition.
The Romanian deadlift is the most functional movement pattern most people never train. Pit shark eliminates the balance demand so you can focus entirely on loading the posterior chain.
Cues — feet neutral, subtle internal rotation of the thighs as you lower, hips shift back, slight anterior pelvic tilt, eyes on the horizon. Go as low as tension allows. More glutes bend the knees slightly. More hamstrings keep them straighter.
Save this for your next pull day.

WOLFF'S LAW — EXPLAINED IN UNDER 30 SECONDS
Hook
"Wolff's Law explained in under 30 seconds."
[TEXT ON SCREEN — Wolff's Law In Under 30 Seconds]
Spoken
"Your bones are not static. They are alive. And they respond to load exactly the same way your muscles do.
Think of your skeleton like a bridge. Engineers build bridges stronger in the areas that carry the most weight. Your body does the exact same thing automatically.
When you place mechanical load on your bones — through resistance training, impact, or bodyweight — your body detects that stress and deposits more bone tissue exactly where it is needed. Remove the load and the bone slowly weakens in that area.
That is Wolff's Law. Your bones adapt to the demands you place on them. Load them and they get stronger. Stop loading them and they get weaker.
This is why resistance training is the most powerful tool we have for preventing osteoporosis. Not calcium supplements. Not medication. Load."
Write Off
That is Wolff's Law simplified. Next video I am breaking down peak bone mass and why what you do before age 30 determines your bone health for life. Follow so you do not miss it.
End Card
I am the Muscle SPT. Want to build muscle and prevent injury for life. Follow for more.
Caption
Your bones adapt to the demands you place on them. That is Wolff's Law.
Load them through resistance training and they get stronger. Stop loading them and they slowly weaken. This is why resistance training is the single most powerful tool for preventing osteoporosis — more effective than supplements and most medications.
Your skeleton is not fixed. It is responding to every demand you place on it right now.
.
.
.

#bonehealth #physicaltherapy #strengthtraining #osteoporosis #themusclespt
Note — Wolff's Law is a well established principle in bone physiology. No specific PMID is required for the general principle. If you make a specific claim about osteoporosis prevention through resistance training in a future video I will pull the PMID at that point.
YOUR POSTURE STARTS AT YOUR FEET — EPISODE 1
Hook
"Your posture problem does not start at your shoulders. It starts at your feet."
[TEXT ON SCREEN — It starts at your feet]
Spoken
"Most people try to fix their posture by pulling their shoulders back or lifting their chin. And it never sticks.
Because they are fixing the roof without ever looking at the foundation.
Think of your body like a building. If the foundation is uneven everything above it will be crooked. Your feet are that foundation. Everything from your ankles to your neck follows whatever happens at the ground.
Here is what that chain reaction looks like.
Your arch collapses. Your shin rotates inward.
[TEXT ON SCREEN — Arch collapse → shin rotates in]
Your shin rotates inward. Your knee caves.
[TEXT ON SCREEN — Knee caves]
Your knee caves. Your pelvis tilts.
[TEXT ON SCREEN — Pelvis tilts]
Your pelvis tilts. Your spine compensates. Your shoulders round. Your head shifts forward.
[TEXT ON SCREEN — One problem. Six consequences.]
One issue at the foundation creates a chain reaction all the way to your neck.
Fix the foundation first. Everything else follows."
Write Off
That is Episode 1 of the Posture From The Ground Up series. Next episode we go deeper into exactly what is happening at your foot and what you can do about it. Follow so you do not miss it.
End Card
I am the Muscle SPT. Want to build muscle and prevent injury for life. Follow for more.
Caption
Most people try to fix their posture by pulling their shoulders back. It never sticks. Because they are fixing the roof without ever looking at the foundation.
Your feet are the foundation of your entire body. Arch collapse leads to shin rotation. Shin rotation leads to knee cave. Knee cave leads to pelvic tilt. Pelvic tilt changes your spine. Your shoulders round and your head shifts forward. One problem at the base. Six consequences above it.
Fix the foundation first. Everything else follows. Episode 1 of the Posture From The Ground Up series. Follow so you do not miss an episode.
.
.
.

#posture #physicaltherapy #injuryprevention #strengthtraining #themusclespt
Note — this is established clinical biomechanics content. If you make specific claims about foot mechanics and postural compensation in future episodes I will pull relevant PMIDs at that point.
I DO NOT WANT TO GET BULKY — HOT TAKE
Hook
"I do not want to get bulky. I hear this every week. Here is the truth."
[TEXT ON SCREEN — I do not want to get bulky.]
Spoken
"Getting bulky is genuinely one of the hardest things a human body can do.
It requires years of progressive resistance training. A consistent caloric surplus. Optimal sleep. Dialed in nutrition. And in most cases for women — hormonal conditions that simply do not exist naturally to support that level of muscle mass.
Here is the biology. Building significant muscle mass requires testosterone as a primary anabolic driver. Women have roughly 10 to 20 times less testosterone than men. That hormonal reality makes the kind of bulk most people fear essentially impossible without pharmaceutical assistance.
What resistance training actually does for most people — it builds lean defined muscle while reducing body fat. The result is a tighter more athletic look. Not bigger. Leaner.
The people you see who look extremely muscular have typically trained for years, eaten in a surplus for years, and in many cases used performance enhancing compounds.
You are not going to accidentally become that person by picking up a dumbbell twice a week.
What you will do is build the muscle that protects your joints, drives your metabolism, and keeps you strong and independent for the rest of your life.
Do not let fear of bulk keep you from the most important thing you can do for your health."
Write Off
Most people watching this will still be afraid to lift heavy next week. The ones who save this and share it with someone who needs to hear it are the ones who actually change. Be that person.
End Card
I am the Muscle SPT. Want to build muscle and prevent injury for life. Follow for more.
Caption
Getting bulky is one of the hardest things a human body can do. It requires years of training, a consistent caloric surplus, and hormonal conditions most people simply do not have.
Women have roughly 10 to 20 times less testosterone than men. The physiology required to build the kind of bulk most people fear does not exist naturally in the female body. What resistance training actually produces for most people is a leaner more defined and more athletic physique.
Do not let fear of bulk keep you from the most important thing you can do for your long term health.
Source: Vingren et al., 2010. Testosterone Physiology in Resistance Exercise and Training. Sports Medicine — PMID 21058750

WOLFF'S LAW — EXPLAINED IN UNDER 30 SECONDS
Hook
"Wolff's Law explained in under 30 seconds."
[TEXT ON SCREEN — Wolff's Law In Under 30 Seconds]
Spoken
"Your bones are not static. They are alive. And they are constantly being broken down and rebuilt.
Think of it like a construction crew with two teams.
Team one — the osteoclasts. Their job is demolition. They break down old bone tissue continuously.
Team two — the osteoblasts. Their job is construction. They build new bone tissue in response to demand.
When you load your skeleton through resistance training your body sends a signal to the construction crew. More load here. Build more here. The osteoblasts get to work and deposit new bone exactly where the stress is applied.
Remove the load and the demolition crew wins. The osteoclasts keep breaking bone down but the signal to rebuild never comes. Over time bone density decreases.
That is Wolff's Law. Your bones adapt to the demands you place on them. Load them and the builders win. Stop loading them and the demolition crew takes over.
This is why resistance training is the most powerful tool we have against osteoporosis. Not calcium. Not supplements. Load."
Write Off
That is Wolff's Law simplified. Next video I am breaking down peak bone mass and why what you do before age 30 determines your bone health for life. Follow so you do not miss it.
End Card
I am the Muscle SPT. Want to build muscle and prevent injury for life. Follow for more.
Caption
Your bones are constantly being broken down and rebuilt by two competing cell types.
Osteoclasts break bone down. Osteoblasts build it back up. When you load your skeleton through resistance training your body signals the osteoblasts to build more bone exactly where the stress is applied. Remove the load and the osteoclasts win. Bone density decreases over time.
That is Wolff's Law. Load your bones and the builders win. Stop loading them and the demolition crew takes over. Resistance training is the most powerful tool we have against osteoporosis. Not calcium. Not supplements. Load.
.
.

The construction crew analogy works really well here because it makes osteoblasts and osteoclasts immediately understandable without anyone needing to know the words. The toddler can follow it. The clinician recognizes the accuracy. That is exactly the balance you are going for.You said: Maybe change the construction crew reference because we've used that Do something else.Maybe change the construction crew reference because we've used that Do something else. I don't know if I like that.Apr 7Claude responded: Here are some alternative analogies for the osteoblast and osteoclast concept:Here are some alternative analogies for the osteoblast and osteoclast concept:
Option 1 — The bank account
Your bone is like a bank account. Osteoclasts are the withdrawals happening every single day automatically. Osteoblasts are the deposits. Load your skeleton and deposits go up. Stop loading it and withdrawals keep coming but deposits stop. Over time your balance runs out.
Option 2 — The gardener
Think of your bone like a garden. Osteoclasts are constantly pruning — cutting back old tissue. Osteoblasts are planting — growing new bone where it is needed. When you load your skeleton you tell the gardener exactly where to plant more. Remove the load and the pruning continues but nothing new grows back.
Option 3 — The road crew
Your bones are like roads. Osteoclasts are potholes forming naturally over time. Osteoblasts are the repair crew that fills them in. But the repair crew only shows up where there is traffic — where load is being applied. No traffic means no repairs. The road deteriorates.
Option 4 — The supply and demand
Your skeleton runs on supply and demand. Osteoclasts create the demand by breaking bone down constantly. Osteoblasts are the supply — they only produce when your body sends the signal that more bone is needed. That signal is load. No load means no signal. No signal means no supply.
Which one feels most like your voice and I will rebuild the full script around it.You said: Idk you pick and give scriptIdk you pick and give scriptApr 7Claude responded: Going with the bank account.Going with the bank account. It is the simplest and most universally understood. Everyone knows what it feels like to have more going out than coming in.
WOLFF'S LAW — EXPLAINED IN UNDER 30 SECONDS
Hook
"Wolff's Law explained in under 30 seconds."
[TEXT ON SCREEN — Wolff's Law In Under 30 Seconds]
Spoken
"Your bones are not static. They are alive. And they are constantly being broken down and rebuilt by two competing cell types.
Think of your bone like a bank account.
Osteoclasts are the withdrawals. They break down old bone tissue every single day automatically. You cannot stop them.
Osteoblasts are the deposits. They build new bone tissue in response to one thing — mechanical demand. Load.
When you resistance train you place stress on your skeleton. Your body detects that stress and sends a signal to the osteoblasts. More bone needed here. Deposit more here. And they do exactly that — building new bone exactly where the load was applied.
Remove the load and the withdrawals keep coming. But the deposits stop. Over time your balance runs out. That is osteoporosis.
That is Wolff's Law. Load your skeleton and deposits win. Stop loading it and withdrawals take over.
Resistance training is the most powerful deposit you can make."
