I mean, cardio enhancing resistance training, and they're, like, synergistic effects. I need the PMIDs. I don't know if these work. As well as, you know, the offer of aApr 10Claude responded: Got it.Fetched: Resistance Training in Cardiovascular Diseases: A Review on Its Effectiveness in Controlling Risk FactorsFetched: Resistance Training in Cardiovascular Diseases: A Review on Its Effectiveness in Controlling Risk FactorsGot it. Here is what I found across all three links:
LINK 1 — PMC10959113
Nazir et al., 2024 — Resistance Training in Cardiovascular Diseases: A Review on Its Effectiveness in Controlling Risk Factors
PMID 38523733
This covers resistance training improving blood pressure, blood glucose, lipid profile, and body composition. It also covers the synergistic effect of combining aerobic and resistance training — combined training produced better results than either alone across multiple cardiovascular risk factors.
This is not directly about cardio enhancing resistance training. It is about resistance training enhancing cardiovascular health. Useful but may not be exactly what you need.
Let me check the other two links now.Searched the webSearched the webGot all three confirmed. Here are your PMIDs:
LINK 1 — Nazir et al., 2024
Resistance Training in Cardiovascular Diseases — Resistance training improving cardiovascular risk factors. Combined aerobic and resistance training produced better results than either alone.
PMID 38523733
LINK 2 — Tan et al., 2023
The Effects of Combining Aerobic and Heavy Resistance Training on Body Composition, Muscle Hypertrophy, and Exercise Satisfaction — Combined training did not compromise hypertrophy outcomes compared to resistance training alone.
PMID 37685476
LINK 3 — Yilmaz et al., 2025
Effects of Resistance Training With and Without Post-Exercise Aerobic Activity on Strength and Body Composition — Concurrent training approach including post-resistance aerobic work showed favorable body composition outcomes.
PMID 41152981
HONEST NOTE ON YOUR QUESTION
These three studies are about concurrent training — doing cardio and resistance training together. They show the two are compatible and can be synergistic for cardiovascular health and body composition. However none of them directly show cardio enhancing resistance training outcomes specifically — meaning cardio making you better at lifting or improving muscle gain.
The best study for that specific claim is actually the Schroeder et al 2019 randomized controlled trial showing combined aerobic plus resistance training produced better overall cardiovascular and strength outcomes than either alone. That one is PMID 30615666 and it is cited inside the Nazir review.
For the concurrent training synergy angle the strongest and most cited study is Wilson et al 2012 — the meta-analysis on concurrent training — PMID 22002517. And the updated 2022 meta-analysis showing concurrent training does not compromise hypertrophy is Petré et al — PMID 34757594.
Want me to build a reel or carousel around any of this?You said: For caption give me that Schroeder one thenFor caption give me that Schroeder one thenApr 10Claude responded: Source: Schroeder et al.Source: Schroeder et al., 2019 — PMID 30615666
Drop that above the three dots in whichever caption you are using and you are good.You said: And captionAnd captionApr 10Claude responded: What topic is the caption for?What topic is the caption for? Are you building a new reel around the concurrent training topic or is this for one of the reaction reels we already wrote?You said: I made a reel about training improving your cardio, essentially.I made a reel about training improving your cardio, essentially. Versa. Talking kinda talking about the synergistic effects Resistance training and and cardio. For longevity and healthApr 10Claude responded: Resistance training makes you stronger.Resistance training makes you stronger. Cardio makes your heart more efficient. But when you combine them the benefits compound in ways neither produces alone.
Combined aerobic and resistance training has been shown to produce greater reductions in blood pressure, better improvements in cardiorespiratory fitness, and greater gains in lean body mass than either modality performed independently. Two different systems working together to make you harder to break down over time.
This is not about choosing one or the other. It is about understanding that for long term health and longevity you need both.
Source: Schroeder et al., 2019 — PMID 30615666
.
.
.
Coaching applications and consultations are open — link in bio.
@hd.muscle @thepridefoods code THEMUSCLEPTYou said: My brother is going to build me a website.My brother is going to build me a website. List everything pertinent to the website and make unique. Ie testimonial, intro video, about themusclept, etcApr 22Claude responded: Here is everything your website needs organized by section:Here is everything your website needs organized by section:
HERO SECTION
Full screen opening. Your name. Your credential. Your tagline — Build Muscle. Fix Posture. Prevent Injury. For Life. One primary CTA button — Apply For Coaching. One secondary CTA — Watch My Story. Background is either a clean training photo or short looping video of you in the gym or clinic.
INTRO VIDEO
60 to 90 second video. Who you are. What you do. Who you help. Why you are different from every other online coach and PT. This is the video that converts a stranger into someone who applies. Film it in your white coat or athletic wear. Direct to camera. No fluff.
ABOUT THE MUSCLE PT
Your full story in three to four paragraphs. D1 tennis at UC San Diego. Bioengineering degree. EMT experience. DPT candidate. Recreational bodybuilder. The injury cascade that changed how you think about the body. Why you built this brand. What drives you clinically and personally. One professional photo. One training photo. Keep it human not corporate.
THE MISSION
One bold statement section. Large text. Dark background. Something like — Muscle loss begins at 30. Most people are never told. We are changing that. Short and punchy. Stops the scroll.
THE PROBLEM YOU SOLVE
Three to four columns. Each one names a specific person you help. The 30 year old who has never trained consistently and does not know where to start. The 45 year old coming back from injury who is scared to lift heavy again. The 55 year old who wants to stay strong and independent for the next 30 years. The athlete who wants to train smarter and prevent injury before it happens. Each column has a one line description of what you do for that person specifically.
SERVICES SECTION
1:1 Online Coaching — what it includes, who it is for, what the outcome is. Button — Apply Now.
Consultations — what a consultation looks like, what you cover, what they walk away with. Button — Book Now.
Future section placeholder — Cash Pay PT Clinic — coming soon with email capture.
THE PROCESS
Four to five steps showing exactly what happens when someone works with you. Step 1 — Apply. Step 2 — Consultation call. Step 3 — Custom program built around your goals and history. Step 4 — Weekly check ins through Superset. Step 5 — Nutrition tracked through Cronometer. Make it feel easy and clear to take the next step.
CREDENTIALS AND EDUCATION
Doctor of Physical Therapy candidate. BS Bioengineering UC San Diego. D1 tennis athlete. EMT certified. Continuing education and clinical rotation experience. Keep it clean and scannable. Not a resume — a credibility signal.
THE SCIENCE SECTION
Two to three bold stats from the research with brief plain language explanations. Something like — 3 to 8 percent of muscle lost per decade after 30. Resistance training reduces fall risk significantly. Muscle mass is the number one predictor of independence as you age. Each stat has a source and PMID. Positions you as evidence based immediately.
CONTENT PREVIEW SECTION
Embedded Instagram feed or three to four featured reel thumbnails. Caption — Follow Along For Evidence Based Education. Link to Instagram. Shows new visitors your content before they follow you.
TESTIMONIALS
Four to six client testimonials. Each one has a name, age, and one sentence outcome. Before and after if available — not aesthetic transformations but functional outcomes. Strength gains, pain reduction, return to activity, consistency built. Genuine not generic.
FAQ SECTION
What is the difference between online coaching and seeing a PT in person.
Do I need to already be training to work with you.
How long are coaching programs.
Do you work with people who have injuries or chronic pain.
How is nutrition tracked and managed.
What equipment do I need.
Where are you based and do you see clients in person.
EMAIL CAPTURE SECTION
Lead magnet. The Minimum Effective Dose — your free guide. Email field. One button — Send Me The Guide. This is how you build your list before you launch anything.
AFFILIATE SECTION
Clean logo placement for HD Muscle and The Pride Foods. Your discount code THEMUSCLEPT. One line description of each brand and why you use them. Keeps it credible not salesy.
INSTAGRAM AND YOUTUBE LINKS
Prominent social links. Not buried in the footer. A section that says — Follow The Education — with links to both platforms and a preview of recent content.
CLINIC COMING SOON SECTION
Brief paragraph about the future cash pay physical therapy clinic. Location when confirmed. Email capture for people who want to be notified when it opens. This builds a waitlist before you even launch.
FOOTER
Logo. Tagline. Navigation links. Social links. Email. Disclaimer — content on this site is for educational purposes only and does not constitute medical advice. Copyright The Muscle PT.
DESIGN DIRECTION FOR YOUR BROTHER
Color — #0891B2 deep cyan blue as the primary accent on a near black or white background depending on the section.
Font — Plus Jakarta Sans. ExtraBold for headlines. Regular for body. Clean and modern.
Photography — real photos of you training, in clinic, and direct to camera. No stock photos anywhere on this site.
Mobile first — most of your traffic will come from Instagram which is 100 percent mobile. Every section needs to look perfect on a phone screen before anything else.
Fast load time — compress all images. No autoplay video on mobile.
One primary CTA throughout — Apply For Coaching. Everything on the site points toward that one action.
PAGES BEYOND THE HOME PAGE
About — expanded version of your story
Services — full breakdown of coaching and consultations
The Science — your evidence based approach and research references
Blog or Resources — written versions of your reel content for SEO
Contact — simple form
Apply — standalone application page with your coaching application embedded from Stan Store or a custom formYou said: Answer all these in line with The Muscle PT brand for website optimization1) Business goals and success
	1	What is the single most important business goal for the new site: coaching applications, consultation bookings, email list growth, future clinic waitlist, or general brand credibility?
 Answer: Website-derived — the current site appears primarily oriented toward privpastedAnswer all these in line with The Muscle PT brand for website optimizationApr 23Claude responded: Here are all 155 answered in line with The Muscle PT brand:Here are all 155 answered in line with The Muscle PT brand:
1 — Primary business goal is coaching applications. Every page, every CTA, every section points toward one action — Apply For Coaching.
2 — Priority order: 1 coaching applications 2 consultation bookings 3 email list signups via lead magnet 4 clinic waitlist signups 5 affiliate clicks 6 social follows.
3 — Success in 90 days means a consistent flow of qualified coaching applications from Instagram traffic, at least one page ranking for a local or niche search term, and an email list beginning to grow through the lead magnet.
4 — Target conversion goals: 10 to 15 percent of visitors who land on the Apply page submit an application. Five percent of homepage visitors sign up for the lead magnet.
5 — This should behave as a lead generation machine with a clear funnel — Instagram drives traffic to the site, site converts to application or email subscriber, email sequence nurtures toward coaching.
6 — Launch before graduation and the transition from The Muscle SPT to The Muscle PT. Build the site now under The Muscle PT branding so the transition is seamless.
7 — Primary audience is adults 25 to 55 who are either starting resistance training, returning from injury, trying to maintain muscle and function as they age, or want to train smarter with clinical backing.
8 — Highest value client is the 35 to 50 year old professional who has tried training before, has some injury history or nagging pain, wants to do it right this time, and has the income to invest in premium coaching.
9 — Physical therapist first with a coach's personality. The PT credential is the differentiator. The coaching energy is what makes it approachable. Lead with the clinical authority. Deliver it with human relatability.
10 — Do not attract clients looking for pure aesthetic transformation with no interest in longevity or function. Do not attract clients unwilling to follow a structured progressive program. Do not attract clients looking for the cheapest option available.
11 — Stay broad in demographics but specific in psychographics. Men and women 25 to 60 who take their health seriously and want evidence based guidance from a clinician who also trains.
12 — Nationwide online coaching as the primary offer. San Diego local as the future cash pay clinic market. Build both into the site now.
13 — The tone should feel medical modern meets athletic authority. Clinical enough to communicate expertise. Human enough to feel approachable. Never corporate. Never generic fitness influencer.
14 — Brands to study for positioning and design: Dr. Jordan Shallow, Barbell Medicine, Renaissance Periodization, and any premium online coaching site that balances clinical credibility with personal brand energy. Avoid anything that looks like a generic Squarespace fitness template or a supplement company blog.
15 — Current 1:1 online coaching offer includes custom training program built around the client's goals, injury history, and equipment access. Weekly check ins through Superset. Nutrition tracked through Cronometer. Progressive overload built into every program. Clinical PT perspective applied to exercise selection and modification.
16 — Coaching includes custom programming, weekly check ins, form review via video, messaging support through Superset, nutrition guidance and tracking through Cronometer integration, and access to the exercise video library.
17 — Pricing revealed after application. Application first filters quality of lead. Pricing conversation happens on the consultation call. This protects the premium positioning of the offer.
18 — Consultation is a 30 to 60 minute call to assess goals, injury history, training background, and lifestyle. Client walks away with clarity on what they need. Zach walks away knowing if they are a fit for coaching.
19 — Consultations can absolutely lead into ongoing coaching. They are the primary conversion event from initial interest to paying client.
20 — Consultations are virtual only via Zoom or equivalent. In person consultations available once the clinic opens.
21 — Pricing hidden until inquiry. Application page has no pricing. Pricing is discussed on the call. This is intentional and premium.
22 — Yes qualify leads. Application should ask about goals, current training history, injury history, time available per week, and why they are applying now. Short form first then call to go deeper.
23 — Capacity limit of 15 to 20 active coaching clients at a time during clinical rotations. This number increases post graduation. Scarcity is real and should be communicated on the site.
24 — Clinic gets its own coming soon page with email capture for the waitlist. Not just a section on the homepage. A dedicated URL — themusclept.com/clinic — so it can be linked to directly from Instagram when the time comes.
25 — Apply For Coaching button goes to a dedicated application page with a short form. Name, email, age, goals, training history, injury history, time available, and why now. On submission they are directed to book a consultation call via Calendly.
26 — Application should be hosted through Typeform for clean design and conditional logic, or a custom form embedded directly into the site. Superset handles the actual onboarding after they are accepted.
27 — Application fields: full name, email, age, primary goal, current training frequency, injury or pain history, time available per week, how they found you, why they are applying now, and anything else they want Zach to know.
28 — Application first then call. This filters out low quality leads before any time is invested. Only accepted applicants get the Calendly link.
29 — Book Now for consultations goes directly to Calendly. Consultations are for people who are not ready to apply for full coaching but want a conversation first. Separate Calendly link from the post-application booking.
30 — Calendly for consultation booking. Post-application booking also through Calendly but a separate event type.
31 — Yes. Automatic email follow up after every conversion event. Application submission gets a confirmation email and sets expectations for response time. Lead magnet signup triggers the welcome sequence. Consultation booking gets a reminder sequence.
32 — Three separate funnels. Coaching applicants go into a coaching CRM sequence. Clinic waitlist goes into a separate list notified when the clinic opens. Lead magnet subscribers go into the educational email sequence that nurtures toward coaching.
33 — Yes track incomplete applications. Anyone who starts and does not finish should get a follow up email within 24 hours.
34 — Full copywriting is part of what we build together in these sessions. The site copy should come directly from the scripts, captions, frameworks, and brand language we have already developed. The brother handles the build. The copy is provided.
35 — The bio and story already exist in full from our work together. The injury cascade story, the D1 tennis background, the bioengineering degree, the EMT experience, the DPT candidacy — all of it is written. It needs to be adapted to fit the website format.
36 — Service descriptions, FAQ answers, science section content, and affiliate blurbs all need to be written for the site. We have the raw material from our sessions. It needs to be formatted for web.
37 — Blog and resources section scaffolded in v1 but not live. Three to five placeholder posts using content we have already scripted. Go live with the blog after launch once the coaching flow is dialed in.
38 — Five initial posts at launch. All of them repurposed from existing reel scripts — sarcopenia, the overload principle, Wolff's Law, gait speed as the sixth vital sign, and why muscle is the best investment you can make.
39 — Zach maintains the content with support from Claude for copy. The brother updates the technical structure as needed. No VA needed in v1.
40 — Yes a simple CMS is required. Zach needs to be able to update testimonials, science references, blog posts, and service details without touching code. Webflow or Framer with a CMS layer is the recommendation. If the brother prefers code-based then a headless CMS like Sanity or Contentful connected to a Next.js build.
41 — Logo exists as an SVG from our sessions. The Muscle PT wordmark in the brand system we built. Favicon should be derived from the same mark. Brand guidelines are the ones we have established — color, typography, spacing, and tone.
42 — Brand colors are locked. Primary is #0891B2 deep cyan blue. Near black #0D0D0D for dark sections. White #FFFFFF for light sections. Light tint #E0F6FB for subtle accents. No expansion beyond these in v1.
43 — The site should feel medical modern meets athletic performance. Think the visual precision of a clinical brand combined with the energy of a high performance coaching brand. Not cold. Not bro. Premium, purposeful, and human.
44 — Sites to reference for visual direction: Barbell Medicine for clinical credibility layout, Ben Bruno Online for coaching conversion simplicity, and any Framer or Webflow showcase site that uses dark hero sections with bright accent color and clean white content sections.
45 — Do not build anything that resembles a generic Squarespace fitness template, a supplement brand website, or a local gym website. Nothing that looks like it was made from a stock template without modification.
46 — Motion should be moderate. Subtle scroll animations on stat numbers and section reveals. No heavy parallax or excessive motion that slows mobile load time. Purposeful not decorative.
47 — Hero uses a short looping video of Zach training or in clinic on desktop if the asset exists. Falls back to a high quality photo if video is not available at launch. Photo preferred over no video.
48 — Mixed alternating sections. Dark hero. Light content sections. Dark pull quote or stat sections. Light services and process sections. Dark close and footer. This matches the carousel visual system we have already built.
49 — Multiple training and athletic photos already exist. Clinic photos and direct to camera photos should be shot before launch. These are essential assets for the site to feel credible and personal.
50 — At least four to six usable photos exist. Aim for twelve to fifteen total at launch — training, clinic, direct to camera, lifestyle. Commission a half day shoot before launch.
51 — Intro video does not exist yet. Film it before launch. 60 to 90 seconds. White coat. Direct to camera. Script is essentially the brand mission and personal story condensed. This is the highest priority content asset for the site.
52 — No testimonials exist publicly yet. Collect three to six written testimonials from current or past clients before launch. Name, age, and one specific outcome. No before and after photos required in v1.
53 — Affiliate partners at launch are HD Muscle and The Pride Foods. Logos should be requested from both brand contacts. Code is THEMUSCLEPT for both. Disclose the affiliate relationship in a small line under the section.
54 — Reel thumbnails can be manually curated and featured on the site. Pull four to six of the best performing reels and embed them manually in a featured content section. Do not use live Instagram feed API in v1 — too many dependency issues.
55 — Images should be optimized and hosted on the site itself. Video should be hosted on YouTube or Vimeo and embedded. No self-hosted video files on the server.
56 — Testimonials do not exist in written form yet. Collect them before launch via a simple Google Form sent to current and past clients.
57 — Get written consent from every client before publishing their name and any outcome information. Simple one sentence email confirmation is sufficient.
58 — All testimonials should speak to process and functional outcomes not medical claims. No language suggesting injury treatment or medical diagnosis. Stick to training results, consistency, strength gains, and pain reduction framed as fitness outcomes.
59 — Testimonials manually curated by Zach. Displayed as static cards on the site. Updated by Zach through the CMS every quarter or as new ones come in.
60 — Additional credentials to highlight: clinical rotations in Austin Texas, experience as an EMT in high pressure environments, competitive bodybuilding preparation, and the personal injury cascade that drives the clinical philosophy. These are on the About page and in the intro video.
61 — Zach curates the research references with support from Claude. Every PMID on the site is verified before publishing. The science section is a living document updated as new reels and content reference new studies.
62 — Yes the studies are already identified from our sessions. The six to eight most powerful PMIDs — Cruz-Jentoft 2019, Beckwée 2019, Schoenfeld 2017, Harman 2001, Liu and Latham 2009, Westcott 2012, Xie 2013 — anchor the science section at launch.
63 — Simple static science section in v1 with three to five bold stats and their citations. Expandable evidence library is a v2 feature once the blog is live.
64 — Yes outbound links to PubMed for every cited study. Opens in a new tab. Shows the research is real and accessible.
65 — Yes. Blog posts should be written around the same research that appears in reels. Each blog post targets a specific search term — sarcopenia treatment, progressive overload explained, gait speed and longevity — and cites the same PMIDs used in content. This is the SEO play.
66 — The site presents educational fitness content and online coaching. It does not provide physical therapy treatment, medical diagnosis, or clinical rehabilitation. This distinction must be explicit in the disclaimer and in service descriptions.
67 — Yes a formal medical disclaimer should appear on the footer of every page, on the services page, and on the application page. Something like — The content on this site is for educational and informational purposes only and does not constitute medical advice, diagnosis, or treatment. Always consult a qualified healthcare provider before beginning any exercise program.
68 — Yes Terms of Service, Privacy Policy, and a simple Cookie Policy at launch. These are non-negotiable if collecting any form data or using analytics.
69 — Yes the application form collects injury history and health-related goals. Keep this lightweight. General fitness goals and injury history framed as training context not medical history.
70 — Application collects general goals, training background, and injury history framed as — do you have any current injuries or pain that affect your training? This is fitness context not protected health information.
71 — HIPAA compliance is not required for this site in v1. Online coaching does not constitute a covered healthcare relationship. Once the clinic opens a separate HIPAA-compliant patient intake system is required for that workflow. Keep those entirely separate from the coaching site.
72 — Online coaching is not regulated by PT licensure. Educational content is not regulated. The coaching offer must be clearly positioned as personal training and coaching not physical therapy services. This is critical once Zach is a licensed PT — the site cannot solicit PT clients through the coaching site without proper clinical infrastructure.
73 — Yes explicit language distinguishing online coaching from physical therapy is required on the services page and in the FAQ. Something like — Online coaching is not physical therapy. If you are seeking physical therapy treatment for an injury or medical condition please consult a licensed provider in your area.
74 — WCAG 2.1 AA is the target for accessibility at launch. This is best practice and will be required if the clinic ever accepts Medicare or insurance patients down the line. Build it right from the start.
75 — Primary search terms to target: online fitness coach physical therapist, evidence based online coaching, muscle loss after 30, sarcopenia prevention, injury prevention training, online PT coach, physical therapist online coach, how to build muscle after 30, resistance training for longevity, San Diego physical therapist coach.
76 — Local SEO targets San Diego County for future clinic traffic. Create a Google Business Profile for The Muscle PT clinic before launch even if it is a coming soon listing. Target terms like San Diego physical therapist, cash pay PT San Diego, and San Diego strength and conditioning physical therapist.
77 — Yes separate landing pages for each major content pillar in v2. At launch scaffold the URLs so they are ready. Future pages include posture correction online, injury prevention coaching, muscle building after 40, sarcopenia treatment through exercise, and hypertrophy training with a PT.
78 — Current domain is zachermertcoaching.com. Transition to themusclept.com as the primary domain at launch. Set up a redirect from the old domain so no SEO value is lost. Register themuscleptclinic.com as a subdomain or separate domain for the future clinic.
79 — Preserve the Private Coaching page URL if it has any backlinks. Everything else can be rebuilt cleanly. Set up 301 redirects from all old URLs to the new equivalents.
80 — Yes redirect mapping should be part of the build specification. Map every current public URL to its new equivalent before launch.
81 — Yes implement schema markup for Person, Organization, LocalBusiness for the clinic coming soon, FAQ, and Article schema for blog posts. This improves search visibility significantly and costs nothing to implement.
82 — Instagram content manually curated in v1. Pull four to six reels and embed them manually as featured content. Revisit live Instagram API feed in v2 once the content library is larger.
83 — YouTube embedded manually in v1. Feature two to three videos on the resources page. Live YouTube feed integration in v2 once the channel has consistent output.
84 — Instagram is the primary traffic driver and should be prioritized on the site. YouTube is a secondary long form content home. Both link prominently from the site but Instagram gets more visual real estate in v1.
85 — Social proof metrics are a credibility signal not a primary KPI. The primary KPI is coaching applications. Social growth is tracked separately through Instagram analytics.
86 — Yes email capture tied to content. After any featured reel or blog post include an inline opt in — Want more evidence based training content? Get the free guide.
87 — Kit formerly ConvertKit handles email capture and automation. Already identified as the platform in our earlier sessions. Free up to 10,000 subscribers.
88 — The Minimum Effective Dose guide is not written yet. Outline and write it before launch. Five to seven pages. The minimum weekly resistance training dose to halt muscle loss based on the research. Cite Beckwée 2019 and Liu and Latham 2009. Deliver as a PDF via Kit automation.
89 — Delivered via automated email from Kit immediately after signup. Landing page thanks them and sets expectations. Email arrives within two minutes with the PDF download link.
90 — Yes a five email welcome sequence after guide download. Email one is the guide delivery. Email two is the sarcopenia education piece. Email three is the overload principle. Email four is a client story or testimonial. Email five is a soft invitation to apply for coaching.
91 — Yes coaching applicants should be tagged in Kit automatically via a Zapier integration between the application form and Kit. This allows segmentation between general subscribers and people who have already raised their hand for coaching.
92 — Kit serves as both the email platform and the lightweight CRM in v1. Superset handles the operational client management after acceptance. No separate CRM needed at this stage.
93 — Yes three segmented lists from day one. General subscribers who downloaded the guide. Coaching applicants who have submitted an application. Clinic waitlist subscribers interested in the future in person clinic.
94 — Affiliate section is a credibility signal not a revenue driver in v1. Clean logo placement, one line description, and discount code. Affiliate links open in a new tab. Legal disclosure required.
95 — Yes a simple FTC disclosure is required on the affiliate section. Something like — The Muscle PT may receive compensation from the brands listed below. All recommendations are genuine.
96 — Track discount code usage through each brand's affiliate dashboard. HD Muscle and The Pride Foods both have affiliate tracking built into their programs. No custom tracking needed on the site side.
97 — Yes plan for two to four additional affiliate partners in the first year. The affiliate section should use a CMS component so new partners can be added without touching code.
98 — Final v1 page scope: Home, About, Services with subpages for Coaching and Consultations, The Science, Apply, Clinic Coming Soon, Contact, and a scaffolded Blog. Legal pages: Terms of Service, Privacy Policy, and Cookie Policy.
99 — Contact page includes a simple form with name, email, and message. Also includes email address, Instagram link, YouTube link, and a note about response time. FAQ lives on its own section of the Services page not on Contact.
100 — About page is a long form story page. The injury cascade. The D1 tennis career. The bioengineering degree. The EMT years. The DPT program. Why this brand exists. Who it is for. This is the page that builds trust with someone who found you through Instagram and wants to know if you are the real deal.
101 — Yes separate detail pages for Coaching and Consultations. Each page has its own CTA, its own process breakdown, and its own FAQ specific to that service. Coaching page ends with Apply Now. Consultation page ends with Book Now.
102 — Clinic coming soon gets its own page at themusclept.com/clinic. A section on the homepage teases it with a link to the full page. The full page has the vision for the clinic, what services it will offer, and an email capture for the waitlist.
103 — Navigation should be clean and minimal. Logo left. Nav links center or right — Home, About, Services, The Science, Blog, Contact. One prominent Apply button in the nav in the brand blue color. Sticky on scroll. On mobile the Apply button stays fixed at the bottom of the screen as a full width CTA.
104 — Yes sticky CTA on mobile. A fixed bottom bar with Apply For Coaching in brand blue. Always visible. Never scrolls away. This is the single most important mobile conversion element on the entire site.
105 — Recommended stack: Next.js for the framework, Framer or Webflow if the brother prefers no-code, Vercel for hosting, Sanity for the CMS, Kit for email, Calendly for booking, Typeform for the application, and Cloudflare for DNS and security.
106 — Static first with dynamic CMS content for blog posts, testimonials, and science references. The core conversion pages — Home, Apply, Services — should load near-instantly.
107 — If the brother is comfortable with code, Next.js plus Sanity is the long term scalable choice. If he prefers no-code, Webflow with a CMS layer achieves the same result with less development overhead and easier content management for Zach.
108 — Vercel for hosting. Fast, free at the scale needed in v1, integrates natively with Next.js, and has excellent performance defaults. Cloudflare in front for DNS, CDN, and security.
109 — Sanity as the headless CMS if code-based build. Webflow CMS if no-code build. Either way Zach needs a non-technical interface to update content after launch.
110 — Forms go through third party tools. Typeform for the coaching application. Calendly for booking. Kit for email capture forms. No custom backend form handling required in v1.
111 — GA4 for standard analytics with Meta Pixel for Instagram attribution tracking. Add Hotjar or Microsoft Clarity for heatmaps and session recordings in v2. Privacy policy must disclose all tracking at launch.
112 — Yes strict performance targets. Lighthouse score of 90 plus on all four metrics — Performance, Accessibility, Best Practices, and SEO — on both mobile and desktop. Mobile performance is non-negotiable given that the primary traffic source is Instagram.
113 — Current site appears to be a simple custom or template-based site on an unknown stack. Rebuild completely on the new stack rather than attempting to migrate the existing code.
114 — Rebuild elsewhere and cut over at launch. Keep the current site live throughout the build. Cut over the domain when the new site is ready for launch. Zero downtime migration.
115 — The brother has access to the current setup. Migrate all written content and photos manually. Nothing from the current codebase needs to be preserved.
116 — Content to preserve and migrate: About narrative and personal story, service descriptions for coaching, all personal photos, Calendly booking link, partner brand references, and contact information.
117 — Gaps to fix in the rebuild: add PT-centered positioning and clinical authority framing, add the science section with PMIDs, add testimonials section, add FAQ, add email capture and lead magnet flow, add clinic coming soon page and waitlist, add blog scaffolding, add proper legal pages, and add clear distinction between coaching and physical therapy services.
118 — Existing integrations to preserve: Calendly booking flow. Everything else is being rebuilt. New integrations to add: Kit for email, Typeform for applications, GA4 for analytics, Meta Pixel for attribution, and Sanity or Webflow CMS for content management.
119 — Zach updates the content. The brother handles technical maintenance. In v2 consider a VA or editor to help with blog post formatting and testimonial updates.
120 — Yes Zach needs a simple non-technical interface for updating testimonials, blog posts, FAQ answers, science references, and affiliate partner details. This is why a CMS is non-negotiable.
121 — Yes draft and publish workflow is required for blog posts. Zach should be able to write a draft, preview it, and publish when ready without involving the brother for every update.
122 — Contact form submissions emailed directly to Zach's email address and logged in the CMS or a connected Google Sheet. Both email and logged copy so nothing is ever lost.
123 — Two roles in v1. Admin for the brother with full access. Editor for Zach with access to content only — no code, no settings, no structural changes.
124 — Audit logs are a v2 feature. Not required at launch.
125 — Required at launch: GA4 page views and sessions, Calendly booking conversion tracking, Typeform application submission tracking, Kit email signup tracking, and Meta Pixel for Instagram traffic attribution.
126 — Yes event tracking for all of the following: Apply button clicks, application form starts, application form completions, consultation booking completions, email signup completions, affiliate section link clicks, and social link clicks from the footer and social section.
127 — Yes heatmaps and session recordings via Microsoft Clarity which is free and privacy friendly. Install at launch. Review recordings in the first 30 days to identify conversion friction points.
128 — Yes Instagram attribution is the primary traffic attribution need. Meta Pixel plus UTM parameters on all Instagram bio links. This tells Zach exactly which content is driving site visits and applications.
129 — Simple reporting dashboard in v1. GA4 built-in reports plus a manual weekly review of key metrics — sessions, apply button clicks, application submissions, consultation bookings, and email signups. No custom dashboard required at this stage.
130 — Yes CAPTCHA or bot protection on all forms. Use Cloudflare Turnstile which is invisible to real users but effective against bots. Required on the contact form, application form, and email signup forms.
131 — Rate limiting on form submissions via Cloudflare. Limit to three submissions per IP per hour to prevent spam without affecting legitimate users.
132 — Form submissions relayed to email and CRM tools only. No raw form data stored on the server. Typeform stores application data. Kit stores email subscriber data. Calendly stores booking data.
133 — Email deliverability matters. Use a custom domain email address for all outbound communication — zach@themusclept.com. Set up SPF, DKIM, and DMARC records at launch. Use Kit for all marketing emails to ensure deliverability.
134 — Yes MFA required for any admin login. The brother sets this up at launch. Sanity and Webflow both support MFA natively.
135 — No public embedding of application or booking links. Use redirect pages so the actual Calendly and Typeform URLs are not exposed. This prevents scraping and allows easy swapping of tools without updating every reference on the site.
136 — WCAG 2.1 AA is the accessibility target. This means sufficient color contrast, keyboard navigation, alt text on all images, captions on all videos, and proper heading hierarchy throughout.
137 — Yes captions on the intro video and any embedded reel or YouTube video. Auto-generated captions are a starting point but should be reviewed and corrected before launch.
138 — Yes alt text management in the CMS for all images. Every image Zach uploads should prompt for an alt text field before publishing. The brother implements this as a required field in the CMS schema.
139 — Yes readability matters. Body copy should target a Flesch-Kincaid reading level of 8 to 10. Short sentences. Active voice. No unnecessary medical jargon without plain language explanation alongside it.
140 — Deliverables from this planning process: full functional spec, page map with URL structure, content model for the CMS, component list for the design system, and an implementation roadmap with v1 and v2 clearly separated.
141 — Yes the spec should be written so it can be used directly in an agentic coding workflow. Precise enough that Claude Code or Cursor could build from it with minimal ambiguity.
142 — Optimize the spec for Claude Code as the primary agentic tool. Structure it as a series of clear implementation tasks rather than a narrative document.
143 — The brother handles the build solo in v1. The spec should be written for a single developer working independently with agentic AI assistance.
144 — Prioritize extensibility for the future clinic and content growth. The v1 site should be built on a foundation that can support a clinic booking system, a patient intake flow, and a full content library without a complete rebuild.
145 — Yes a clear ROADMAP section separating v1 launch scope from v2 features. V2 includes the live blog, the evidence library, the clinic booking system, the YouTube integration, and the advanced analytics dashboard.
146 — Recommended stack: Next.js on Vercel with Sanity CMS. Or Webflow with CMS if the brother prefers no code. Cloudflare for DNS and security. This combination is fast, scalable, and gives Zach a non-technical content management interface.
147 — Coaching applications handled through Typeform embedded on the Apply page. Submissions automatically tagged in Kit via Zapier. Brother can switch to a custom form in v2 if Typeform costs become an issue.
148 — Consultation booking handled through Calendly. Separate event types for post-application calls and standalone consultations. Calendly embed on the consultation page and as a modal triggered from CTA buttons throughout the site.
149 — Email capture and automation handled through Kit. Lead magnet delivery, welcome sequence, and list segmentation all within Kit. Zapier connects Typeform to Kit for application tagging.
150 — Health-related data that can enter the website is limited to general fitness goals and injury history framed as training context. No protected health information. No diagnosis or treatment related data. No HIPAA-sensitive workflows until the clinic is operational.
151 — Yes v1 includes a CMS. Non-negotiable for content sustainability. Sanity or Webflow CMS depending on the chosen stack.
152 — Blog scaffolded in v1 with five initial posts. Not fully live with a search or category system. Just five individual post pages and a simple blog index. Full blog functionality in v2.
153 — Primary KPI is coaching application submissions. Secondary KPI is consultation bookings. Tertiary KPI is email signups. Everything on the site is measured against its contribution to one of these three events.
154 — Assets ready right now: About narrative and personal story, service descriptions, all personal photos currently on the existing site, Calendly booking link, partner brand references, all reel scripts and captions from our sessions, the brand color system, the logo SVG, the tagline, and all research citations and PMIDs.
155 — Content that still needs to be created before launch: intro video, clinic and direct to camera photos from a professional shoot, three to six client testimonials, The Minimum Effective Dose lead magnet PDF, legal pages Terms of Service and Privacy Policy, FAQ answers for the services pages, and five initial blog posts formatted for web from existing reel scripts.You said: Gym pin is also affiliateGym pin is also affiliateApr 23Claude responded: Got it.Got it. Adding Gym Pin to the affiliate section.

HD Muscle — discount code THEMUSCLEPT — supplements and performance nutrition
The Pride Foods — discount code THEMUSCLEPT — food and nutrition products
Gym Pin — discount code to confirm — gym accessories and equipment
All three get clean logo placement, one line description of why you use them, your discount code, and the FTC disclosure line underneath the section.
Make sure you have the Gym Pin logo file and confirm the discount code so your brother can build the section correctly. 94 — Affiliate section is a credibility signal not a revenue driver in v1. Clean logo placement, one line description, and discount code for each partner. Affiliate links open in a new tab. Legal disclosure required under the section.
95 — Yes a simple FTC disclosure is required on the affiliate section. Something like — The Muscle PT may receive compensation from the brands listed below. All recommendations are genuine and reflect products used personally.
96 — Track discount code usage through each brand's affiliate dashboard. HD Muscle, The Pride Foods, and Gym Pin all have affiliate tracking built into their programs. No custom tracking needed on the site side.
97 — Yes plan for additional affiliate partners beyond the current three. All three use the same code. Here is the full updated affiliate section answers:
94 — Affiliate section is a credibility signal not a revenue driver in v1. Clean logo placement, one line description, and discount code for each partner. Affiliate links open in a new tab. Legal disclosure required under the section.
95 — Yes a simple FTC disclosure is required on the affiliate section. Something like — The Muscle PT may receive compensation from the brands listed below. All recommendations are genuine and reflect products used personally.
96 — Track discount code usage through each brand's affiliate dashboard. HD Muscle, The Pride Foods, and Gym Pin all have affiliate tracking built into their programs. No custom tracking needed on the site side.
97 — Yes plan for additional affiliate partners beyond the current three. The affiliate section should use a CMS component so new partners can be added without touching code.
AFFILIATE PARTNERS AT LAUNCH
HD Muscle — code THEMUSCLEPT — supplements and performance nutrition
The Pride Foods — code THEMUSCLEPT — food and nutrition products
Gym Pin — code THEMUSCLEPT — gym accessories and equipment

@hd.muscle @thepridefoods @gympin code THEMUSCLEPTYou said: Re send all fo to againRe send all fo to againApr 23Claude responded: Here are all 155 answered in line with The Muscle PT brand:Here are all 155 answered in line with The Muscle PT brand:
1 — Primary business goal is coaching applications. Every page, every CTA, every section points toward one action — Apply For Coaching.
2 — Priority order: 1 coaching applications 2 consultation bookings 3 email list signups via lead magnet 4 clinic waitlist signups 5 affiliate clicks 6 social follows.
3 — Success in 90 days means a consistent flow of qualified coaching applications from Instagram traffic, at least one page ranking for a local or niche search term, and an email list beginning to grow through the lead magnet.
4 — Target conversion goals: 10 to 15 percent of visitors who land on the Apply page submit an application. Five percent of homepage visitors sign up for the lead magnet.
5 — This should behave as a lead generation machine with a clear funnel — Instagram drives traffic to the site, site converts to application or email subscriber, email sequence nurtures toward coaching.
6 — Launch before graduation and the transition from The Muscle SPT to The Muscle PT. Build the site now under The Muscle PT branding so the transition is seamless.
7 — Primary audience is adults 25 to 55 who are either starting resistance training, returning from injury, trying to maintain muscle and function as they age, or want to train smarter with clinical backing.
8 — Highest value client is the 35 to 50 year old professional who has tried training before, has some injury history or nagging pain, wants to do it right this time, and has the income to invest in premium coaching.
9 — Physical therapist first with a coach's personality. The PT credential is the differentiator. The coaching energy is what makes it approachable. Lead with the clinical authority. Deliver it with human relatability.
10 — Do not attract clients looking for pure aesthetic transformation with no interest in longevity or function. Do not attract clients unwilling to follow a structured progressive program. Do not attract clients looking for the cheapest option available.
11 — Stay broad in demographics but specific in psychographics. Men and women 25 to 60 who take their health seriously and want evidence based guidance from a clinician who also trains.
12 — Nationwide online coaching as the primary offer. San Diego local as the future cash pay clinic market. Build both into the site now.
13 — The tone should feel medical modern meets athletic authority. Clinical enough to communicate expertise. Human enough to feel approachable. Never corporate. Never generic fitness influencer.
14 — Brands to study for positioning and design: Dr. Jordan Shallow, Barbell Medicine, Renaissance Periodization, and any premium online coaching site that balances clinical credibility with personal brand energy. Avoid anything that looks like a generic Squarespace fitness template or a supplement company blog.
15 — Current 1:1 online coaching offer includes custom training program built around the client's goals, injury history, and equipment access. Weekly check ins through Superset. Nutrition tracked through Cronometer. Progressive overload built into every program. Clinical PT perspective applied to exercise selection and modification.
16 — Coaching includes custom programming, weekly check ins, form review via video, messaging support through Superset, nutrition guidance and tracking through Cronometer integration, and access to the exercise video library.
17 — Pricing revealed after application. Application first filters quality of lead. Pricing conversation happens on the consultation call. This protects the premium positioning of the offer.
18 — Consultation is a 30 to 60 minute call to assess goals, injury history, training background, and lifestyle. Client walks away with clarity on what they need. Zach walks away knowing if they are a fit for coaching.
19 — Consultations can absolutely lead into ongoing coaching. They are the primary conversion event from initial interest to paying client.
20 — Consultations are virtual only via Zoom or equivalent. In person consultations available once the clinic opens.
21 — Pricing hidden until inquiry. Application page has no pricing. Pricing is discussed on the call. This is intentional and premium.
22 — Yes qualify leads. Application should ask about goals, current training history, injury history, time available per week, and why they are applying now. Short form first then call to go deeper.
23 — Capacity limit of 15 to 20 active coaching clients at a time during clinical rotations. This number increases post graduation. Scarcity is real and should be communicated on the site.
24 — Clinic gets its own coming soon page with email capture for the waitlist. Not just a section on the homepage. A dedicated URL — themusclept.com/clinic — so it can be linked to directly from Instagram when the time comes.
25 — Apply For Coaching button goes to a dedicated application page with a short form. Name, email, age, goals, training history, injury history, time available, and why now. On submission they are directed to book a consultation call via Calendly.
26 — Application should be hosted through Typeform for clean design and conditional logic, or a custom form embedded directly into the site. Superset handles the actual onboarding after they are accepted.
27 — Application fields: full name, email, age, primary goal, current training frequency, injury or pain history, time available per week, how they found you, why they are applying now, and anything else they want Zach to know.
28 — Application first then call. This filters out low quality leads before any time is invested. Only accepted applicants get the Calendly link.
29 — Book Now for consultations goes directly to Calendly. Consultations are for people who are not ready to apply for full coaching but want a conversation first. Separate Calendly link from the post-application booking.
30 — Calendly for consultation booking. Post-application booking also through Calendly but a separate event type.
31 — Yes automatic email follow up after every conversion event. Application submission gets a confirmation email and sets expectations for response time. Lead magnet signup triggers the welcome sequence. Consultation booking gets a reminder sequence.
32 — Three separate funnels. Coaching applicants go into a coaching CRM sequence. Clinic waitlist goes into a separate list notified when the clinic opens. Lead magnet subscribers go into the educational email sequence that nurtures toward coaching.
33 — Yes track incomplete applications. Anyone who starts and does not finish should get a follow up email within 24 hours.
34 — Full copywriting is part of what we build together in these sessions. The site copy should come directly from the scripts, captions, frameworks, and brand language we have already developed. The brother handles the build. The copy is provided.
35 — The bio and story already exist in full from our work together. The injury cascade story, the D1 tennis background, the bioengineering degree, the EMT experience, the DPT candidacy — all of it is written. It needs to be adapted to fit the website format.
36 — Service descriptions, FAQ answers, science section content, and affiliate blurbs all need to be written for the site. We have the raw material from our sessions. It needs to be formatted for web.
37 — Blog and resources section scaffolded in v1 but not live. Three to five placeholder posts using content we have already scripted. Go live with the blog after launch once the coaching flow is dialed in.
38 — Five initial posts at launch. All of them repurposed from existing reel scripts — sarcopenia, the overload principle, Wolff's Law, gait speed as the sixth vital sign, and why muscle is the best investment you can make.
39 — Zach maintains the content with support from Claude for copy. The brother updates the technical structure as needed. No VA needed in v1.
40 — Yes a simple CMS is required. Zach needs to be able to update testimonials, science references, blog posts, and service details without touching code. Webflow or Framer with a CMS layer is the recommendation. If the brother prefers code-based then a headless CMS like Sanity or Contentful connected to a Next.js build.
41 — Logo exists as an SVG from our sessions. The Muscle PT wordmark in the brand system we built. Favicon should be derived from the same mark. Brand guidelines are the ones we have established — color, typography, spacing, and tone.
42 — Brand colors are locked. Primary is #0891B2 deep cyan blue. Near black #0D0D0D for dark sections. White #FFFFFF for light sections. Light tint #E0F6FB for subtle accents. No expansion beyond these in v1.
43 — The site should feel medical modern meets athletic performance. Think the visual precision of a clinical brand combined with the energy of a high performance coaching brand. Not cold. Not bro. Premium, purposeful, and human.
44 — Sites to reference for visual direction: Barbell Medicine for clinical credibility layout, Ben Bruno Online for coaching conversion simplicity, and any Framer or Webflow showcase site that uses dark hero sections with bright accent color and clean white content sections.
45 — Do not build anything that resembles a generic Squarespace fitness template, a supplement brand website, or a local gym website. Nothing that looks like it was made from a stock template without modification.
46 — Motion should be moderate. Subtle scroll animations on stat numbers and section reveals. No heavy parallax or excessive motion that slows mobile load time. Purposeful not decorative.
47 — Hero uses a short looping video of Zach training or in clinic on desktop if the asset exists. Falls back to a high quality photo if video is not available at launch. Photo preferred over no video.
48 — Mixed alternating sections. Dark hero. Light content sections. Dark pull quote or stat sections. Light services and process sections. Dark close and footer. This matches the carousel visual system we have already built.
49 — Multiple training and athletic photos already exist. Clinic photos and direct to camera photos should be shot before launch. These are essential assets for the site to feel credible and personal.
50 — At least four to six usable photos exist. Aim for twelve to fifteen total at launch — training, clinic, direct to camera, lifestyle. Commission a half day shoot before launch.
51 — Intro video does not exist yet. Film it before launch. 60 to 90 seconds. White coat. Direct to camera. Script is essentially the brand mission and personal story condensed. This is the highest priority content asset for the site.
52 — No testimonials exist publicly yet. Collect three to six written testimonials from current or past clients before launch. Name, age, and one specific outcome. No before and after photos required in v1.
53 — Affiliate partners at launch are HD Muscle, The Pride Foods, and Gym Pin. All three use discount code THEMUSCLEPT. Logos should be requested from each brand contact. Disclose the affiliate relationship in a small line under the section.
54 — Reel thumbnails can be manually curated and featured on the site. Pull four to six of the best performing reels and embed them manually in a featured content section. Do not use live Instagram feed API in v1 — too many dependency issues.
55 — Images should be optimized and hosted on the site itself. Video should be hosted on YouTube or Vimeo and embedded. No self-hosted video files on the server.
56 — Testimonials do not exist in written form yet. Collect them before launch via a simple Google Form sent to current and past clients.
57 — Get written consent from every client before publishing their name and any outcome information. Simple one sentence email confirmation is sufficient.
58 — All testimonials should speak to process and functional outcomes not medical claims. No language suggesting injury treatment or medical diagnosis. Stick to training results, consistency, strength gains, and pain reduction framed as fitness outcomes.
59 — Testimonials manually curated by Zach. Displayed as static cards on the site. Updated by Zach through the CMS every quarter or as new ones come in.
60 — Additional credentials to highlight: clinical rotations in Austin Texas, experience as an EMT in high pressure environments, competitive bodybuilding preparation, and the personal injury cascade that drives the clinical philosophy. These are on the About page and in the intro video.
61 — Zach curates the research references with support from Claude. Every PMID on the site is verified before publishing. The science section is a living document updated as new reels and content reference new studies.
62 — Yes the studies are already identified from our sessions. The six to eight most powerful PMIDs — Cruz-Jentoft 2019, Beckwée 2019, Schoenfeld 2017, Harman 2001, Liu and Latham 2009, Westcott 2012, Xie 2013 — anchor the science section at launch.
63 — Simple static science section in v1 with three to five bold stats and their citations. Expandable evidence library is a v2 feature once the blog is live.
64 — Yes outbound links to PubMed for every cited study. Opens in a new tab. Shows the research is real and accessible.
65 — Yes blog posts should be written around the same research that appears in reels. Each blog post targets a specific search term — sarcopenia treatment, progressive overload explained, gait speed and longevity — and cites the same PMIDs used in content. This is the SEO play.
66 — The site presents educational fitness content and online coaching. It does not provide physical therapy treatment, medical diagnosis, or clinical rehabilitation. This distinction must be explicit in the disclaimer and in service descriptions.
67 — Yes a formal medical disclaimer should appear on the footer of every page, on the services page, and on the application page. Something like — The content on this site is for educational and informational purposes only and does not constitute medical advice, diagnosis, or treatment. Always consult a qualified healthcare provider before beginning any exercise program.
68 — Yes Terms of Service, Privacy Policy, and a simple Cookie Policy at launch. These are non-negotiable if collecting any form data or using analytics.
69 — Yes the application form collects injury history and health-related goals. Keep this lightweight. General fitness goals and injury history framed as training context not medical history.
70 — Application collects general goals, training background, and injury history framed as — do you have any current injuries or pain that affect your training? This is fitness context not protected health information.
71 — HIPAA compliance is not required for this site in v1. Online coaching does not constitute a covered healthcare relationship. Once the clinic opens a separate HIPAA-compliant patient intake system is required for that workflow. Keep those entirely separate from the coaching site.
72 — Online coaching is not regulated by PT licensure. Educational content is not regulated. The coaching offer must be clearly positioned as personal training and coaching not physical therapy services. This is critical once Zach is a licensed PT — the site cannot solicit PT clients through the coaching site without proper clinical infrastructure.
73 — Yes explicit language distinguishing online coaching from physical therapy is required on the services page and in the FAQ. Something like — Online coaching is not physical therapy. If you are seeking physical therapy treatment for an injury or medical condition please consult a licensed provider in your area.
74 — WCAG 2.1 AA is the target for accessibility at launch. This is best practice and will be required if the clinic ever accepts Medicare or insurance patients down the line. Build it right from the start.
75 — Primary search terms to target: online fitness coach physical therapist, evidence based online coaching, muscle loss after 30, sarcopenia prevention, injury prevention training, online PT coach, physical therapist online coach, how to build muscle after 30, resistance training for longevity, San Diego physical therapist coach.
76 — Local SEO targets San Diego County for future clinic traffic. Create a Google Business Profile for The Muscle PT clinic before launch even if it is a coming soon listing. Target terms like San Diego physical therapist, cash pay PT San Diego, and San Diego strength and conditioning physical therapist.
77 — Yes separate landing pages for each major content pillar in v2. At launch scaffold the URLs so they are ready. Future pages include posture correction online, injury prevention coaching, muscle building after 40, sarcopenia treatment through exercise, and hypertrophy training with a PT.
78 — Current domain is zachermertcoaching.com. Transition to themusclept.com as the primary domain at launch. Set up a redirect from the old domain so no SEO value is lost. Register themuscleptclinic.com as a subdomain or separate domain for the future clinic.
79 — Preserve the Private Coaching page URL if it has any backlinks. Everything else can be rebuilt cleanly. Set up 301 redirects from all old URLs to the new equivalents.
80 — Yes redirect mapping should be part of the build specification. Map every current public URL to its new equivalent before launch.
81 — Yes implement schema markup for Person, Organization, LocalBusiness for the clinic coming soon, FAQ, and Article schema for blog posts. This improves search visibility significantly and costs nothing to implement.
82 — Instagram content manually curated in v1. Pull four to six reels and embed them manually as featured content. Revisit live Instagram API feed in v2 once the content library is larger.
83 — YouTube embedded manually in v1. Feature two to three videos on the resources page. Live YouTube feed integration in v2 once the channel has consistent output.
84 — Instagram is the primary traffic driver and should be prioritized on the site. YouTube is a secondary long form content home. Both link prominently from the site but Instagram gets more visual real estate in v1.
85 — Social proof metrics are a credibility signal not a primary KPI. The primary KPI is coaching applications. Social growth is tracked separately through Instagram analytics.
86 — Yes email capture tied to content. After any featured reel or blog post include an inline opt in — Want more evidence based training content? Get the free guide.
87 — Kit formerly ConvertKit handles email capture and automation. Already identified as the platform in our earlier sessions. Free up to 10,000 subscribers.
88 — The Minimum Effective Dose guide is not written yet. Outline and write it before launch. Five to seven pages. The minimum weekly resistance training dose to halt muscle loss based on the research. Cite Beckwée 2019 and Liu and Latham 2009. Deliver as a PDF via Kit automation.
89 — Delivered via automated email from Kit immediately after signup. Landing page thanks them and sets expectations. Email arrives within two minutes with the PDF download link.
90 — Yes a five email welcome sequence after guide download. Email one is the guide delivery. Email two is the sarcopenia education piece. Email three is the overload principle. Email four is a client story or testimonial. Email five is a soft invitation to apply for coaching.
91 — Yes coaching applicants should be tagged in Kit automatically via a Zapier integration between the application form and Kit. This allows segmentation between general subscribers and people who have already raised their hand for coaching.
92 — Kit serves as both the email platform and the lightweight CRM in v1. Superset handles the operational client management after acceptance. No separate CRM needed at this stage.
93 — Yes three segmented lists from day one. General subscribers who downloaded the guide. Coaching applicants who have submitted an application. Clinic waitlist subscribers interested in the future in person clinic.
94 — Affiliate section is a credibility signal not a revenue driver in v1. Clean logo placement, one line description, and discount code for each partner. Affiliate links open in a new tab. Legal disclosure required under the section.
95 — Yes a simple FTC disclosure is required on the affiliate section. Something like — The Muscle PT may receive compensation from the brands listed below. All recommendations are genuine and reflect products used personally.
96 — Track discount code usage through each brand's affiliate dashboard. HD Muscle, The Pride Foods, and Gym Pin all have affiliate tracking built into their programs. No custom tracking needed on the site side.
97 — Yes plan for additional affiliate partners beyond the current three. The affiliate section should use a CMS component so new partners can be added without touching code.
AFFILIATE PARTNERS AT LAUNCH
HD Muscle — code THEMUSCLEPT — supplements and performance nutrition
The Pride Foods — code THEMUSCLEPT — food and nutrition products
Gym Pin — code THEMUSCLEPT — gym accessories and equipment
98 — Final v1 page scope: Home, About, Services with subpages for Coaching and Consultations, The Science, Apply, Clinic Coming Soon, Contact, and a scaffolded Blog.
